Taking measured data from a community in Changchun as a case study, comparative analysis with the R-C model shows that the proposed method achieves lower MAPE (4.08% and 3.41% respectively) and ME (1.39min and 1.58min respectively) for evaluating the upward and downward regulation capabilities of electric heating, with higher accuracy, verifying the feasibility of the method.

title: Modular Multiplicative Divisor Labeling in Vertex Switched Jellyfish Graph
authors: K. Vijayalakshmi, M. Babu
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This paper investigates the Modular Multiplicative Divisor (MMD) labeling of jellyfish graphs under vertex switching when the number of tentacles is even. A vertex labeling function <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$f(v)$</tex> is defined to assign distinct positive integers to each vertex, ensuring that adjacent vertices satisfy the modular conditions required for MMD labeling. Correspondingly, an edge labeling function <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{f}^{*}(\boldsymbol{u v})$</tex> is derived from the vertex labels such that each edge label respects the MMD criteria. It is formally shown that the total sum of all edge labels is congruent to zero modulo the total number of vertices, confirming the consistency of the labeling scheme. The results rigorously establish that vertex-switched jellyfish graphs admit a valid MMD labeling, providing a mathematical framework for modular graph transformations.

title: Study on the Multi Energy Integration Development in Offshore Energy Islands
authors: Jinmao Chen, Xudong Wang, Wanli Xu, Jing Zhang, Xing Su, Long Huang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Offshore energy islands are innovative carriers for the in-depth integration of marine space and clean energy. They integrate the production of offshore wind power, offshore photovoltaics (PV), and wave energy, while coupling seawater desalination, green hydrogen production and storage, and electrochemical energy storage and conversion. Relying on offshore step-up substations, submarine cables, and hydrogen transmission pipelines to build a transportation network, they form a clustered commercial model centered on power supply, hydrogen supply, and fishery. As a key fulcrum for energy transition and the achievement of the "dual carbon" (carbon peaking and carbon neutrality) goals, this paper systematically sorts out the energy production system, conversion technology, transportation channels, and benefit model of offshore energy islands. It analyzes the current progress in technological breakthroughs and engineering practices, points out challenges such as adaptability to extreme climates, high initial investment costs, and ecological impact assessment, and looks forward to directions including floating platform technology and nuclear-renewable hybrid systems. This study provides theoretical references and practical insights for the large-scale development and commercial application of offshore energy islands.

title: Multi-objective Double-layer Optimization Scheduling Method for Multi-energy Virtual Power Plants
authors: Hongyang Jin, Haichao Yan, Xiangluan Dong, Wei Wang, Dong Zhang, Yuzhou Duan
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the development of energy transition and distributed energy systems, multi-energy virtual power plants (VPPs) as a new form of grid dispatch and resource integration are gradually attracting attention from both academia and industry. This paper, starting from the energy autonomy mechanism, explores the energy balance, coordinated dispatch, and environmental-economic dispatch strategies within multi-energy systems. Firstly, it analyzes the basic composition and operation mechanism of multi-energy virtual power plants. Secondly, based on the establishment of an energy autonomy mathematical model, it introduces environmental factors and economic costs to form an environmental-economic dispatch model. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through numerical simulation experiments. The results show that the method not only improves the energy utilization efficiency within the virtual power plant but also minimizes the dispatch cost while meeting environmental protection requirements.

title: Bounding Uncertainty Risk in Data Center Energy Management using an Improved Information Gap Decision Theory
authors: Siran Lu, Qing Li, Yingzhao Tang, Junbing Wen, Xiaoqiang Guo, Shixing Ding
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the deep integration of data centers as core computational infrastructure and integrated energy systems, the Data Center Integrated Energy System (DC-IES) has become a key direction for achieving efficient energy management, but its scheduling operations face significant challenges due to multiple uncertainties. To address the multiple uncertainties posed by IT loads, multi-energy loads, and renewable energy output in Data Center Integrated Energy Systems (DC-IES), this paper proposes an uncertainty risk bounding method for data center energy management based on the improved Information Gap Decision Theory (IGDT). By constructing robust and opportunity-based DC-IES optimization models, the optimal scheduling strategy is solved directly under different levels of uncertainty, thereby generating a risk interval of cost fluctuations. Simulation results indicate that this method can clearly reveal the cost-uncertainty trade-off relationship, providing decision-makers with a quantitative basis for risk.

title: Research on Motor Overheating Protection Method Based on Thermal Model Parameter Optimization
authors: Xiping Pei, Wei Li, Changsheng Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: In industrial applications, asynchronous motors not only need to have good mechanical properties, but their thermal overload protection is also critical. However, motor manufacturers usually only provide basic operating parameters and lack key thermal characteristics data, which makes temperature rise prediction difficult. For this reason, this paper proposes a stator temperature prediction method for motors based on temperature rise curves. The method constructs a thermal model based on the heat balance differential equation, and implements numerical computation through a MATLAB solver. Under the condition of constant ambient temperature, the method can be adapted to different loads and cyclic changes in working conditions, and realize the accurate prediction of temperature rise. Finally, the TEFC induction motor model is built in ANSYS for simulation verification, and the results show that the predicted values of the proposed model are highly consistent with the simulation results, which verifies the feasibility of the method. The research results can provide theoretical support for the design of motor thermal protection and protector.

title: Architectural Enhancements to YOLOv12 for Improving Detection of Thoracic Pathologies in Chest X-rays
authors: Ruxandra Andro, Cristian-Cosmin Vancea
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: In recent years, artificial intelligence has played a pivotal role in advancing medical imaging, particularly in the early detection of thoracic diseases through chest X-rays. Latest efforts have been successful in proposing deep learning solutions for accurate classification of different pathologies. The objectives have been refocused towards improving the detection of different signs of disease. We propose an original deep learning model for affected area detection. The model is based on the YOLOv12 object detection architecture, which was enhanced specifically for medical use cases. The improvements include attention mechanisms, deformable convolutions, and feature aggregation modules such as FPN and PANet. We created a custom dataset, mainly a combination of two public chest X-ray datasets, which were augmented to train our model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a mAP@0.5 of 0.374 and a Recall of 0.519, outperforming existing models, including baseline YOLOv12, YOLOv11, YOLOv8, and Faster R-CNN on these key metrics. Despite the architectural enhancements, inference time remained competitive at 6.3 ms per image. The model shows high reliability in identifying four key thoracic pathologies: cardiomegaly, aortic enlargement, pulmonary nodules/masses, and pleural effusion. These findings suggest that optimized YOLOv12-based models can serve as effective diagnostic support tools in clinical and remote healthcare settings.

title: Multi-Frequency Impedance Modeling and Stability Analysis of LCL-Type Grid-Connected Inverter Clusters Under Weak Grid Conditions
authors: Jiaxin Zhang, Zijun Liu, Wenhua Luo, Xiqiang Chang, Zhiyong Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Under weak grid conditions, the interaction between LCL-type grid-connected inverters and grid impedance easily induces high-frequency resonance, which compromises system stability. Traditional modeling methods often approximate the sampling process as a proportional link 1/T. This simplification introduces distortion near the boundaries of the primary frequency band above the Nyquist frequency, leading to the erroneous prediction of non-existent unstable regions in stability analysis. To solve this problem, this paper proposes a multi-frequency impedance modeling method that considers sampling aliasing effects, starting from the perspective of modeling and analyzing the input impedance of grid-connected inverters. The specific contributions of this method are twofold: first, it accurately characterizes the high-frequency characteristics of the system; second, it effectively eliminates the false unstable regions predicted by traditional models and enhances the stability of inverter clusters in weak grids. Finally, experimental results validate that the proposed method can avoid stability misjudgment caused by traditional modeling distortions, providing a reliable basis for the stability analysis of LCL-type grid-connected inverter clusters under weak grid conditions.

title: Machine Learning Based Soil Nutrient Analysis in Rural Bengaluru: A Naive Bayes Approach
authors: Shobha N, Jagadishwari V, Chandrakala B M, Suma V, Harsha. S
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Soil nutrients are an important part of healthy plant growth. Macronutrients (nitrogen, potassium and phosphorus) and micronutrients (iron, zinc, copper and magnesium) are necessary for plants and these are managed and substituted as fertilizer. Soil nutrient analysis is necessary to find nutrient availability, to identify the amount of acidity, alkalinity and salinity in the soil and to recommend on the quantity of manure and fertilizers according to the soil analysis value and according to the crop. The present study aimed to find the soil fertility of rural villages of Bengaluru region. Naive Bayes classification algorithm is applied on soil nutrient data to find soil fertility.

title: GPS-Enabled Attendance Tracking System: A Location-Based Approach for Enhanced Accuracy and Security
authors: Ravikumar, Sanjay S R, Viren J, Surendar Kumar H P, Yashas S, Keerthishree B T
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Traditional attendance management systems in educational institutions face significant challenges including inefficiency, susceptibility to proxy attendance, and manual errors that compromise data reliability. This paper presents a comprehensive GPS-enabled attendance tracking system that combines advanced geofencing algorithms, Kalman filtering, and location-based authentication to automate and secure attendance management. The proposed system validates student presence within predefined classroom boundaries using GPS technology with optimized filtering parameters (process noise <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$Q=0.1 I$</tex>, measurement noise <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R=10 I$</tex>, sampling interval <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta t=1 \mathrm{s}$</tex>), reducing GPS error from 3-5 m to 1-2 m. Experimental validation conducted over six months with 500 students demonstrates 95.2 % geofence detection accuracy, 94.5 % location-based authentication accuracy, and 92.1 % effectiveness in preventing proxy attendance. The system reduces administrative workload by 80 % while maintaining minimal battery consumption of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{7} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> daily. Additionally, comprehensive privacy and security measures ensure compliance with data protection standards.

title: A Coordinated Control Method for Renewable Energy Transmission Section Overload with Multiple Adjustable Resources
authors: Hao Huang, Xiangkuan Wan, Yuanhang Mu, Jiayang Wang, Siyu Lu, Ke Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: As the proportion of renewable energy connected to the grid continues to increase, the grid structure becomes increasingly complex, placing higher demands on power system dispatch and safety control. Meanwhile, the geographical imbalance between power supply and load has made the issue of transmission section capacity overload a key concern for system safety operation and dispatch. In response, this paper proposes a multi-timescale optimization model based on section power prediction, which comprehensively considers the coordinated operation of multiple adjustable resources at different speeds. Taking a wind-fired multi-resource system equipped with energy storage devices as an example, based on the principle of prioritizing renewable energy consumption, this paper establishes a proactive-real-time coordinated control strategy for wind-fired multi-resource systems incorporating energy storage devices. An optimization model is developed to achieve optimal regulation costs during the proactive scheduling phase and minimal adjustment time during the real-time adjustment phase, thereby ensuring power system and transmission section safety.

title: Distributed Secondary Control for Voltage Regulation in Microgrids Using an Event-Triggered Communication Mechanism
authors: B. Chiranjeev Rao, Shimpy Ralhan, Mahesh Singh, Vishnu Kumar Sahu
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: The emerging adoption of distributed generation in DC microgrids requires more complex secondary control schemes that can guarantee voltage regulation, power sharing, and less communication overheads. This paper introduces a dynamically controlled secondary control architecture of the coordinated voltage regulation of a number of distributed generators (DGs) under different conditions of power demand. In contrast to traditional time-based strategies, the given approach will only update the control actions if the predetermined triggering conditions have been broken, which will considerably decrease the amount of unneeded communication and control updates. The performance of the scheme is measured in two working conditions, which are constant power demand and load step changes. The outcome of the simulation shows that there is smooth convergence in consensus, stable voltage condition and sound transient operation in all DG units. Further, the initiation of activity is limited to short bursts, which proves the effectiveness of event-driven mechanism in reducing communication load but maintaining the stability and flexibility of the system. These findings confirm the suggested plan to be a credible and scalable solution of intelligent microgrid management of energy.

title: Harmonizing Electrical Injection and Optical Waveguide Through Solution-Processed Hole Injection/Transport Layers in OLED
authors: Ting Xu, Kele Zhao, Lei Jin, Lingqiang Meng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Energy, Materials, and Photonics (EMP)
abstract: Employing solution-processed holes injection/ transport layers (HITLs) in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) provides a pathway for harmonizing electrical injection and optical waveguiding. This work explores the regulation of the composition of solution-processed HITLs to facilitates charge injection into the emissive layer while enhancing light outcoupling of OLEDs. Employing solution-processed HITLs in OLEDs reduced the turn-on voltage from 3.8 to 3.2 V, indicating improved hole injection. Especially, at a high brightness of 5000 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{c d} / \mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>, solution-processed HITLs in OLEDs demonstrates an 8.81 % enhancement in power efficiency, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$21 ~\text{lm} / \mathrm{W}$</tex> compared to vacuum-deposited HITLs in OLEDs. By modifying the composition of solution-based PEDOT:PSS/TAPC as HITL, optical simulation guided the optimization of HITL composition and thickness for ideal optical waveguiding to achieve boost light outcoupling in OLEDs. This study explores new strategies via optical simulation for high-performance and cost-effective versatile solution-processed HITLs for OLED lighting and displays.

title: Lessons Learned from Setting Up Kubernetes for Operations on Temporal Graph Neural Networks
authors: Fotis Sofoulis, Georgios Drakopoulos, Leonidas Theodorakopoulos, Spyros Sioutas
publish: 2025 10th South-East Europe Design Automation, Computer Engineering, Computer Networks and Social Media Conference (SEEDA-CECNSM)
abstract: Infrastructure is one of the mainstays of contemporary deep learning as it determines the size of the data which can be stored as well as the efficiency of the operations performed on them. This is especially true in graph-based methods where the conventional table-oriented setup does not apply, as most graph operations translate to a considerable number of join operations resulting in considerable complexity. Graph databases and graph processing tools deployed over distributed infrastructure is one efficient way to address this issue. This was the case with a Neo4j instance deployed over Apache Spark for storing a temporal graph neural network (TGNN) and performing community structure discovery. The motivation behind the proposed infrastructure, which is described in detail, is to rely exclusively on standard and readily available hardware and networking equipment. Furthermore, implications and experience gained while setting it up are explained. The latter includes lessons about storage allocation, networking, and package dependencies.

title: FollowFusion: Online MVS for Terrain Adaptive Viewpoint Generation
authors: Zhenyu Xia, Lin Chen, Pengcheng Han, Shuhui Bu
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: In Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-based aerial surveying for geographic information acquisition, meticulously designed flight trajectories and images captured can generate precise 3D models, thereby simplifying the reconstruction process for large-scale scenes. Existing research primarily relies on an explore-then-exploit strategy combined with offline reconstruction for 3D scene modeling. However, the need for a second flight and extended offline processing makes this approach inefficient. To address this, we propose a real-time scene reconstruction method based on view path planning. Our method incrementally reconstructs the target scene online by analyzing current terrain data. It dynamically adjusts the Above Ground Level (AGL) to maintain a consistent Ground Sample Distance (GSD) while maximizing Multi-View Stereo (MVS) performance to generate optimal viewpoints. The scene model is generated in real-time based on the captured image data. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm can achieve high-quality 3D reconstruction in a single flight without any prior information.

title: Enhanced Adaptive Virtual Impedance Strategy for Fault Current Limiting in Grid-Forming Converters
authors: Fengshun Jiao, Hao Zhang, Weijie Gao, Hezhi Cui, Yanyu Liang, Haoyu Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Grid-forming (GFM) converters exhibit voltage-source characteristics. During grid faults, the converter may be forced to deliver excessive fault current, which can exceed the safe operating area of the semiconductor devices. The adaptive virtual impedance control strategy is a method to address the overcurrent issue of grid-forming converters under grid faults. This strategy is capable of dynamically adjusting the virtual impedance value in accordance with the magnitude of the fault current, so as to limit the fault current. Nevertheless, this approach fails to limit the fault current beneath the preset current threshold, causing inconvenience for the design of controller parameters and practical application. To enhance the current-limiting effect, this paper proposes an improved adaptive virtual impedance control strategy by introducing an integrator to eliminate the steady-state error of the fault current. In order to analyze the stability of the proposed control strategy, this paper establishes a dq-axis impedance model, and the impedance stability criterion and the generalized Nyquist criterion are used to analyze the influence of control parameters on the stability of grid-forming converter. The effectiveness of the proposed improved adaptive virtual impedance strategy on fault current suppression and the correctness of the stability analysis theory were verified through Matlab/Simulink simulation and OPAL-RT semi-physical simulation platform.

title: Pre-Disaster Inspection Method for Distribution Networks in Typhoon Scenarios
authors: Zejin Li, Yuxiong Huang, Gengfeng Li, Huili Tian, Qi Liu, Shunqi Zeng, Chen Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The inspection of distribution networks in typhoon scenarios faces multiple challenges, including the uncertainty of disaster situations and the coupling of time and space. This paper constructs a pre-disaster inspection allocation model based on reinforcement learning algorithms. Additionally, state decomposition and action masking techniques are designed to optimize the performance of the reinforcement learning algorithm. A testing environment for the inspection and recovery of distribution networks is established, and simulations are conducted under various typhoon intensities and fault scenarios. Comparing the results with traditional reinforcement learning algorithms, the proposed model demonstrates superior overall performance in power restoration, providing valuable guidance for dynamic pre-disaster inspections.

title: Optimization Strategy for Power System Restoration Considering Social Function and Starting Characteristics of Load
authors: Yinbo Wu, Zhenlan Dou, Jian Sun, Ying Zhang, Chunyan Zhang, Jiaying Chen, Kang Sun, Lili Hao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper proposes a power system restoration method and system that takes social functions and load starting characteristics into account. Firstly, based on the absolute power outage duration, the method calculates the power supply restoration importance parameters for raw material preservation, production operation, and load starting characteristics. Secondly, it introduces social function correction coefficients such as the production function of equipment groups, the probability of electrical energy substitution, and the multi-energy coordinated restoration process. Furthermore, it dynamically corrects the power supply restoration importance of users and nodes, and generates a restoration strategy based on this. Through case verification, the proposed method can effectively improve the net benefit of the restoration process, with the benefit increasing by approximately 5.51% compared with the traditional fixed importance method, which demonstrates its advantages in refined energy management and optimal utilization of resources.

title: Restricted Threshold Based Compressive Sensing Using Group Sparse Representation Rt-Gsr
authors: Tejaswini Chetty, Anshuj Jain, Paresh Rawat, Laxmi Singh, Sanjeev Gupta
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: The Group based Compressive Sensing (CS) methods such as Grupp-sparse representation (GSR) have been widely used for storing the image data. However, conventional sparse representation models assume independence among transform coefficients and fail to capture the inherent correlations in natural images. Consequently, these methods often yield over-smoothed reconstructions, texture loss, and degraded performance under noise or model mismatch. To overcome these limitations, in this paper a restricted-Threshold based GSR (RT-GSR) based CS recovery framework is proposed, that optimally selects the restricted iteration counts for standard GSR method to avoided over smothering of reconstructed image. Unlike traditional methods RT-GSR imposes joint sparsity on groups of similar patches, effectively modeling both spatial and structural dependencies. Proposed RT-GSR framework incorporates an iteration restriction mechanism based on an early PSNR stabilization threshold, enabling the algorithm to automatically terminate once the reconstruction quality becomes stable. it is concluded that as the sub rate increases the restriction count also decreases and PSNR increases by using RT-GSR.

title: Multimodal Transformer-Based Lung Disease Detection and Preventive Health Advisor Using Chest X-Rays and Patient Metadata
authors: Thummalapalli Eswar Sai Venkata Prathap, Bhumika Karsh, Ram Kumar Karsh, Madhumita Banerjee
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Accurate and early detection of lung diseases remains a critical challenge in clinical radiology, particularly in resource-constrained settings where expert interpretation may be limited. This work proposes an advanced AI-based decision support system that combines high-resolution chest X-ray imaging with structured patient metadata to improve diagnostic accuracy across multiple pulmonary conditions. Our model architecture integrates a pre-trained EfficientNet backbone for rich image feature extraction with a custom Vision Transformer (ViT) that captures complex spatial dependencies among image patches. Simultaneously, tabular clinical features—including age, sex, smoking status, BMI, blood pressure, heart rate, and body temperature—are processed through dense neural layers to provide additional patient context. The system employs extensive data augmentation and per-image standardization to enhance generalization, while mixed-precision GPU training improves computational efficiency. The resulting multimodal model demonstrates improved classification performance compared to image-only baselines, effectively identifying high-risk conditions such as pneumonia, cardiomegaly, and emphysema with clinically relevant confidence thresholds. Additionally, we present a user-friendly graphical interface designed to assist healthcare providers by delivering interpretable disease probability scores, personalized risk summaries, and practical medical recommendations. This study highlights the potential of multimodal deep learning systems to augment radiological workflows, supporting telemedicine, triage, and automated screening in underserved healthcare environments.

title: Study on User Behavior Interaction Model under Coupling of Electric and Carbon Market
authors: Zeqi Ge, Jieping Han, Wanyu Zhao, Fangfei Lv, Yuhan Liu, Xi Tang, Wendong Shao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: In response to the "dual carbon" goal, this article constructs a user behavior interaction model under the coupling of electricity and carbon markets, addressing the limitations of existing research on user side homogenization and elastic exogeneity. The model incorporates the state constraints of distributed photovoltaics and energy storage, changes in equipment energy efficiency, and the third type of uncontrollable load into the framework, forming a triple trade-off of "energy consumption comfort emissions"; And set up policy scenarios such as time of use carbon pricing, quota tightening, and differentiated subsidies. Based on typical daily simulations, it is shown that user side resources significantly improve load controllability, peak shaving and valley filling, and improve power supply reliability; Tightening quotas and differentiated subsidies can achieve synergy between user cost reduction and emission reduction without significantly reducing comfort; The bidirectional feedback mechanism avoids exogenous assumptions and more accurately characterizes the impact of market signals on user decisions, providing a quantitative basis for demand side management and electricity carbon co pricing. The comprehensive impact of three types of policies on user welfare, emission reduction, and reliability was compared, indicating that targeted subsidies are superior to unified subsidies.

title: A Comparative Study of Robust Strategies for Single-Area Power Systems
authors: S J Lolu, M A Bazaz
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Load Frequency Control (LFC) is critical for ensuring stable operation of power systems, especially under diverse operating conditions. Within the analyzed single-area power network, the area control error (ACE) has been effectively managed despite uncertainties in system dynamics and the presence of external disturbances. The most recent study on disturbance rejection mechanisms has revealed that Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC) is an effective controller for controlling uncertainty in systems, whether the system is linear or nonlinear. The importance of the ADRC controller resides in its model-free nature, that requires little understanding of the system model. The extended state observer (ESO) facilitates continuous estimation and compensation for disturbances, whether originating within the system or from external environments. However, certain process disturbances, such as time-delay, makes ADRC controller design more difficult. A solution to this problem is to use a Disturbance Observer (DO) which improves system performance. This study presents a comparative analysis of six control strategies: General Active Disturbance Rejection Control (ADRC) Controller with neglected delay, Delayed Input ADRC Controller, Smith Predictor ADRC Controller (ADRC-SP), Disturbance Observer (DO), PID (Proportional plus Integral plus Derivative) Controller, and PI (Proportional plus Integral) Controller, for their effectiveness in LFC of a single-area power system. Controllers are evaluated based on key performance metrics such as settling time, rise time, overshoot, and Integral of Absolute Error (IAE) under three scenarios: step load change, variable wind speed penetration, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> constant wind speed penetration. The results highlight the superior performance of the Disturbance Observer in minimizing settling time, overshoot, and Integral Absolute Error (IAE), making it the most robust and efficient controller among the evaluated strategies.

title: TinyMl-Based Embedded Analytics Model Compression and Hardware Co-Design
authors: Pavithra S, Narthika S, Praveen M
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: TinyML represents the paradigm shift of running machine learning algorithms directly on powerconstrained microcontrollers and small edge devices. These technologies are not only capable of running these computational operations in on premises hardware, but they can also reduce dependence on a continuous cloud infrastructure, resolves time to respond, and time to travel cut down, and produce higher privacy of sensitive data. This integration becomes especially beneficial in the monitoring of industrial machinery, structural health monitoring, biomedical signal analysis, and environmental monitoring, where continuous and real-time processing in remote or bandwidth constrained environment is essential. Its underlying architecture utilizes sophisticated methods for quantization, pruning, and neural network architecture searching to minimize complex models while maintaining little loss in predictive performance At the same time, co-design methodologies enable hardware efficiencies that support intelligent functionality at milliwatt consumption profiles. The algorithm-level optimizations and architecture-level adaptations together provide for local sensor level decisionmaking which removes the need for a lot of backhauling and makes the overall system more robust. But TinyML is not simply about miniaturisation of existing technology, it is a clear shift to the provision of ubiquitous sensing intelligence integrated into devices and supported by low-power processing. This development opens new pathways for scalable deployments in smart agriculture, wearable diagnostics, asset monitoring and sustainable infrastructure. Against a backdrop of increasing global research effort, TinyML is being positioned as a cornerstone of democratizing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> in the emerging field of ultralow-power embedded intelligence that are accessible, reliable, and environmentally sustainable, at scale.

title: Near-Field Channel Modeling and Beamforming in 6G Wireless Networks
authors: T. Haritha, Swathi Dasi, N. Bhovaneswari, P. Srinivasa Reddy, L. Tirumala Sai, Shaik Sultan Basha
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This work examines the problem of estimating channels for near-field communication in the upcoming 6G wireless technology, which will utilize frequencies in the terahertz and millimeter wave ranges. By using uniform linear arrays (ULAs) and near-field beamforming, a new, effective way is presented to model and estimate channel characteristics. The suggested method uses SVD to accurately describe the channel. To make the most of the way near-field waves move through space, distance-dependent spherical wavefront modeling is also used Additionally, the degrees of freedom (DoF) are examined using both empirical estimates and analytical approximations to demonstrate the transition from near field to far field. The approach demonstrates effectiveness through simulation results, exhibiting improved estimation accuracy, resilience, and computational efficiency. This work gives a practical and scalable approach to 6G communication systems with high frequencies and overcomes the challenge of near-field propagation.

title: Remaining Useful Life Prediction of Lithium-Ion Battery Across Temperature Based on Dual-Branch Deep Learning
authors: Chen Li, Kaile Zhou, Zhifeng Guo
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Early prediction of the remaining useful life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries remains a significant challenge, especially under unknown temperature conditions. This study proposes a novel predictive framework that addresses this issue by incorporating two distinct branches. On one hand, the model utilizes the historical data of the target battery to predict its early RUL; on the other hand, it computes the RUL by matching the target battery with reference batteries through similarity metrics, and then calculating the difference. Ultimately, the predictions from both branches are fused through a weighted aggregation to estimate the RUL of the target battery under a specific temperature. In this framework, the selection of reference batteries is based on multi-dimensional similarity calculations and temperature-aware virtual sample generation techniques, which enhance the accuracy of reference battery matching. Experimental results show that the final root mean square error (RMSE) is 0.65, and comparative experiments validate the effectiveness of the reference battery selection method. This approach plays a crucial role in improving prediction accuracy, especially in cross-temperature scenarios. In conclusion, this study provides valuable insights and practical guidance for early RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries in cross-temperature environments.

title: Research On Time Identification Methods for Stray Currents in HVDC Transmission Projects Based on Generative Adversarial Networks
authors: Yiwen Li, Xin Mei, Ling Ruan, Wanting Deng, Yibo Cui, Wei Rao, Shanshan Ke, Zeyang Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Earth stray currents originating from HVDC transmission projects, urban rail transit, and geomagnetic storms demonstrate divergent characteristics and typological variations, compounded by the sporadic nature of monopolar operations in HVDC systems. To address the critical challenge of sample scarcity, this study develops a GAN-based temporal identification framework for HVDC stray currents. The methodology encompasses: 1) Generative adversarial modeling for synthetic data augmentation, 2) Combined application of first-order difference and CUSUM algorithms for step change detection and directional analysis. This integrated approach establishes a robust data foundation for stray current analysis and mitigation strategies.

title: A Sensitivity-Aware and PSO-Driven Differential Privacy Method with Customized Budgets for Structured Data Perturbation_supp1-3673144.pdf
authors: Seong Oun Hwang
publish: N/A
abstract: Differential privacy (DP) is the leading standard for privacy protection, providing rigorous privacy guarantees for various data. However, its conventional approach of treating all records uniformly regarding privacy risk and using a non-adaptive privacy budget (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula>) often compromises data utility in subsequent analyses. This uniform treatment and fixed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula> can introduce significant perturbations, making the secondary use of shared data challenging. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel record-sensitivity-aware and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)-driven customised <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula>-DP method for data perturbation. Our approach significantly enhances data utility without compromising privacy in data sharing by introducing three key optimisations to the traditional DP framework: First, we partition records into three sensitivity classes (high, medium, and low) based on the privacy risk. Second, we adopt a PSO mechanism to determine the optimal <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula> for each partition, perturbing data with a variable <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula> that considers sensitivity, rather than using a single, fixed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula> for the entire dataset. Finally, noise is injected by grouping attributes horizontally, rather than adding noise to each attribute independently, to prevent the generation of inconsistent values in the perturbed data. Detailed experiments on real benchmark and synthetic datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method in terms of utility and privacy across seven evaluation metrics, compared to the latest state-of-the-art <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula>-DP methods.

authors: Aiqi Jiang, Arkaitz Zubiaga

title: Modified Logarithm Averaging Method for Clumping Index Calculation Using Tracing Radiation and Architecture of Canopy (TRAC) Measurements
authors: Zhiguo Liang, Xiguang Yang, Ying Yu, Rongshan Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The clumping index (CI) is a key parameter for modeling radiative transfer in plant canopies, but its retrieval leads to significantly reduced accuracy in highly heterogeneous environments. To improve the accuracy of CI quantification and to assess the performance of various CI algorithms under ecologically heterogeneous conditions, five conventional methods based on the Tracing Radiation and Architecture of Canopies (TRAC) instrument, namely the logarithm methods (LX), the gap size distribution methods (CC and P), the combination of gap size distribution and logarithm methods (CLX), and the modified gap size distribution algorithm (CMN), along with a novel LX+ method developed from the LX approach in this study, are evaluated in three 100 m × 100 m natural secondary forests stands (hardwood, coniferous, and mixed broadleaf coniferous) located at Maoer Mountain Forest Farm in Heilongjiang Province, China. The key findings include: (a) The LX+ method demonstrates enhanced sensitivity and accuracy in highly heterogeneous stands. (b) The P and LX methods show no significant variation trends with increasing view zenith angle. In contrast, the CC, CLX, and LX+ methods exhibit a pattern in which the CI values first increase rapidly and then stabilize as the zenith angle increases. In particular, the LX+ method yields the highest precision for CI estimates among all methods. (c) The LX+ method achieves optimal accuracy with varying segment lengths (Coefficient of variation=11.5%) and has strong explanatory ability for heterogeneous canopy structures with different segment lengths. The results demonstrate that the LX+ method significantly enhances characterization of multiscale canopy heterogeneity CI while maintaining the highest accuracy across all experimental scenarios.

title: Self-Oscillating Joule Thief Converters for MEMS Electromagnetic Energy Harvesting: Analytical Modeling and Experimental Validation
authors: Shengkai Su, Snorre Aunet
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: MEMS electromagnetic energy harvesters face a critical cold-start bottleneck: ultralow voltage combined with high source impedance. Existing startup converters optimize for either low voltage or high impedance, rarely both. This article addresses this challenge by adapting the self-oscillating joule thief topology through closed-form analytical modeling. Unlike prior semianalytical approaches requiring iterative simulation, our framework enables simulation-free design exploration of bipolar junction transistor (BJT)- and N-type metal-oxide-semiconductor (NMOS)-based implementations. For both implementations, the analytical model predicts output voltage within 10% of experimental results over the measured voltage, load, and source-resistance sweeps. The NMOS version demonstrates a 50 mV start-up from a 300 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\Omega}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> source impedance. Experimental validation with a 300 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\Omega}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> electromagnetic energy harvester shows a tradeoff in practice: zero-threshold NMOS starts at 0.35 g rms acceleration but provides 43% boost; low-threshold NMOS starts at 0.5 g but achieves 93% boost—both outperforming TI BQ25570’s 0.7 g startup requirement. By positioning the joule thief as a specialized startup circuit complementing high-efficiency main converters, this framework addresses a key barrier to self-powered MEMS deployment and high-impedance source energy harvesting.

title: Formation and Elimination of Leakage in the Fabrication of Bi-2223/Ag Tape
authors: X B Ma, S N Zhang, L H Jin, X Y Xu, X Q Liu, B T Shao, J F Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: 37-filamentary Bi2Sr2Ca2Cu3O10+δ (Bi-2223) superconducting tapes were prepared by the powder-in-tube (PIT) technique. Leakage occurring during high-temperature processing is a major issue that degrades both the engineering critical current density (Jₑ) and mechanical properties of Bi-2223 conductors. In this work, the critical silver sheath thickness required to eliminate leakage was systematically investigated by correlating leakage behavior with local sheath thickness in different tapes. The results demonstrate that the leakage can be effectively eliminated when the silver sheath thickness exceeds 38 μm, which is identified as the critical threshold. Furthermore, increasing the wall thickness of the outer Ag alloy tube is shown to be an efficient approach for producing optimized Bi-2223 superconducting tapes. When the wall thickness of the outer Ag-alloy tube is increased from 0.6 mm to 0.8 mm or greater, the silver sheath thickness in the tapes exceeds 42 μm, which was above the critical value required for leakage suppression. These findings provide a practical guideline for reducing leakage in Bi-2223 tapes and offered a viable strategy applicable to other multifilamentary superconducting tapes and wires.

title: AerialExtreMatch: A Benchmark for Extreme-View Image Matching and Localization
authors: Rouwan Wu, Zhe Huang, Xingyi He, Yan Liu, Shen Yan, Sida Peng, Maojun Zhang, Xiaowei Zhou
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Image matching serves as a core component for UAV localization guided by satellite imagery. However, this task remains highly challenging due to the extreme viewpoint discrepancies between low-altitude UAV images and nadir-view satellite maps. Existing datasets primarily focus on ground-level or high-altitude UAV imagery, lacking sufficient coverage of the geometric variations typical of real aerial scenarios. To address these limitations, we introduce AerialExtreMatch, a large-scale, high-fidelity dataset tailored for extreme-view image matching and UAV localization. It consists of approximately 1.5 million synthetic image pairs rendered from high-quality 3D scenes, simulating diverse UAV and satellite viewpoints to enable robust training of image matching models. To support fine-grained evaluation, we construct a hierarchical benchmark with 32 difficulty levels. These are defined using three geometric criteria: overlap ratio, scale variation, and pitch difference. In addition, we collect a real-world UAV localization dataset with geo-aligned reference maps of varying visual quality. Extensive experiments involving 16 representative detector-based and detector-free methods demonstrate that models trained on AerialExtreMatch achieve substantial performance gains in both image matching and real-world localization under extreme-view conditions. The dataset and code are available on https://github.com/Xecades/AerialExtreMatch https://github.com/Xecades/AerialExtreMatch.

title: Multi-Reference Frames Based Occupancy Information Difference and Correlation Entropy Coding for MPEG G-PCC
authors: Chuang Ma, Qi Zhang, Ge Li, Hongyu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Efficient compression of massive 3D point clouds remains challenging under limited storage and bandwidth. The Moving Picture Experts Group (MPEG) has developed the geometry-based point cloud compression (G-PCC) standard and is currently at the FDIS stage, referred to as Enhanced G-PCC. This paper proposes a novel inter prediction attribute coding based on the Region Adaptive Hierarchical Transform (RAHT) and entropy coding framework to enhance the coding efficiency. 1) Multi-Reference Frames: A low-delay prediction architecture is introduced to improve temporal correlation utilization, which has not yet been explored in G-PCC inter RAHT attribute compression, with a lightweight motion-based switch to disable multi-reference prediction for large motion. 2) Occupancy Information Difference: The inter eligibility based on occupancy information difference scheme is employed to select valid inter reference blocks, while hybrid prediction scheme based on occupancy information difference is further introduced to enhance prediction accuracy. 3) Correlation Entropy Coding: A new entropy coder is developed to exploit the intrinsic correlation among color components. By jointly leveraging these techniques, the proposed method efficiently exploits spatial and temporal regularities of dynamic point clouds, achieving significant performance gains. Experimental results show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art Enhanced G-PCC reference software (TMC13-v31), with average coding gains of 3.17% for reflectance on the Cat3 dataset and 2.81%, 16.92%, and 12.21% for the Luma, Cb, and Cr components on the Cat2 dataset under MPEG common test conditions. The method, Occupancy Information Difference, has already been adopted into the MPEG Enhanced G-PCC standard.

title: Doppler Effect Mitigation for High-Speed Train Localization with UWB/IMU Fusing
authors: Wan-Ning He, Xin-Lin Huang, Zhongwei Xu, Cheng Li, Shui Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Accurate location information is an essential prerequisite for location-based service and operation safety in railways. Ultra wide band (UWB) fused with inertial measurement unit (IMU) has been thought as a promising localization technique for trains. However, due to trains’ high speed, ranging results from UWBs are seriously influenced by Doppler effect. Most existing work focused on Doppler effect mitigation in single round of communication, but ignored its negative impact on two-way time of flight (ToF) and fusion performance. In this paper, we propose an adaptive fusion model with ranging rectifications for high-speed trains. Firstly, two-way ToF ranging errors caused by Doppler effect are studied. Secondly, Doppler effect is mitigated effectively, with prior information of trains’ states in time series. Finally, an adaptive UWB/IMU fusion scheme is proposed, where residual errors of UWB observations after Doppler effect mitigation are modeled by a closed-form expression. Simulation results show that, regarding single time-slot localization, the proposed ranging rectification algorithm outperforms four typical algorithms, with gains of 78.42%, 60.22%, 58.09%, and 28.38%, in terms of RMSE. Regarding continuous localization with temporal correlation, the proposed adaptive fusion model achieves gains of 75.07%, 73.27%, 53.52% and 70.02%. Furthermore, its effectiveness is verified in practical platform.

title: MPVFedLoc: Enabling Pervasive Indoor Localization Through Multi-Perspective Views and Distributed Federated Learning
authors: Yaping Zhu, Ying Qiu, Junyuan Wang, Feng Yan, Shengjie Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: With camera-equipped phones becoming essential items carried by people, utilizing multi-perspective views (MPV) captured from the surrounding environment has emerged as a promising approach to achieve pervasive localization in indoor environments. This MPV-based localization requires extensive data, necessitating the use of crowdsourcing for data collection and training. In this distributed process, multiple clients can process and share results, raising concerns about privacy breaches. To address this challenge, this paper introduces federated learning (FL) into MPV-based localization, resulting in the MPVFedLoc algorithm, which facilitates distributed learning without exchanging raw local data. However, FL-based methods often experience reduced accuracy due to data heterogeneity. To overcome this, we propose a model self-supervised federated learning framework within MPVFedLoc. This framework integrates self-supervised learning at the model level and incorporates a model self-supervised loss into the local training objective to mitigate the bias between the global and local models. To evaluate the performance of MPV-based localization, we construct a benchmark dataset named TJF_Building and conduct extensive experiments. Additionally, we compare the performance of MPVFedLoc with three state-of-the-art FL methods in both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings. Experimental results demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of MPVFedLoc, particularly in handling heterogeneous scenarios. Further experiments on two typical image classification datasets also highlight the potential of MPVFedLoc for diverse tasks.

title: Series-Arc Analysis Using a Normalized FFT Harmonic Energy Ratio in Power Distribution Systems with SFCL
authors: Min-Ho Yoon, Seung-Su Choi, Sung-Hun Lim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Detecting series arc faults in motor-connected distribution systems is challenging due to the dynamic impedance of induction motors. The inclusion of a Resistive Superconducting Fault Current Limiter (SFCL) further complicates detection by potentially dampening the high-frequency arc signatures required for identification. This paper experimentally investigates whether a harmonic-based detection metric remains robust under the combined influence of motor inrush dynamics and SFCL resistive operation. We utilize a three-cycle sliding Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to compute a band-limited harmonic energy ratio, H(k) over the 200–2000 Hz band. Tests compare wye and delta starting methods and two arc locations: the main feeder (Case 1) and the motor branch (Case 2). Experimental results show that under the same line voltage, the delta connection produced a larger natural inrush(≈65A) compared to the wye connection (≈26A). The SFCL effectively functioned as a soft-starter, limiting the delta inrush to 35 A via a resistive quench(≈80V). Although this resistive damping attenuated the absolute arc current spectra, the normalized metric H(k) successfully distinguished arc faults. In Case 1, H(k) consistently exceeded the baseline by a factor of 3 to 8. In Case 2, despite a masking effect caused by the parallel resistive load under wye starting, the metric remained reliable, showing a distinct 5-fold increase in the delta configuration and a gradual rise at the motor terminals. This study validates that the proposed harmonic-energy metric provides a practical, connection-aware indicator for series arc detection, capable of overcoming the signal suppression introduced by the SFCL.

title: Prospects of kinetic inductance current sensors for multiplexed microcalorimeter readout
authors: Galen C. O'Neil, Simon R. Bandler, Ian Fogarty Florang, John A. Mates, Daniel S. Swetz, Thomas R. Stevenson, Leaf A. Swordy, Joel N. Ullom, Michael R. Vissers, Jordan D. Wheeler, Wonsik Yoon, Paul Szypryt
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Superconducting microcalorimeter arrays with hundreds to thousands of pixels are enabling new measurements and capabilities in exotic atom spectroscopy, x-ray astronomy, nuclear materials analysis, and many other fields. We consider the prospects of multiplexing microcalorimeters with the kinetic inductance current sensor (KICS), an alternative to the superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) historically used to read out cryogenic microcalorimeters. We show that the amplifier limited noise of the KICS is determined by its dynamic range, which is a free design parameter and tuneable with a DC current bias. We predict that KICS can meet the requirements to read out prototype metallic magnetic calorimeter (MMC) pixels designed for the Lynx x-ray satellite mission concept with the use of low-latency tone tracking. We propose a KICS design that has both high coupling efficiency and high isolation between the RF and DC components of the circuit. We argue that the KICS provides engineering trade-offs that are better suited to the readout of microcalorimeters when compared to the analogous microwave SQUID multiplexer.

title: MSDT: A Secure Blockchain-Based Multi-Subset Data Trading Protocol with Atomicity and Quality Guarantees
authors: Kaping Xue, Zhuo Xu, Jiawen An, Yingjie Xue, Xinyi Luo, Wentuo Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Access to high-quality data resources is fundamental for generating insights, making decisions, and fostering innovation across various sectors. Data trading makes this possible, by which businesses, researchers, and developers can acquire the critical datasets necessary to propel forward-thinking developments. Traditionally, data trading is conducted through centralized platforms, which face challenges such as single points of failure and user mistrust. To address these issues, blockchain-based data trading protocols have been proposed, offering an alternative way for data trading with no trusted third-party involvement. However, ensuring trading atomicity (i.e. the data requester gets the data and the data owner gets the payment atomically) and data quality in the absence of a trusted third party is challenging. This paper introduces MSDT, a blockchain-based data trading protocol that guarantees trading atomicity and data quality. MSDT is a multi-subset data trading protocol, allowing users to verify data quality during the trading process. In MSDT, a state channel-based trading contract is designed, which reduces on-chain costs while ensuring transactions are executed atomically. Additionally, to deal with potential malicious behaviors of data owners, a staking mechanism and trading strategy are provided. Security and performance analyses demonstrate that MSDT ensures trading atomicity and data quality verification while maintains efficiency comparable to existing solutions.

title: A Pyramid-Free, Memory-Efficient RGB-D Visual Odometry Accelerator via Algorithm–Hardware Codesign
authors: Li-Yang Huang, Pin-Yi Lin, Shao-Yi Chien
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Recent advancements in spatial computing have transformed the interaction paradigm between digital information and physical environments, enabling context-aware applications that enhance daily activities across healthcare, education, entertainment, and industry. At the heart of spatial computing lies visual odometry (VO), which estimates real-time device motion to accurately align virtual content with the real world. However, achieving real-time RGB-D VO on mobile and wearable platforms remains challenging due to limited computing resources, particularly stringent memory constraints. In this article, we present a memory-efficient RGB-D VO accelerator developed via algorithm–hardware codesign. Our method significantly reduces memory consumption by employing a hybrid pipeline that integrates sparse, feature-based initialization with dense, direct-based optimization, thereby eliminating memory-intensive image pyramids. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first pyramid-free RGB-D VO accelerator that achieves real-time operation with only 128 kB of on-chip SRAM, demonstrating the effectiveness of algorithm–hardware codesign in memory-constrained environments. Implemented in TSMC 40-nm CMOS technology, the proposed architecture achieves a 32.78% reduction in memory usage, a 10.20% smaller chip area, and a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.68\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> improvement in frame rate compared to baseline designs, efficiently processing RGB-D data at 34.4 f/s using only 128 kB of on-chip memory.

title: Acoustic 3D Printing of High-Melting-Point Polymers
authors: Mikko Koskenniemi, Joel Jääskeläinen, Martin Weber, Edward Hæggström, Ari Salmi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Ultrasonics, Ferroelectrics, and Frequency Control
abstract: Acoustic 3D printing, A3P, uses focused acoustic energy to locally melt or cure materials, however adoption has been limited by the niche materials used so far. We previously showed the first A3P of a thermoplastic by mixing polycaprolactone powder with water (coupling liquid) to displace trapped air and allow ultrasound transmission. Here, we demonstrate A3P of high-melting-point thermoplastics, polylactic acid (PLA) and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). To achieve this, we use a transducer consisting of a flat PZT disc attached to a focusing aluminium lens for efficient heat dissipation and higher acoustic output, replacing the earlier bowl type focusing element. Replacing water with high-boiling-point polar coupling liquids, namely propylene glycol (PG), polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400), and glycerol, enables operation up to at least 150 °C. PEG-400 yields the most rigid PLA prints and the fastest print times, whereas PG is the most compatible with TPU. We printed the first mechanically functional print made with A3P, a TPU cable tie. These results expand the material set of A3P to widely used 3D printing polymers, putting A3P one step closer to widespread adoption.

title: AI-Driven Synthetic Threats in Cybersecurity: A User-Centered Framework for Awareness, Detection, and Protective Behavior
authors: Reem Almarwani, Maryam Almarwani, Fatimah Almarwani
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has ushered in a new generation of cybersecurity threats driven by highly realistic AI-generated content, including deepfakes, synthetic audio, and algorithmically produced text. Recent international assessments highlight escalating risks related to identity impersonation, large-scale deception, and the manipulation of digital evidence, underscoring the need for socio-technical defense models that go beyond purely technical detection. This paper introduces and empirically validates the AI-Cyber-User Awareness Framework, a unified socio-technical model that explains how AI-driven threats interact with user cognition and cybersecurity behavior. The framework is grounded in Technology Threat Avoidance Theory (TTAT), cognitive security principles, and contemporary research on AI-enabled deception. It is constructed through a structured literature analysis of twenty-five peer-reviewed studies and then quantitatively validated using survey data from n = 144 participants in a Middle Eastern context. The empirical study operationalizes key constructs—AI threat awareness, detection competence, AI literacy, risk perception, and protective cybersecurity behavior—and evaluates their relationships using correlation, regression, and mediation analysis. Results reveal a pronounced confidence-competence gap: objective detection accuracy remains low (mean = 42.53%) despite moderate perceived preparedness. Detection competence emerges as the strongest predictor of protective cybersecurity behavior and significantly mediates the influence of awareness and AI literacy. By integrating technical, cognitive, and behavioral dimensions into a single explanatory model, the proposed framework advances theoretical understanding of AI-driven cyber deception and provides a practical foundation for designing human-centered cybersecurity interventions that prioritize detection skill development rather than awareness-raising alone.

title: BiSTAC: A Class of Bipedal Soft Terrestrial–Aerial Bicopters
authors: Yangguang Liu, Yimin Zhu, Yue Wang, Ruoyu Ren, Lixian Zhang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This paper presents the design, modeling, and motion control of BiSTAC, a novel hybrid terrestrial–aerial vehicle. BiSTAC features an innovative configuration that integrates a bicopter propeller system with a flexible bipedal locomotion mechanism, thereby enabling both bicopter flight and bipedal walking. Compared with other configurations of hybrid terrestrial–aerial vehicles, BiSTAC achieves functional reuse of its actuation system: the same set of actuators simultaneously supports both aerial flight and terrestrial walking, thereby improving the utilization efficiency of the locomotion mechanism while significantly simplifying the overall structural design. The dynamic model of BiSTAC is established, and both an aerial mode controller and a terrestrial controller are designed and implemented. Real-world experiments were conducted on the physical prototype, including flight performance experiments, terrestrial walking experiments, and bimodal locomotion experiments. The results are presented to validate the feasibility of the structural design of BiSTAC and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed controllers.

title: Multi-modal Cooperative Forecasting for Cloud Images and Photovoltaic Power Generation
authors: Baoyu Wang, Mao Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of solar radiation fluctuations at photovoltaic power plants caused by the varying transmittance characteristics of different cloud types. It proposes an ultra-short-term photovoltaic power forecasting method that integrates lightweight cloud image recognition technology with an ARIMA-LSTM hybrid model. First, a lightweight multi-feature interactive guidance architecture is introduced. This employs a multi-feature interaction module to accurately identify structural characteristics in cloud images. Then, the encoding quantization combined with an attention mechanism is used to effectively fuse multi-modal data, including cloud features and power measurements. Finally, an ARIMA-LSTM hybrid time series forecasting model has been constructed that uses the combined effects of linear feature extraction and non-linear pattern learning to achieve precise ultra-short-term power prediction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach can accurately characterize the dynamic optical properties of clouds, effectively solving the problems of significant photovoltaic power fluctuation and high complexity model in cloudy weather conditions.

title: Decoding brain states from EEG using Transformer architectures
authors: Sabina Volcov, Eugen-Richard Ardelean, Vasile Vlad Moca, Raul Cristian Mureşan, Mihaela Dînşoreanu
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) remains one of the most widely used non-invasive methods for investigating brain function, due to its high temporal richness and accessibility. Still, the complex and noisy nature of EEG signals makes decoding neural states a challenging task, particularly when using traditional signal processing or shallow machine learning approaches. In recent years, deep learning models, such as Transformers, have emerged as a powerful alternative to classical machine learning methods, thanks to their capacity for modeling long-range dependencies and learning contextual representations. In this paper, we explore the application of Transformer architectures to EEG data for the purpose of binary and three-class classification in a visual perception task. To evaluate model performance, we compare the Transformer against four other models: XGBoost, Random Forest, a simple Multi-Layer Perceptron and a Long Short-Term Memory network. Our findings suggest that attention-based models are capable of capturing informative temporal dependencies in EEG signals, with preprocessing playing a critical role in optimizing representation quality. This work highlights the potential of Transformer models for non-invasive neural decoding and contributes to the development of more effective tools for cognitive neuroscience and brain-computer interfaces.

title: Critical Node Identification in Power Systems via an Improved DomiRank Algorithm
authors: Lijuan Li, Fengzhao Tian, Guoqing Luo, Yabin Fu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: An improved DomiRank algorithm is developed to enhance the identification of critical nodes in power grids. The approach establishes a dynamic weighted directed graph incorporating power flow load rates and line reactance, and introduces virtual nodes to more accurately capture the connectivity of source and load nodes. A load-driven competition mechanism is integrated into the algorithm, enabling a unified representation of structural characteristics and operational states in evaluating node importance. The effectiveness of the method is verified on the IEEE 39-bus and IEEE 118-bus systems, where comparative analysis with existing approaches using Loss of Load Rate and Topological Efficiency demonstrates superior consistency and greater capability in revealing system vulnerabilities. The proposed algorithm provides a robust analytical basis for formulating power grid defense strategies.

title: From Static Betting to Dynamic Adaptation: Harmonizing the Shorter-Term Forward Market with the Spot Market
authors: Junhan Qin, Qiao Sun, Dingran Cheng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Under China's "dual carbon" directive, the high penetration of renewable energy has created a significant price divergence between the medium-to-long-term contract and spot electricity markets. This phenomenon stems from a fundamental "mechanism mismatch": a contract regime designed for 'security and stability' clashes with a spot market that reflects 'dynamic reality.' Current research has focused on forecasting for either long-term or short-term horizons, systematically neglecting the crucial intermediate timescale—the shorter-term forward market, best represented by weekly forecasts—which is essential for harmonizing these market segments. To fill this void, this paper systematically posits that weekly net-load interval forecasting is the key technical bridge to reconcile the markets and enable a next-generation power market design. The paper first dissects the physical origins of net-load uncertainty and its non-linear amplification into price risk. It then critically assesses the limitations of prevailing interval forecasting and price modeling techniques. Building on this analysis, we propose a dynamic market mechanism design philosophy that leverages weekly uncertainty quantification to transition core market parameters, such as contracting ratios and price limits, from static rules to dynamically adaptive controls responsive to forward-looking risk assessments. The paper concludes by advocating for a new, integrated "prediction-decision" paradigm, thereby providing the scientific foundation for a new-type power system truly resilient to high uncertainty.

title: Intrusion Detection System for Networks Using Hybrid ML Algorithms
authors: Vasanth Nayak, Rashya Shetty, Nisarga Shetty, Ashika, Jashmi M R
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Network Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) play a key role in protecting computer networks from advanced cyber-attacks. However, most existing IDS rely on a single machine learning (ML) algorithm, limiting their detection ability for multi-aspect and dynamic threats. Single-model paradigms suffer from issues of overfitting and poor generalizability to new instances. This work introduces a better hybrid IDS with supervised ML techniques for two standard test sets: CICIDS2019 and TII-SSRC 23. Mutual Information-based feature selection was applied in the case of CICIDS2019 to preserve only the most significant features. Massive preprocessing-normalization, balance, and cleaning-were performed on the massive TII-SSRC-23 dataset, reducing its size to 1 GB from 30 GB without losing most traffic features. The Random Forest and SVM models were trained and cross-validated. As a complement to prediction from datasets, the system has a new feature for real-time forecasting of network attacks through Wi-Fi or LAN interfaces in order to have maximum real-world utilization and responsiveness. The hybrid IDS is therefore better positioned to offer precision, responsiveness, and usability in real-world situations than the single-model IDS of the past.

title: Neuro-Symbolic AI Framework for Automated Concept Reinforcement in Skill-Oriented STEM Education Environments
authors: P. Suria Thilagam, Muhammedali Chalikandy Puthiyapurayil, Sweta Ravindran, N. Sheik Hameed, S. Vijayakumar, R S Sabeenian
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: STEM education increasingly leverages AI-based tutoring systems, yet most rely on opaque neural models that lack interpretability, while symbolic systems struggle to handle unstructured learner responses. Existing approaches [10], [14] often achieve predictive accuracy but fail to deliver conceptlevel, pedagogically aligned, and explainable feedback, limiting skill reinforcement. This study proposes a hybrid NeuroSymbolic AI framework integrating RoBERTa-based contextual embeddings with a symbolic reasoning engine to detect conceptual gaps and provide targeted reinforcement. Implemented in Python using the MathE Mathematics Learning and Assessment dataset, the model processes normalized mathematical notation, maps concepts to a knowledge graph, and applies rule-based verification via SymPy. Results demonstrate a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 4. 5 \%}$</tex> accuracy, representing a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 3 \%}$</tex> improvement over the best baseline, with notable gains in precision <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(93.8 \%)$</tex> and recall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(94.1 \%)$</tex>. The approach ensures interpretable, adaptive learning feedback, bridging the gap between prediction and pedagogy, and offers a scalable, highperformance solution to enhance personalized STEM education outcomes.

title: Control Strategy for DRU-MMC Hybrid Bipolar HVDC Transmission System Integrated with Offshore Wind Power
authors: Xikai Huang, Huangqing Xiao, Huichen Gan
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Symmetrical monopole wiring scheme is adopted for the offshore DC projects that have been put into operation. However, for large-scale remote offshore wind power transmission system, symmetrical monopole wiring scheme presents challenges such as poor reliability and low operational flexibility. A DRU-MMC hybrid bipolar HVDC transmission system integrated with offshore wind power is proposed in this paper along with a system-specific black start strategy to meet the dual goals of lightweight construction and high dependability. Firstly, the system's topology is introduced and its structural characteristics are analyzed. Subsequently, the black start strategy for the system is designed, and the steady-state control strategy is introduced. A simulation model of a ±320 kV/1000 MW DRU-MMC hybrid bipolar HVDC transmission system has been constructed in PSCAD/EMTDC. The simulation results verify the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed topology and its black-start strategy.

title: Electronic Control Unit Mechanical Architecture Optimization using Machine Learning
authors: Diana-Nicoleta Zaha, Virginia Niculescu, Ioan Cosmin Leordean, Diana Zaharie-Butucel
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: This study presents a machine learning-based approach to optimize the design and thermal performance of Electronic Control Units (ECUs) by predicting the Heat Transfer Coefficient (HTC), a key metric in thermal management. ECUs play a critical role in vehicle systems, and efficient thermal control is essential for their reliability. Traditional CFD simulations, while accurate, are time-consuming and resource-intensive. To address this, the study uses data from Computational Fluid Mechanics (CFD) simulations and applies Machine Learning (ML) models to predict HTC based on geometric and thermal inputs. Results show that these models can streamline the design process, supporting rather than replacing simulation engineers, and enabling faster evaluation of viable configurations. This approach promotes more agile, efficient, and sustainable ECU design in the automotive industry.

title: Large-Signal Synchronization Stability Analysis of Electronic Converters Based on Hybrid Grid-Forming and Grid-Following Control Strategy
authors: Shuai Liang, Liangzhong Yao, Jinglei Deng, Zilu Zhang, Tianhao Li, Yao Lin
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Grid-forming control has become the dominant control strategy for renewable energy grid integration, due to its capabilities in providing inertia and voltage support. However, while GFM control is susceptible to small-signal stability issues under strong grid conditions, it is also prone to trigger current limiting during large disturbance transients, which make the converter fail to operate under a normal grid-forming control mode.
