By integrating the synchronization mechanisms of grid-forming and grid-following controls, hybrid grid-forming and grid-following control combines the advantages of both, making it a growing research focus. The grid-connection topology and typical control strategy of hybrid grid-forming and grid-following control are given first in this paper. Subsequently, a time-domain model for analyzing the large-signal synchronization stability of hybrid grid-forming and grid-following control is established. Based on this model and the phase portrait analytical method, the key system and control parameters influencing the large-signal synchronization stability characteristics are investigated. Finally, a grid-connected power electronic converter model based on hybrid grid-forming and grid-following control is developed on the PSCAD/EMTDC simulation platform, and the time-domain simulations are conducted to validate the aforementioned analysis.

title: Charging Infrastructure Planning for Promoting Vehicle to Grid: A Two-Stage Framework Considering Willingness of Electric Vehicle Owners
authors: Xuewei Zhang, Qianqian Yan, Zhenhong Lin, Haobo Dong
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The development of vehicle to grid (V2G) technology and distributed generation resources (DGRs) is driving the transformation of distribution systems toward greater decarbonization and operational flexibility. This phenomenon poses challenges to their coordinated deployment. Hence, this paper proposes a two-stage planning framework to provide suggestions for the coordinated deployment of V2G charging piles and DGRs. Stage 1 integrates real GPS travel data with a V2G willingness model to predict charging demand and V2G capacity of electric vehicles (EVs). Stage 2 optimizes the location and capacity of V2G charging piles and DGRs based on charging demand and V2G capacity, aiming to minimize the social cost associated with V2G. Tianhe District, Guangzhou, China, fed by a 21-bus distribution system is selected as test system to verify the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Research indicates that V2G charging piles tends to be concentrated in areas where EVs have prolonged parking durations. In the design of V2G contracts, annual revenue of V2G, battery degradation warranty period, and battery capacity under warranty should be set as high as possible. For guaranteed state of charge and daily plugged-in hours, values of 0.3 and 5 hour are respectively found to be optimal.

title: Mitigating Power Fluctuations in Energy Storage Systems Using ANN Controller & Optimization Techniques
authors: Rashmi Mishra, Md. Khaja Mohiddin
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This This work illustrates an innovative control scheme for a photovoltaic (PV) model integrated with a Lithium-ion battery-supercapacitor Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS). To maximize battery longevity, an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) controller is deployed to manage power distribution, effectively shielding the battery from high-frequency current transients and intermittent strain. A coordinated closed-loop control strategy, utilizing bi-directional DC-DC converters, facilitates precise charging and discharging cycles. During charging, the ANN optimizes the absorption of active power from the grid. In discharge mode, the controller enables the decoupled regulation of active and reactive power, ensuring stable energy injection into the local microgrid. To ensure peak performance, the weights and biases of the ANN controller are optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO), and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) techniques. This optimization focuses on minimizing peak current stress and enhancing the dynamic response of the HESS. Comparative analysis demonstrates that the proposed ANN-based strategy significantly reduces the high-power requirements placed on the Lithium-ion battery by intelligently diverting transient loads to the supercapacitor. The results confirm a substantial improvement in battery service life and overall system efficiency, highlighting the potential of neural-control architectures for sustainable, long-term energy storage performance.

title: A Frequency and Voltage Support Control Method of Doubly-Fed Wind Power System Linked to DC Grid
authors: Xueshen Cui, Shengyu Ye, Yifei Li, Qihui Liu, Qiwei Zhang, Muyang Zhou
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the increasing integration of renewable energy and power electronics in modern DC distribution network, it is essential for renewable energy generation systems to exhibit certain capabilities in frequency and voltage support. This paper focuses on the topology of doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG) system connected to medium-voltage DC distribution network via diode rectifier and proposes a control method of frequency and voltage support without Phase-Locked Loop (PLL). On one hand, the orientation angle is derived through the integration of given frequency, thereby achieving stator frequency support. Meanwhile, the d-axis component of the stator voltage is controlled to zero via PI regulation to ensure the accuracy of orientation. On the other hand, a voltage intermediate loop is introduced between the active power outer loop and the current inner loop. By means of deloaded operation, the voltage support capability is realized through releasing active power during voltage drop of DC grid. Furthermore, to coordinate frequency and voltage support strategy, the setting of given frequency is regulated in proportion to stator voltage, which ensures the magnetic linkage remains constant throughout the entire wind speed range. A 5MW DFIG system model is developed in the MATLAB/Simulink. The effectiveness of the proposed control strategy is verified by simulation results.

title: Research on Power Emergency Long Text Generation Technology Enhanced by Knowledge Graph Retrieval
authors: Zehao Zhang, Jie Feng, Zeyu Liu, Cheng Guan, Yexin Zhang, Xiyuan Xu, Baiju Yan
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Aiming at the problems such as lack of professional knowledge, generation of illusions and insufficient context consistency existing in the generation of long texts for power emergency, this paper proposes a long text generation method based on knowledge graph retrieval enhancement. This method adaptively searches for key entities and relationships in the knowledge graph through graph reinforcement learning, integrates local features and global features to achieve precise knowledge retrieval, and constructs a global-local multi-agent large model framework: The global model generates summaries and table of contents to determine the overall structure, while the local model generates chapter content based on the retrieved structured knowledge. Through the iterative interaction of multiple agents, the professionalism and coherence of long texts are optimized. The experimental results show that the proposed method significantly improves the accuracy and consistency in the task of generating long texts for power emergencies, providing great assistance for power emergency response.

title: A Blockchain-based Framework for Microgrids Energy Trading Using Greedy Mechanisms
authors: Iulia-Olivia Cipleu, Viorica Rozina Chifu, Cristina Bianca Pop, Ionut Anghel, Tudor Cioara
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: This paper proposes a decentralized framework for managing energy transfers between interconnected microgrids (MGs) and the main grid, using smart contracts implemented on a blockchain infrastructure. Using a predictive control mechanism (MPC) to estimate local consumption and production, the Distributed System Operator (DSO) can detect energy imbalances at the MG level and initiate the process of redistributing energy between MGs to cover the imbalances. Microgrids participate in the trading process through a coordinated notification system, avoiding allocation conflicts. The selection of trading partners is based on transfer scores in a greedy manner, and energy exchanges are tokenized through the ERC-20 standard. Experimental results indicate that, following the trading process, MGs reach an energy balance, with stability levels ranging between $85.85 \%$ and $90.13 \%$. On average, $94.3 \%$ of the energy demand is covered locally, and only $5.6 \%$ comes from imports from the main grid.

title: A Review of Optimal Dispatch Strategies for Active Distribution Networks
authors: Qianran Cheng, Yaping Li, Xinxin Zhang, Shengchun Yang, Wenbo Mao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper reviews the research status of optimal dispatch strategies for active distribution networks (ADNs) and systematically analyzes their technical characteristics from dimensions such as multi-objective, multi-time scale, and multilevel. The optimal dispatching algorithm for ADNs effectively improves the flexibility, economy, and reliability of ADN operation by integrating controllable elements such as distributed generations, energy storage systems, controllable loads, electric vehicles, and microgrids, considering prediction accuracy under different time scales, and coordinating the interests of multiple subjects. Meanwhile, the study explores the role of demand response (DR) dispatch and virtual power plants (VPPs) dispatch as two market-oriented means in ADNs, providing a reference for promoting the technical development of ADN optimal dispatch strategies.

title: Power System Forest Corridor Safety Early Warning Based on Analytic Hierarchy Process
authors: He Wang, Shengzong Zhang, Shengxu Zhou, Jiazhi Guo, Zihan Jia, Yiqi Xiong, Xiaoting Du
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To prevent faults in power system forest corridors, this study employs the Delphi–Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) and a scoring correction formula as the core technical framework, integrating a risk assessment correction mechanism to construct a comprehensive fault risk assessment and early-warning model. First, the Delphi method and a nine-point scale are applied to conduct expert evaluations of risk factors associated with forest corridors in power systems, introducing a methodological integration of Delphi and AHP to improve the objectivity and consistency of expert judgment. Second, a hierarchical risk assessment model is established, constructing a judgment matrix to calculate the relative weights of each influencing factor, followed by consistency and feasibility verification to ensure model reliability. Subsequently, a correction formula is proposed to optimize the comprehensive risk score, enhancing its precision and robustness. Finally, a power system risk assessment and early-warning system is developed, with simulation experiments and real-world data applied to validate the system’s effectiveness, stability, and the rationality of the proposed correction mechanism.

title: Research on Optimization Calculation of Wideband Transient Measurement Software
authors: Yinglong Diao, Zhaoyang Qu, Wenting Li, Jie Cao, Xiaoyu Xu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The calculation software plays a crucial role in the secondary-side measurement system of impulse voltage testing. The accuracy of the software has a significant impact on the measurement uncertainty of the whole measurement system. In this study, the test data generator (TDG 2.0.6) specified in IEC 61083:2013 was used to evaluate the measurement accuracy of the software in the secondary side of a lightning impulse measurement system. The test results indicate that the T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> data of the waveforms with oscillation exceeds the allowable error. To address this issue, interpolation and dual curve-fitting methods were applied to improve the accuracy of the measurement software. A comparative analysis with two foreign software packages demonstrates that the measurement accuracy of all three software systems is generally at the same level.

title: Analysis and Comparison of Techniques for Artificially Preparing Typical Aero-Engine Disk Material Defects
authors: Xingyu Zhang, Huimin Zhou, Guo Li, Shuiting Ding, Gong Zhang, Bo Zhen
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: Material defects in titanium alloys, powder alloys, and wrought superalloys can compromise the structural integrity of aero-engine disks, thereby leading to uncontained failure, which poses a significant threat to aircraft safety. Studying how material defects affect rotor disks provides technical support for improving aircraft safety. Since natural defects rarely occur during normal production, researchers have developed methods to prepare defect-containing materials artificially. These methods primarily utilize hot isostatic pressing (HIP) to bond the defects into materials. Artificial defects are now well-established for titanium alloys and powder metallurgy alloys and are widely used in research. This paper describes the existing methods for creating artificial defects in titanium alloys and powder metallurgy alloys. Besides, this paper presents experiments creating "dirty white spot" defects in GH4169 superalloy. The experimental results show that although GH4169 with dirty white spot defects prepared by HIP can meet the requirements of ultrasonic testing research, the low mechanical strength of the HIP interface will affect the fatigue test results. Then, the differences and causes of the three types of materials containing artificial defects were compared and analyzed.

title: Transient Synchronization Stability Analysis of Multi-VSCs Grid-Connected System Considering Coupling Effects
authors: Gaoshang Zhao, Jun An, Jing Li, Yibo Zhou
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Under severe grid faults, the transient synchronization stability of multiple voltage source converters (MVSCs) becomes more complex than that of single-converter systems due to intricate interactions and couplings. However, existing research lacks a clear and systematic understanding of the underlying mechanisms. Hence, this paper establishes an equivalent nonlinear model of MVSCs that accounts for coupling effects. Based on this model, we analyze the influence pathways and coupling mechanisms of interactive terms in the mathematical model of MVSCs, and examine three typical dynamic modes during transients. Simulation results verify the revealed coupling laws and dynamic behaviors. This study deepens the understanding of the dynamic evolution and multi-converter coupling mechanisms in MVSCs under large disturbances, providing theoretical insights for parameter tuning and control strategy optimization.

title: Calculation Method and Experimental Verification of Additional Losses in Distribution Transformers Caused by Power Quality Issues
authors: Longfei Wang, Yi Wang, Shuangyin Dai, Wenjun Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The calculation of additional losses in distribution transformers is a key foundation for accurately quantifying the extra energy consumption caused by power quality disturbances, supporting equipment lifespan assessment and the formulation of energy-saving strategies, and ensuring the economical and safe operation of distribution networks. However, most existing calculation models focus on a single type of power quality disturbance. To address this issue, calculation models for additional losses under three types of power quality disturbances are established, and field test verification is conducted based on State Grid's comprehensive power quality test platform. The results show that the models have small errors between calculated and measured values with reliable accuracy; three-phase unbalance and harmonics induce nonlinear growth of additional losses, and the impact of harmonic content is greater than that of harmonic order; the influence of voltage deviation on additional losses is related to load conditions, and increasing voltage during peak load periods and reducing voltage during off-peak periods can reduce losses. This research provides technical support for the accurate evaluation of additional losses and energy-saving optimization in distribution networks.

title:
A Bilayer Lif/C
                    <sub>60</sub>
                    Interfacial Strategy for Hight Carrir Mobility Inverted Pbs QD SWIR Photodetectors
authors: Andong Zhong, Tianjing Guo, Qian Chen, Yong Xia, Wei Chen, Haodong Tang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Energy, Materials, and Photonics (EMP)
abstract: Lead sulfide (PbS) quantum dot (QD) short-wave infrared (SWIR) photodetectors have witnessed remarkable performance breakthroughs, showcasing immense potential for high-sensitivity infrared detection applications. However, the presence of numerous uncoordinated surface states at QD interfaces create trapping sites, while these defects induce detrimental interactions between QDs and adjacent functional layers, adversely impacting carrier injection and transport. To address these challenges, we propose an innovative bilayer <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{LiF} / \mathrm{C}_{60}$</tex> interface structure strategy. The dissociated fluoride ions from LiF effectively coordinate with lead ions to diminish QD surface defect density. Simultaneously, the LiF modification layer serves as a protective barrier, preventing direct contact between PbS QDs and the electron transport layer, thereby minimizing physical damage to QDs and significantly enhancing device performance.

title: Reliable Data Routing in Parallel Systems Through Identification of All Possible 1-Fault Tolerant Beacon Sets in Honeycomb Torus Networks
authors: Libin Bright J M, Simon Raj F
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Interconnection networks are essential components in parallel computing where multiple processors communicate to perform complex computations effectively. These networks optimize data routing by ensuring high-speed, reliable communication, and low latency, thereby enhancing overall system performance and fault tolerance. The effectiveness of an interconnection network is determined by a variety of parameters, including latency, bandwidth, fault tolerance and routing algorithms. One crucial component that determines these characteristics is the concept of beacon dimension, also known as metric dimension. Beacon set (metric basis or a minimum resolving set) of a graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{G}$</tex> is defined as the smallest set of nodes required to uniquely determine the positions of every other node using shortest path distances. The cardinality of a beacon set is known as beacon dimension, denoted as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\operatorname{dim}(G)$</tex>. The total number of all possible beacon sets of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex> is defined as BIGS index. The 1-fault tolerant beacon dimension of a graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex> is defined as the minimum number of nodes required such that even after the removal of any one node from the beacon set, the remaining nodes can still uniquely determine the positions of all nodes in the graph <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex>. The total number of all possible 1-fault tolerant beacon sets of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G$</tex> is defined as 1DRW index. In this study, the 1-fault tolerant beacon dimension of Honeycomb Torus networks, denoted as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{H C T}(\boldsymbol{n})$</tex>, have been determined. This network enhances traditional Honeycomb-based structures by improving connectivity and increasing fault tolerance. 1-fault tolerant beacon dimension analysis of this network directly contributes to improving localization, navigation, fault management, and routing efficiency, making it a critical parameter for real-world applications in parallel computing, robotics, and sensor systems. Further, all possible 1fault tolerant beacon sets of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{H C T (n) }$</tex>, are computed using extended BIGS (Bharati-Indira-George-Simon) algorithm. In addition to this, 1DRW index, Beacon index, and PJS index of Honeycomb Torus networks have been examined.

title: Research On Dynamic Analysis Method for Operational Reliability of Important Power Communication Services Based on Time-Space Correlation
authors: Yuting Lu, Shengchao Yan, Naiping Zhang, Xianfeng Gao, Shu Zhou, Dongkun Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper proposes a dynamic analysis method for the operational reliability of important power communication services based on spatiotemporal correlation. By constructing a service reliability model and a hidden data mining algorithm based on knowledge graphs, it achieves spatiotemporal correlation risk assessment and analysis for operation and maintenance. It proposes an algorithm for dynamic analysis of important service reliability and risk response strategies, implements a real-time intelligent analysis algorithm for routing planning, supports functions such as route recommendation during planning and auxiliary analysis during operation, and comprehensively supports intelligent decision-making and automatic generation of risk response strategies for communication scheduling operations under complex operating conditions.

title: Independent Energy Storage Frequency Regulation Capacity Bidding Strategy Considering the Initial SOC Dynamic Adjustment
authors: Jiajie Liu, Yanbing Jia, Zihui Wang, Bo Zhang, Ran Tan, Chengchen Du
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the large-scale grid integration of renewable energy, the bidding strategy for energy storage (ES) stations, as high-quality frequency regulation (FR) resources, is crucial for sustainable development. However, the conventional approaches of a fixed initial state of charge (SOC) and a singular focus on revenue maximization limit the utilization efficiency and market revenue of ES stations. Therefore, an FR bidding strategy for ES stations that incorporates dynamic adjustment of the initial SOC is proposed. Firstly, the probabilistic distance fast reduction method is employed to model the uncertainty of FR signals. Secondly, a dynamic initial SOC adjustment strategy based on energy accumulation is proposed. Finally, a day-ahead FR capacity bidding model is established, which considers the costs of the ES station and aims to optimize the rate of return. The simulation results indicate that the proposed strategy enhances the regulation capability of the ES station in response to asymmetric FR signals, and the formulation of the optimal bidding strategy improves its FR revenue.

title: Non-Intrusive Detection and Localization of Radial Deformation in Transformer Windings Using Correlation-Based Eigenvalue Analysis
authors: Manoj Samal, Mithun Mondal
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Radial deformation (RD) in transformer windings represents a critical fault condition that threatens power system reliability. Current diagnostic methods either require invasive procedures or lack precise fault localization capabilities. This paper presents a novel non-intrusive methodology for detecting and localizing RD using terminal impedance measurements and correlation-based eigenvalue analysis. The approach employs ladder network modeling with direct parameter estimation, eliminating iterative optimization while enabling precise fault localization through eigenvalue pattern matching. Comprehensive validation through circuit simulations, finite element analysis (FEA), and experiments on a 70 kVA transformer demonstrates 100% detection accuracy with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.95. Parameter estimation exhibits errors below 6% compared to FEA models, confirming the method's reliability for practical transformer condition monitoring applications.

title: Two-Stage Market Trading Strategy for Distribution Network Clusters Based on Electricity Price Guidance
authors: He Wang, Yiwen Wang, Caiqi Zhou, Yang Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To address supply–demand fluctuations in distribution networks with high renewable penetration, this paper proposes a two-stage market trading strategy based on electricity price guidance. A dual-layer framework integrating day-ahead planning and intra-day adjustment is established to coordinate multi-cluster trading. In the day-ahead stage, clusters formulate trading plans according to wind–solar forecasts and load characteristics; in the intra-day stage, hourly corrections are made considering real-time load and electric-vehicle (EV) charging/discharging behavior. A price mechanism enables internal power complementarity and cross-cluster coordination. Case studies on the IEEE-33 bus system verify that the proposed strategy effectively guides demand response and EV storage participation, reduces peak–valley differences and operating costs, and enhances renewable energy utilization and system stability. This research provides a feasible reference for market-oriented and low-carbon operation of distribution networks.

title: Research on Wind Turbine Multi-source Data-driven Predictive Diagnosis and Active Risk Early Warning Method for Safe Operation of the Wind Power System
authors: Leqi Chi, Tao Ma, Lin Tan, Yuhang Lang, Xinran Lv, Xiaoguang Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The integration of large-scale wind power into the Energy Internet presents significant challenges for grid security due to sudden turbine component failures. This study presents a sequential LSTM-Transformer framework for early fault prediction of wind turbine gearbox and bearing components, utilizing high-frequency sensor data from the NREL dataset. The model captures both short-term temporal dynamics and long-range dependencies in multivariate vibration and temperature signals, enabling accurate, pre-fault predictions. Based on these forecasts, a predict-and-derate control strategy is developed to turn abrupt turbine shutdowns into managed, grid-friendly power adjustments. System-level simulations show that this approach smooths plant-level power output, reducing the Rate of Change of Frequency by 25% and lowering frequency nadir deviations by 18% during impending failures. By connecting component-level predictive maintenance with system-level control, the framework provides a validated pathway for enhancing the security, resilience, and flexibility of wind-integrated power systems, highlighting the potential of predictive digital twin-enabled wind farm operation.

title: Voiceprint-based fault monitoring system for wind turbines
authors: Wenquan Li, Yinke Dou, Xiaoyi Zhang, Bin Wang, Liwei Kou, Yuchen Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To address the challenges of difficult fault detection and high maintenance costs during wind-turbine operation, we design a fault monitoring system for wind turbines based on bone-conduction voiceprint sensors. The system adopts a layered architecture—comprising a perception layer, a network transmission layer, and an application layer—and leverages edge computing to perform real-time acquisition and preliminary analysis of acoustic signals from critical components. Abnormal segments are sent via wired links to a server for storage and alerting. An RK3588 SoC serves as the computing core, integrating multi-channel audio interfaces and Gigabit Ethernet. A deep-learning framework combining ResNet with the CBAM attention module enhances weak-feature extraction, while a lightweight strategy reduces parameters and latency to meet embedded deployment constraints. Field tests in an operating wind farm show that the accuracy-oriented configuration (ResNet+CBAM) achieves 97.20% multi-class diagnostic accuracy (a +1.25 percentage-point gain over the baseline ResNet). The lightweight model attains 94.86% accuracy with only 0.88M parameters, a 3.43 MB footprint, and 0.46 ms single-frame inference latency, significantly cutting compute and memory costs. Results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves high accuracy and low latency while retaining engineering deployability, effectively supporting intelligent O&M of wind-power equipment.

title: Adaptive Resonance Suppression Strategy Based on Dynamic Grid Impedance Identification for Grid-connected Inverters
authors: Guangxi Li, Fuchang Yue, Yang Gu, Mingzhe Zhu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Grid impedance can decrease the resonance stability of grid-connected inverters, where the value of grid impedance is hardly to be determined. To address this issue, this paper proposes an adaptive control scheme with the consideration of phase-locked loop, which implements a dynamic harmonic injection-based grid impedance identification method to accurately identify the value of grid impedance. By modifying the PLL input signal to mitigate issues caused by excessive grid impedance, the proposed approach effectively suppresses resonance. Experimental results conclusively validate both the correctness and effectiveness of the implemented strategy.

title: RetinoScope: Advancing Eye Disease Diagnosis Through AI-Powered Multi-Fusion Classification
authors: R.L.Mani Malar, R. Valarmathi
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Retinal diseases should be detected early and accurately to avoid vision loss. It is a research paper on RetinoScope, a state-of-the-art AI-based multi-fusion platform to perform automated classification of retinal diseases such as diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and macular degeneration based on fundus images as well as OCT images. RetinoScope is a triple-stream model, combining a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract local features, a Residual Attention Network (RANet) to enhance the features with lesion focus, and a Vision Transformer (ViT) to have a global understanding. Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) is used to combine features of these streams at the feature level and Weighted Probabilistic Averaging is used to combine features at the decision level with a Genetic Algorithm (GA-WPA). The framework utilizes preprocessing methods such as contrast enhancement, filtering of vessels and optic discs and augmentation of the data in order to enhance generalization. Given explainable AIs such as Grad-CAM and Attention Rollout, explainable heatmaps are clinically interpretable. RetinoScope was evaluated on multiple datasets, which are APTOS 2019, MESSIDOR 2, Retina OCT 2017, DRIONSDB and HRF and showed 98.9% accuracy, 99.1% sensitivity, and 98.7 % specificity, which is higher than single architecture models. These findings indicate that RetinoScope provides a powerful, interpretable, and clinical solution to automated retinal disease diagnosis, which allows cross-modality synergy and allows further development of teleophthalmology solutions.

title: 3D Electronic-Photonic Heterogeneous Interconnect Platforms Enabling Energy-Efficient Scalable Architectures For Future HPC Systems
authors: Anirban Samanta, Shun-Hung Lee, Chun-Yi Cheng, Samuel Palermo, S. J. Ben Yoo
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: 3D interconnects have emerged as a solution to address the scaling issues of interconnect bandwidth and the memory wall problem in high-performance computing (HPC), such as High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM). However, the copper based electrical interconnect retains fundamental limitations. Dense I/O for high-speed signals lead to degraded signal quality for end-to-end links, necessitating additional circuits to mitigate signal impairments and resulting in poor energy efficiency, We propose a 3D chiplet stacking electronic-photonic interconnect (EPIC) platform, which offers a solution by moving the high speed data communication interface to the optical domain across the 3D stack by using Through Silicon Optical Vias (TSOV), while retaining the functionality of electrical TSVs and 2.5D interconnects for power delivery and short-reach low-latency communications. We then benchmark the proposed model against state-of-the-art 3D electrical interconnects to demonstrate our 3D EPIC platform beating the 3D electrical interconnects to >10 TB/s/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> bandwidth density. We present a pathway to extend our demonstrated, industry-ready design to achieving ≤100 fJ/bit high-speed communication.

title: Advanced Machine Learning Optimization of a Graphene-Based Terahertz Biosensor for Urine Glucose Detection
authors: Hamza Wertani, Hamza Ben Krid, Aymen Hlali, Hassen Zairi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: This article presents a tunable graphene-based terahertz (THz) biosensor for high-accuracy and label-free urine glucose detection. By exploiting the electrostatic tunability of graphene, the proposed sensor exhibits well-defined and controllable resonance shifts in response to refractive index variations associated with different urine glucose levels. The device operates within a biologically relevant refractive index range of 1.335–1.347 RIU and achieves a high sensitivity of up to 800 GHz/RIU, enabling reliable discrimination among normal, low, moderate, and high urine glucose concentrations. In addition, the biosensor demonstrates a high quality factor of 49.17 and a figure of merit (FOM) reaching 2.376 RIU<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>, confirming its strong spectral selectivity and sensing efficiency. To further enhance prediction accuracy and optimize sensing performance, a machine-learning-based approach using a K-nearest neighbor (KNN) regression model is employed, yielding a high coefficient of determination (R<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{2} = 0.988$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) with reduced computational complexity. The strong sensing performance is supported by effective plasmonic field confinement inherent to the graphene-based THz biosensor. Owing to its compact design, high sensitivity, and compatibility with data-driven analysis, the proposed THz biosensor represents a promising platform for noninvasive urine glucose monitoring and future integration into intelligent biomedical sensing systems.

title:
AHC-NeRF:
                    <u>A</u>
                    utonomous,
                    <u>H</u>
                    igh-Quality Neural Reconstruction of Two-Layer
                    <u>C</u>
                    omplex Nested Transparent Objects
authors: Youcheng Cai, Fan Gao, Yibo Zhao, Li Li, Ligang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Reconstructing transparent objects with high fidelity presents significant challenges due to complex light refraction and reflection. Existing methods rely on intentionally designed patterns observed behind the transparent object to infer the correspondence between rays and the background, thereby improving the precision of the reconstruction. However, they are hindered by a refraction-tracing-based strategy that fails to reconstruct complex nested transparent objects and a tedious view-capture strategy relying on images captured from empirically determined viewpoints. To overcome these obstacles, we propose AHC-NeRF, an autonomous, high-quality neural SDF-based framework designed for reconstructing two-layer complex nested transparent objects. Firstly, our framework combines neural SDF with single-pixel imaging, a reflection-based method, which utilizes point-pair priors as guidance to achieve high-quality reconstruction of both the outer and inner surfaces. Secondly, we propose an adaptive single-pixel imaging method that achieves an acceleration of 1-2 orders of magnitude compared to vanilla single-pixel imaging for the acquisition of point-pair priors. Finally, we introduce a novel view-planning strategy that progressively identifies the viewpoints with the highest information gain throughout the optimization process, thereby achieving high-quality surface reconstruction. Extensive experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that AHC-NeRF outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: A novel active-microwave method to estimate 1 km vegetation optical depth over the Heihe River Basin
authors: Zhixuan Lu, Lei Fan, Gabrielle De Lannoy, Xiaojing Bai, Xiangzhuo Liu, Zanpin Xing, Zhilan Zhou, Jiangyuan Zeng, Frédéric Frappart, Jian Peng, Tao Che, Liying Geng, Xiaojun Li, Xin Li, Nicolas Baghdadi, Jean-Pierre Wigneron
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The present study proposes a method for retrieving C-band vegetation optical depth (VOD) using high-resolution Sentinel-1 VV-polarized backscatter data over the Heihe River Basin. The Water Cloud Model (WCM) is coupled separately with two semi-empirical soil backscatter models—the Dubois model and the Oh model—as well as with the linear empirical Ulaby model. Pixel-wise soil model parameters are derived using a Random Forest approach based on land surface parameters, while vegetation model parameters are calibrated on a daily basis. This combined strategy effectively enhances the accuracy of VOD retrieval. The performance of the three VOD products was evaluated through correlation analysis with biomass and vegetation indices, followed by a comparative analysis among the different VOD retrievals. The results indicate that the VOD derived from the WCM coupled with the Dubois model outperforms the alternative approaches. This is evidenced by its higher correlations with vegetation indices, yielding average correlation coefficients (R) of 0.80 with the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), 0.80 with the Leaf Area Index (LAI), and 0.80 with the Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI). Furthermore, a higher degree of agreement was found between the soil backscatter and the simulations using the Dubois model, with a coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.82, a Root Mean Square Error of 1.83 dB, a bias of 0.18 dB and a Mean Absolute Error of 1.2 dB. The findings of this study demonstrate that the proposed VOD algorithms, utilizing the WCM in conjunction with the Dubois model, offers the prospect of active high-resolution VODs

title: Molecular-driven Multi-view Hypergraph Contrastive Learning for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction_supp1-3672913.pdf
authors: Qiao Ning
publish: N/A
abstract: Recent concerns have arisen over adverse reactions caused by drug combinations, and drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction helps identify potential risks by forecasting interactions between drugs. Previous methods have primarily explored drug interactions from the superficial level of drug molecules, often overlooking the internal structural information of the molecules. To this end, we propose Mol-HCL, a multi-view hypergraph contrastive learning framework based on molecular view. In this framework, we construct the molecular view to learn the internal information of drug molecules and, based on this, develop structural view and semantic view. These three views collaboratively learn both intra-molecular and inter-molecular information. Subsequently, we incorporate hypernodes into the structural view and design a novel hyperchain, integrating it into the semantic view to capture latent neighbor drug node structural relationships and long-range DDI chain semantic information. After that, contrastive learning is performed between the structural hypergraph and the molecular view, as well as between the semantic hypergraph and the molecular view, to enhance the representations learned from the molecular view. Finally, we conduct experiments on two real-world scientific datasets. The experimental results demonstrate a significant improvement of Mol-HCL over existing methods, showcasing its effectiveness and advantages in DDI prediction. The data and source code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ningq669/Mol-HCL</uri>.

title: Key Factors Affecting Dynamic Reliability in GaN HEMTs and How Can They Be Mitigated?
authors: Swastik Kumar Sahu, Kaushik Mazumdar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: Dynamic reliability remains a major barrier to large scale deployment of GaN based high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs) in demanding RF and power applications. In this paper we analyse recent experimental and modeling advances that link microscopic trap physics to macroscopic, application-level failure modes such as current collapse, gate/drain lag, dynamic on resistance increase, and threshold-voltage drift. We compare characterization methods (pulsed I–V, DLTS variants, charge-pumping, RF large-signal testing, and optical mapping) in terms of temporal and spatial resolution and practical relevance. Mitigation approaches at device, material, and circuit/system levels are analysed including surface passivation, field-plate design, buffer engineering, gate-stack optimization, thermal management, and cascode/circuit solutions.

title: On Harnessing EEG Signals for the Comprehensive Assessment of Neurological Disorders: A Review_supp1-3673111.pdf
authors: Abdelkader Nasreddine Belkacem
publish: N/A
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a scientific technique used to analyze and decode brain activity to identify different neurodegenerative diseases and psychiatric disorders. The insufficiency of specialists and neurologists is attributed to the prevalence of intricate disorders. Prompt and accurate diagnosis is crucial in managing any healthcare issue. EEG signal has shown its value in the prompt identification and prediction of diseases, hence providing valuable assistance to specialists and neurologists. This systematic review examines and analyzes the use of EEG signals for the diagnosis of different disorders and the identification of their biomarkers, drawing on existing research. The research aims to examine the use of EEG signals in the diagnostic process and determine whether a disorder may be predicted using EEG signals in advance. This raises novel research inquiries, which are investigated via 100 academic publications. Findings: EEG signals have been shown to be effective not only for early-stage diagnosis but also for predicting diseases and disorders before they arise, such as Bipolar detection, cancer detection, and the prediction of harmful brain activities. The results indicate that EEG signals can provide novel prospects for more exploration, such as the prediction and prognosis of diseases.

title: SentinelEdge: An Attention-Based Defense for Real-Time Mitigation of Adversarial Thermal Manipulations in System-on-Chips
authors: Mehdi Elahi, Mohamed R. Elshamy, Abdel-Hameed A. Badawy, Ahmad Patooghy
publish: ACM Transactions on Embedded Computing Systems
abstract: None

title: Low-Latency Telepresence via Gaussian Splatting with Safety-Aware Control for Mobile Manipulators
authors: Gyuree Kang, Yeonchan Kim, Juhyeong Roh, David Hyunchul Shim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: While telepresence promises immersive remote access, delivering stable, low-latency, and safe viewpoint control in complex real-world settings remains an open challenge. Museum-like environments, in particular, are visually dense, frequently reconfigured, and poorly matched to static map-based systems, making accurate global 3D reconstruction difficult to maintain. At the same time, producing the natural 6-DoF head motion essential for high-quality immersion is hindered by platform instability and the morphological differences between humans and robots. To address these limitations, we present a mobile-manipulator-based telepresence system that provides smooth, 6-DoF viewpoint control using a high-DoF robotic arm. We also integrate with an on-demand 3D reconstruction pipeline based on Gaussian Splatting, which enables low-latency local rendering and reduces perception delay during rendering phase. The system further supports natural-language navigation for intuitive large-space navigation and integrates a real-time collision-avoidance strategy to ensure safe operation near delicate artifacts in cluttered exhibition environments. Through extensive experiments conducted in a real museum-like indoor setting, we demonstrate that our system delivers low-latency visual feedback, stable and precise viewpoint control, and reliable collision-free operation, enabling responsive, immersive, and safe telepresence in environments where conventional methods struggle to achieve comparable performance.

title: Fortifying Deepfake Detection With AI and Postquantum Cryptography-Based Watermarking Technique
authors: Ch. Rupa, P. Asmitha, S. Nikitha, Jijing Cai, Kai Fang, Thippa Reddy Gadekallu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: In the digital era, deepfakes have become a critical threat to social media platforms and online information systems, undermining public trust and enabling misinformation campaigns at unprecedented scales. The advancements of artificial intelligence have promoted the rapid development of deepfake technology which enables the manipulation of visual content in highly realistic ways. While this technology holds potential for creative applications, it poses serious risks to digital platforms, public trust, and authenticity to online information. This growing concern demands efficient deepfake detection mechanisms. However, many studies primarily focus on facial manipulations, overlooking nonfacial alterations thereby leaving critical vulnerabilities unaddressed. So, this article proposes a novel approach that combines custom autoencoder (CA) based watermarking with postquantum cryptographic technique to detect the facial and nonfacial deepfakes. The proposed system detects deepfakes by extracting facial region as region of interest (ROI) and nonfacial region as nonregion of interest (NROI) using RetinaFace. A $\text{SHA}_{256}$ hash, h ${}_{1}$ is generated for NROI and encrypting it using Nth-degree truncated polynomial ring (NTRU). This encrypted h ${}_{1}$ is embedded into ROI using CA to generate watermarked image. During extraction, NROI of watermarked image is reprocessed to calculate a new hash, h ${}_{2}$ . The encrypted h ${}_{1}$ is extracted from ROI and decrypted to retrieve h ${}_{1}$ , which is then compared with h ${}_{2}$ . Any mismatch reveals potential deepfakes. Experimental validation shows that our method achieves a high peak signal-to-noise ratio of 80.29 dB and an structural similarity index measure of 0.90, indicating that the watermark is invisible. It achieves 96% detection accuracy on our test set. Unlike most current methods that focus only on the face, our approach also identifies tampering with the body or background. Additionally, by using the NTRU encryption algorithm, it is protected against future quantum computer attacks. This makes it a practical and secure solution for safeguarding real-world digital media.

title: Transferable Adversarial Attacks on 3D Point Cloud Semantic Segmentation via Diffusion Models in Autonomous Driving
authors: Yizhou Wang, Libing Wu, Zhuangzhuang Zhang, Lijuan Huo, Jiaqi Feng, Jing Wang, Jiong Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: With the rapid advancement of LiDAR-based 3D semantic segmentation, it has provided effective support for comprehensive perception in autonomous driving. However, the vulnerabilities of existing segmentation models have not been fully explored, posing potential risks to the security of autonomous systems. Existing adversarial attacks on point cloud segmentation primarily focus on applying global perturbations to point coordinates, inevitably introducing noticeable outliers and resulting in suboptimal attack performance. Inspired by the remarkable performance of diffusion models in LiDAR scene completion tasks, we propose a novel adversarial attack framework using diffusion models targeting 3D semantic segmentation. First, we model forward and reverse diffusion processes for input point cloud scenes, then introduce targeted perturbations during the reverse diffusion sampling stage. Specifically, we initialize Gaussian noise and add it to partial input point clouds, followed by stepwise denoising for point cloud reconstruction. During each denoising step, we identify the most discrimination-sensitive critical points and apply adversarial gradients exclusively to these selected points for guiding updates. Furthermore, we design a triple-loss function to supervise adversarial point cloud generation, achieving a balance between attack performance and perturbation magnitude. Finally, we conduct comprehensive experiments on two autonomous driving datasets, SemanticKITTI and nuScenes. The experimental results indicate that our method delivers significant attack performance against six mainstream semantic segmentation models, with average relative mIoU drops reaching 61.14% and 51.55% respectively. Moreover, the proposed method successfully transfers to point cloud classification and part segmentation tasks, demonstrating superior attack performance and transferability.

title: Buffer-free HEMT with Ultra-Thin AlN Back Barrier and GaN channel Suppressing Drain Lag and Quiescent Drain Current Drift
authors: Yachao Zhang, Kelin Wang, Xing Chen, Wenjun Liu, Hong Zhou, Huantao Duan, Yue Hao, Jincheng Zhang
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: In this work, we investigate a buffer-free gallium nitride high electron mobility transistor (GaN HEMT) featuring an ultra-thin AlN back barrier and GaN channel structure. It effectively suppresses defect activation, significantly reducing the trapping/de-trapping of the two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG). The device demonstrates excellent characteristics in suppressing drain lag and quiescent drain current (IDQ) drift performances. Pulse measurements reveal that under 20 V drain bias, drain lag decreases from 48.8% to 12.5%, while under RF stress, IDQ drift reduces from 64.03% to 14.1%, with 90% recovery time shortened from 470 μs to 15 μs. This work provides an effective solution at the material-structure level for enhancing the RF amplification stability and reliability of GaN HEMTs.

title: Movable Antenna-Enabled Integrated Sensing and Communication for Range-Doppler Sidelobe Suppression
authors: Qingxia Feng, Meng Hua, Chunguo Li, Yongming Huang, Luxi Yang
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This paper investigates a movable-antenna (MA)-enabled integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system and formulates a joint optimization problem for minimizing the integrated sidelobe level (ISL). Specifically, we jointly optimize the transmit beamforming and the antenna position vector subject to multiuser quality-of-service (QoS) requirements, radar illumination power constraint, and a total transmit power budget. The resulting problem is a highly nonconvex optimization problem. To address it, we develop a two-layer particle swarm optimization (TL-PSO) algorithm that incorporates semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxation in the inner layer. Simulation results show that the proposed MA-enabled ISAC system achieves pronounced sidelobe suppression by exploiting the additional spatial degrees of freedom provided by antenna position optimization and consistently outperforms a baseline with fixed-position antennas (FPAs).

title: A Deep Learning Approach for Dynamic Modeling of Stimulated Raman Scattering in Chalcogenide Microstructured Optical Fibers_supp1-3670396.pdf
authors: Jun Fu
publish: N/A
abstract: Stimulated Raman scattering (SRS) plays a pivotal role in applications such as optical communications, fiber optic sensing, and spectral analysis. However, traditional modeling methods like the split-step Fourier method (SSFM) are computationally demanding. In response to these challenges, we propose a novel deep learning framework based on a hybrid neural network, specifically architected to capture the complex spatio-temporal dependencies inherent in nonlinear pulse propagation. Our model offers rapid and precise predictions of SRS behavior, alleviating the need for computationally expensive simulations like SSFM. To validate the model’s performance, we conducted experiments using chalcogenide microstructured optical fibers (MOFs), which are attracting attention due to their high Raman gain coefficient and wide spectral range in the mid-infrared (MIR) region. Specifically, we demonstrate the first successful generation of MIR SRS in a 2-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m direct-pumped suspended-core As<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> MOF, which provides a crucial real-world dataset for model validation. The results demonstrate that our hybrid neural network is 116 times faster on a GPU and 44 times faster on a CPU compared to SSFM while maintaining accuracy and generalization. This significant acceleration paves the way for real-time analysis and inverse design of nonlinear photonic systems, tasks previously intractable with traditional methods.

title: Phase Locking a 4.7 THz Quantum Cascade Laser to a Stable Laboratory Synthesizer
authors: Sajjad Mahdizadeh, Matthias Justen, Urs U. Graf, Jürgen Stutzki
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: We demonstrate phase locking of a 4.7 THz distributed feedback quantum cascade laser (QCL) to a stable laboratory synthesizer. The QCL operates at 4747.9 GHz with 1 mW output power, mounted on the first stage of a pulse-tube cryocooler with mechanical dampers. Despite residual mechanical vibrations, the QCL radiation is successfully coupled out through a series of cold and warm mirrors and focused onto a room-temperature superlattice diode (SLD). The SLD is pumped by a 182.6 GHz signal from a diode frequency multiplier chain and generates the 26th harmonic at 4748.2 GHz for harmonic mixing as a phase reference. This produces a 327 MHz intermediate frequency (IF) that is amplified, filtered, and fed to a balanced diode mixer phase detector. The phase-locked loop employs a loop filter to generate correction signals for QCL current adjustment, reducing the unlocked linewidth from approximately 10 MHz to 1 Hz (limited by resolution bandwidth of spectrum analyzer). In addition of providing absolute frequency reference and ability of introducing fine frequency steps, the system demonstrates remarkable robustness, maintaining phase lock for several hours and even under low signal-to-noise conditions such as 1 dB of signal over noise at 3 MHz resolution bandwidth.This robustness comes from tailored PLL design, incorporating a diode ring mixer as phase detector and a linear phase narrow band filter, and proves its suitability for practical heterodyne array receivers targeting the [OI] line at 4.7 THz.

title: Reliable and Robust Chaos-based Mode Modulated OAM Multiplexing Transceiver Design for Underwater Wireless Communications
authors: Junyu Xia, Lin Zhang, Ao Qi, Zuwei Chen, Huaiyin Lu
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This letter presents a novel chaos based mode modulated (MM) orbital angular momentum (OAM) multiplexing transceiver for underwater wireless communication (UWC) systems with no requirement for perfect channel state information (CSI). The basic idea is to utilize the correlation and autocorrelation property of chaotic sequences to combat the mode crosstalk interference induced by the turbulence. At the transmitter, the chaos-based mode modulation is conducted in a differential way, which exploits the mode to deliver additional user data. Then at the receiver, a novel detection method which combines the differential chaotic demodulation with the maximum-likelihood (ML) detection is proposed. Thus no CSI is required, and the robustness can be enhanced. Subsequently, we derive the theoretical bit error rate (BER) expressions. Simulation results validate the theoretical analysis, and demonstrate that the proposed scheme can achieve better reliability and robustness performance than existing methods under turbulent UWC channels.

title: Cross-modal Context-aware Learning for Visual Prompt Guided Multimodal Image Understanding in Remote Sensing
authors: Xu Zhang, Jiabin Fang, Zhuoming Ding, Jin Yuan, Xuan Liu, Qianjun Zhang, Zhiyong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Recent advances in image understanding have enabled methods that leverage large language models for multimodal reasoning in remote sensing. However, existing approaches still struggle to steer models to the user-relevant regions when only simple, generic text prompts are available. Moreover, in large-scale aerial imagery many objects exhibit highly similar visual appearances and carry rich inter-object relationships, which further complicates accurate recognition. To address these challenges, we propose Cross-modal Context-aware Learning for Visual Prompt–Guided Multimodal Image Understanding (CLV-Net). CLV-Net lets users supply a simple visual cue—a bounding box—to indicate a region of interest, and uses that cue to guide the model to generate correlated segmentation masks and captions that faithfully reflect user intent. Central to our design is a Context-Aware Mask Decoder that models and integrates inter-object relationships to strengthen target representations and improve mask quality. In addition, we introduce a Semantic and Relationship Alignment module: a Cross-modal Semantic Consistency Loss enhances fine-grained discrimination among visually similar targets, while a Relationship Consistency Loss aligns textual relations with visual interactions, identifying meaningful commonalities to guide model output. Comprehensive experiments on two benchmark datasets show that CLV-Net outperforms existing methods and establishes new state-of-the-art results. The model effectively captures user intent and produces precise, intention-aligned multimodal outputs. The code will be publicly available at: https://github.com/XuZhang1211/CLV-Net.

title: Error Characterization and Correction of a Simple Circuit Model for Accurate and Rapid Resonant Frequency Estimation in Traction Power Supply Systems
authors: Kejian Song, Jianing Cheng, Mingli Wu, Georgios Konstantinou, Zhongbei Tian, Chi Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: Harmonic resonance phenomena that arise in traction power supply systems (TPSS) can lead to severe operational consequences. Accurate determination of the resonant frequency in TPSS is essential for developing and implementing effective countermeasures to mitigate resonance risks. Existing estimation methods, including simple model-based calculations, high-fidelity simulation methods, operational data-driven identification strategies, and active harmonic injection techniques, each suffer from inherent limitations and none offers a solution that combines high accuracy, simplified modeling, and practical applicability. This article proposes a rapid estimation method for the resonant frequency based on a simplified circuit model. Quantitative analysis reveals considerable discrepancies between estimated resonant frequency and its reference, caused by round-off error during analytical derivations. Sensitivity analysis is performed to identify the key parameters influencing estimation accuracy. By bounding these parameters within reasonable ranges, the minimum expansion order required to limit approximation errors is determined. Correction coefficients are defined and precomputed to refine the initial estimation. Validation through comparisons with field measurements from TPSS and high-fidelity simulation results demonstrates that the proposed method enables accurate resonant frequency estimation.

title: 2025 International Conference on Energy, Materials, and Photonics (EMP)
authors: N/A
publish: N/A

title: Falsification of Autonomous Systems in Rich Environments
authors: Khen Elimelech, Morteza Lahijanian, Lydia E. Kavraki, Moshe Y. Vardi
publish: ACM Transactions on Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: None

title: MLTiming: Pairwise-Constrained Learning for Improving Timing Resolution in TOF-PET
authors: José A. Avellaneda, Victor Sánchez-Tembleque, Miriam Caballero Rodríguez, Luis M. Fraile, José M. Udías, Joaquín L. Herraiz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: Accurate timing characterization of detected radiation events in Positron Emission Tomography (PET) provides Time-of-flight (TOF) information for detected coincidences, which improves the signal-to-noise ratio of the reconstructed images. In this work, we propose a method, called MLTiming, to train machine learning (ML) models to assign accurate timestamps to events measured in radiation detectors training using just the signals detected by the individual single-channel detectors, without the need of resorting to other detector signals nor simulations. We performed a proof-of-concept study using three different neural network (NN) architectures, including the recently proposed Kolmogorov-Arnold network (KAN), trained with event pairs constructed with the measured signal and a delayed version of it. The delay is used as a label in the training process. The proposed trained model was evaluated with coincidence data acquired with a <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">22</sup>Na point source using a pair of LaBr<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>(Ce) crystals in the shape of truncated cones coupled to fast photomultiplier tubes (PMTs). The detected γ-ray pulses were sampled at 5 Gsamples/s. A 3% improvement in Coincidence Time Resolution (CTR) over an ultra optimized genetic algorithm–enhanced digital CFD (D-CFD) method (246±3 ps to 238±1 ps) was achieved. The possibility of training ML models for each individual detector using only the information contained in their own detected events may facilitate the use of ML models for large and medium-sized scanners, as well as other research areas. MLTiming is available at https://github.com/wjosefw/MLTiming.git.

title: Unifying Unsupervised and Offline RL for Fast Adaptation Using World Models
authors: Daniel Khapun, Dan Rosenbaum
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning has proven an effective method to solve many intricate tasks, yet it still struggles with data efficiency and generalization to novel scenarios, as required in settings such as robotics. Recent approaches to deal with this include (1) unsupervised pretraining of the agent in an environment without reward signals, and (2) training the agent using offline data coming from various possible sources. In this paper we propose to consider both of these approaches together, resulting in a setting where different types of data streams are available and fast online adaptation to new tasks is required. Towards this goal we consider the Unsupervised Reinforcement Learning Benchmark and show that unsupervised training's primary value lies in its use as a source of exploration trajectories, beyond its role in pretraining a policy. Following this observation we develop a method based on a world-model as a generative model of offline exploration data and model predictive control (MPC) planning. We show that this approach outperforms previous methods and demonstrates task adaptation which is 10 times faster than previously shown. We then propose a setup that includes access to both unsupervised exploratory data and offline expert demonstrations when testing the agents' online performance on adaptation to novel tasks in the environment.

title: Inference-Time Enhancement of Generative Robot Policies via Predictive World Modeling
authors: Han Qi, Haocheng Yin, Aris Zhu, Yilun Du, Heng Yang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: We present generative predictive control (GPC), a framework for inference-time enhancement of pretrained behavior-cloning policies. Rather than retraining or fine-tuning, GPC augments a frozen diffusion policy at deployment by coupling it with a predictive world model. Concretely, we train an action-conditioned world model on expert demonstrations and random exploration rollouts to forecast the consequences of action proposals produced by the diffusion policy, then perform lightweight online planning that ranks and refines these proposals via model-based look-ahead. This combination of a generative prior with predictive foresight enables test-time adaptation. Across diverse robotic manipulation tasks—state- and vision-based, in simulation and on real hardware—GPC consistently outperforms standard behavior cloning and compares favorably to other inference-time adaptation baselines.

title: MCF-Det: A Cross-Domain Fusion Framework for Few-Shot Blade Defect Detection
authors: Xin Wen, Kai Li, Yu He, Haixu Yin, Kechen Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Industrial aero-engine blades are safety-critical, yet labeled blade-defect images are scarce, whereas related steel-surface defects are well annotated. This setting results in cross-domain few-shot defect detection: transferring knowledge from steel to blades under limited target supervision. Such transfer is hindered by material bias, diluted defect cues in multiscale features, and unstable few-shot decision boundaries. We propose MCF-Det, a YOLOv10-based framework that addresses these issues via three integrated modules: material knowledge transfer module aligns material-aware features to suppress transfer bias; cross-domain feature adaptation module enhances defect-sensitive responses through spatial–channel reweighting; few-shot defect calibration module calibrates logits using a contrastively learned prototype bank. The framework enables end-to-end detection without altering YOLOv10 deployment interfaces and with bounded inference overhead. On a proprietary blade dataset under a unified few-shot protocol, multi-scale cross-feature fusion detection (MCF-Det) improves mean average precision (mAP) by 5.4 points over the strongest of 16 retrained baselines, while additional cross-material transfer and stress tests offer supplementary robustness analysis under larger appearance shifts. K-shot analysis and nonparameteric tests confirm consistent and significant gains in low-shot regimes. We release code, configurations, and scripts to reproduce all results reported on public benchmarks under the reported splits and seeds.

title: SMInject: Specious Malignant Injection Attacks With Semantically-Enhanced Tokens in Cross-Modal Retrieval
authors: Ju Jia, Wenyi Zhang, Jiabao Guo, Xiaojun Jia, Siqi Ma, Jie Gui, Robert H. Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: The pre-training multimodal models have achieved remarkable success with powerful cross-modal understanding capabilities, while easily being affected by deliberate injection attacks. Although the deceptive injection attacks are harmful, they are valuable in revealing the vulnerability and improving the robustness for multimodal models. Unfortunately, the existing multimodal injection attacks pay less attention to the complicated roles of different modality-related causal correlation, which results in such attacks being susceptible to detection and defense. To alleviate this issue, we propose a novel specious malignant injection attack framework, called SMInject, which exploits both the irrationality and causal correlation across diverse modalities to stealthily manipulate the space of output. To enhance the stealthiness, we generate deceptive injections to assemble the concepts by analyzing causal correlation under four types of attacks. To further boost the effectiveness, the malignant injections are guided to penetrate in the encoded embedding space by designing the premise-hypothesis consensus alignment. Extensive experiments on representative multimodal models demonstrate that our SMInject achieves over 14% higher attack success rate and 6% higher Hit@5 metric than state-of-the-art methods while preserving the overall utility of models. Moreover, we highlight that the SMInject also exhibits the desired transferability by investigating the impact of contextual factors, such as similar attack profiles, imperceptible noise perturbations, etc. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SMInject-0DBC.

title: Subword Tokenization for Agglutinative Low-Resource Languages: A Case Study on Crimean Tatar
authors: Enghin Omer, Mariana Mocanu
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Low-resource languages face significant challenges in the development of robust natural language processing (NLP) tools due to the scarcity of standardized corpora and linguistic resources. Tokenization is a critical step in natural language processing, yet existing tokenization algorithms often struggle with agglutinative and low-resourced languages due to their rich morphological structure and limited training data. In this paper, we present the creation of a corpus for Crimean Tatar, a severely under-resourced Turkic language. To address orthographic inconsistencies and dialectal spelling variations, we utilize bilingual dictionaries to normalize and consolidate different variants of the same word. Leveraging the linguistic similarity between Crimean Tatar and Turkish, we utilize a large Turkish corpus alongside a smaller Crimean Tatar corpus to adapt and improve subword tokenization algorithms such as Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), WordPiece, and Unigram. We present a fine-tuning algorithm that incorporates a golden set of annotated tokenizations and a curated Crimean Tatar vocabulary to adjust token boundaries. To further address context-sensitive morpheme boundaries, where some morphemes should be merged or separated depending on usage, we model tokenization as a character-level sequence labeling task. A BiLSTM model trained on the golden dataset is introduced, assigning “$\mathbf{B}$” (begin) and “$\mathbf{I}$” (inside) labels to characters to predict token splits. This approach demonstrates improved handling of context-dependent segmentation and outperforms traditional tokenization methods. We provide detailed analyses of the corpus composition, normalization strategies and tokenizers creation and evaluation. Our results demonstrate that a larger corpus of a very similar agglutinative language, in this case Turkish, can improve the performance of the tokenizer, but more important is the usage of a vocabulary. However, a model which takes into account the context of characters can better tokenize words composed of morphemes than tokenizers based on learning merging rules.

title: Machine Learning Approach to Predict Tunnel FET Characteristics Using Random Forest Regression
authors: Ajaykumar Dharmireddy, T. Sony Kanaka Deepti, V. Monika, Y. Jaahnavi, P. Bhavani Bharagavi, Y. Avinash
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: TFETs (Tunnel Field-Effects Transistors) are assumed to be ultra-low-power devices because they have steep subthreshold swing and a more favourable scaling characteristic. Their electrical characteristics, however, cannot be adequately simulated using extensive Technology Computer-Aided Design (TCAD) simulations, which are computationally intensive and time-consuming to analyze. In an attempt to overcome this bottleneck, a proposal for a machine learning (ML)-based surrogate modeling framework is provided where the prediction of the critical TFET properties of transfer curves, Ion, Ioff, threshold voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$V_{\text{th}}$</tex>), and subthreshold swing (SS) is endeavored with the help of the Random Forest Regression (RFR) model. The data collected based on TCAD simulations and literature reports was processed, trained, and tested on various ML models such as the Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Support Vector Regression (SVR) and RFR. The calculated R2 of the proposed RFR model was better than ANN and SVR, with a much smaller MSE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 2 1} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{-\mathbf{7}}$</tex>) and an RMSE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 7 9} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{-\mathbf{4}}$</tex>), showing that the model is robust and exhibits better generalization within the conditions of the devices used. Also, the ranking of intrinsic feature importance offered by RFR has a better interpretation rate and complies well with the previous findings on TCAD. These findings place RFR as a sound and efficient substitute for TFET modelling, with lower computational expense and intact physical stability, and thus add another facet and a practical approach to future device design optimization, assisted by a novel and powerful ML

title: Research on Collaborative Optimal Operation Strategy of Hydropower-DC Transmission at the Sending End and Wind-Solar Power at the Receiving End Based on Resource Endowment
authors: Xipeng Zhang, Zhong Liu, Yuanhang Cheng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Driven by the "dual carbon" goals, the coordinated operation between western hydropower and eastern renewable energy is crucial within China's "West-East Electricity Transmission" framework. This study addresses spatiotemporal and extreme weather challenges by establishing a comprehensive framework integrating quantitative evaluation, refined modeling, and efficient solution methods. A multi-dimensional indicator system is developed, covering security, renewable utilization, mutual support, and resilience. An integrated sending-receiving end model is built on the TEAP3 platform, incorporating 8,760-hour annual time-series data, unit commitment, and grid constraints, with computational bottlenecks overcome via invalid constraint identification and parallel solving. Simulations of extreme scenarios — ensuring supply security and promoting renewable integration — reveal systemic vulnerabilities and complementary potential, leading to scenario-adaptive strategies for cascade hydropower and receiving-end grid operation. Key contributions include a novel multi-dimensional indicator system, an integrated high-fidelity simulation model with accelerated computation, and validated adaptive strategies that enhance system security and clean energy utilization. Results show the strategies reduce power shortages by approximately 340 MW in supply-guarantee scenarios and lower renewable curtailment to below 1.0% in integration-promotion scenarios, with only an approximately 0.3% operational cost increase, providing methodological and practical guidance for optimizing complex hybrid AC/DC grids.

title: Research on coordinated control strategy of three-phase current compensation of traction power supply system based on three ports
authors: Pei Luo, Zhipeng Ou, Qian Ma, Xingyu Zhu, Ting Lei, Teng Xie
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: When the railroad power modulator (RPC) is used to solve power quality problems in electrified railroads, the RPC power supply is used in the bidirectional transmission process due to switching losses and line losses, and such losses affect the accuracy of the RPC power supply compensation, cause three-phase current imbalance on the grid side, and affect the effectiveness of negative sequence current management on the grid side. To address the above problems, this paper proposes a coordinated control strategy based on an isolated three-port full-bridge DC-DC system (TAB) integrating RPC, which combines photovoltaics and energy storage with the DC side of RPC through the TAB topology to realize the compensation of losses in the RPC power supply during the bidirectional transmission process and to solve the three-phase current imbalance problem on the grid side. In order to verify the effectiveness of this management topology, a simulation model is built in MATLAB/ Simulink. The simulation results show that the proposed control strategy can better compensate the power and improve the three-phase current imbalance on the grid side.

title: Enhancing Security Centric Node Identification in Complex Networks Using Genetic Algorithm
authors: Dinesh Rengaswamy
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: The increase in the number of computing devices and the availability of efficient wired and wireless connectivity options have evolved computer networks in terms of their dynamic nature considerably. The emerging IoT, Vehicular, Mobile Ad- Hoc and other cloud-based distributed and cloud-based tech- nologies have a large number of connected mobile devices.
