title: Coordinated Topology Optimization for High-voltage Insulator Structural Design
authors: Xinru Shi, Chao Wang, He Gao, Jianyi Xue, Sile Chen, Zhaoquan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The structural design of insulators plays a critical role in determining their electrical and mechanical properties. This paper presents a topology optimization approach based on a multi-physics finite element simulation model, aimed at addressing the optimization challenges of complex insulating components. A variable-density topology optimization framework is developed, integrating the dual requirements of electrical strength and mechanical strength. The mathematical formulation of the optimization problem facilitates the coordinated design of the spacer’s geometric shape and relative permittivity distribution. Case studies involving spacers and tri-post insulators are presented to examine the sensitivity of the optimization outcomes to different algorithmic parameters. The results demonstrate that the proposed method can generate insulator topologies with improved electric field distribution and excellent mechanical performance, all without relying on prior geometric information of the insulators. This approach offers a novel research perspective for advancing the design and optimization of insulators and holds promising potential for future engineering applications.

title: Recent Advancements in Technologies, Applications, and Regulations of Near-Field Wireless Power Transfer
authors: Arthur Cloet, Hamed Farbakhsh, Michael Kleemann, Ben Minnaert
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Near-field wireless power transfer enables energy transfer via electromagnetic coupling between a power source and a receiver over short distances. This technology improves user convenience and autonomy in both low-power and high-power applications, such as wearable electronics, electric vehicles, and biomedical systems. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the underlying principles, practical applications, regulatory frameworks, and technological challenges associated with near-field wireless energy transfer. The review focuses on capacitive and inductive wireless power transfer. This is supported by a summary of research articles, evaluated using metrics such as power, transfer distance, and frequency. Applications are discussed within the market sectors, medical devices, electric mobility, and unmanned aerial systems. Recent developments in international standards WPC’s Qi2 and SAE’s J2954, as well as health-related standards IEEE C95 and ICNIRP-guidelines are discussed. Finally, the review identifies the critical limitations and trade-offs of capacitive and inductive wireless power transfer technologies.

title: Optimized Mode-Transition Modulation for an Isolated Single-Stage AC–DC Converter
authors: Li Jiang, Yingjie Wang, Tianshi Yu, Hui Wang, Yao Sun, Mei Su
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents an optimized mode-transition modulation strategy for a push–pull dual-active-bridge single-stage AC–DC converter (PPDAB-SSC) targeting wide voltage conversion ratio applications. Four steady-state operating modes are first established to extend the achievable voltage transfer range, and a power-dependent mode transition scheme is formulated to enable real-time switching. Under soft-switching constraints, a global optimization algorithm is developed to minimize the peak current, reducing current stress across four operating modes. Furthermore, a zero-current alignment strategy is introduced to achieve inter-cycle current decoupling, effectively eliminating DC bias and mitigating transient current stress during mode transitions. More importantly, several operating modes inherently realize current decoupling by exploiting the natural zero-crossing points synchronized with the switching actions, which eliminates explicit zero-crossing calculations and simplifies the digital control implementation. Finally, a 1-kW experimental prototype validates the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed modulation strategy.

title: Diffusion Model-Based Data Augmentation with Mel Spectrogram for Automatic Modulation Recognition in Underwater Acoustic Communication
authors: Wenjie Huang, Rong Fan, Yishan Su
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Automatic modulation recognition (AMR) in underwater acoustic (UWA) communications is limited by scarce training data and channel impairments such as multipath propagation, Doppler shifts, and ambient noise. To address these challenges, we propose Mel-Diffusion, a diffusion model–based data augmentation framework. The method first converts time-domain signals into Mel spectrograms for preprocessing. The proposed Mel-Diffusion model, which is a DDPM-based diffusion framework with a Transformer backbone, is then applied for feature enhancement. Finally, the Griffin–Lim algorithm is used to reconstruct the time-domain signals. Building upon this processing pipeline, Mel-Diffusion effectively captures modulation features and latent channel characteristics. In addition, a Depth-Wise–PointWise Feed Forward Network (DPFFN) integrated into the diffusion model reduces model complexity, while Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) sampling improves computational efficiency. Experiments on datasets from the reservoir and the sea demonstrate that Mel-Diffusion generates realistic and diverse signals, improving AMR accuracy by up to 5.51% and 5.27% under data-scarce conditions. After data augmentation, the highest AMR accuracies reach 98.66% and 95.81%, respectively, on the reservoir and sea datasets. These results highlight the effectiveness of Mel-Diffusion as a practical solution for accurate and robust AMR in complex UWA environments.

title: Recognizing Activities of Daily Living for Activity-Centric Energy Efficiency in Residential Buildings: A Human Sensing and Deep Learning Approach_supp1-3673078.docx

title: Bi-Objective Partial Charging and Directional Scheduling in WRSNs: A Structural Decomposition Framework
authors: Chun-Hsien Lin, Yen-Heng Yu, Yingrong Coral Sung, Meng-Hsun Tsai
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The operational sustainability of Wireless Rechargeable Sensor Networks (WRSNs) is fundamentally constrained by the inherent conflict between maximizing the network survival ratio and minimizing the energy expenditure of mobile chargers. While directional charging improves power transfer efficiency, it introduces high-dimensional coupling between beam orientation, dwell time, and visitation sequences, which traditional single-objective heuristics and static scalarization methods fail to resolve effectively. This paper proposes a unified Strategic Regime-Switching framework based on a structural decomposition perspective. We first employ a density-aware clustering scheme and an Angle Set Cover Greedy (ASCG) algorithm to discretize the continuous directional charging space into strategic stop-orientation pairs. To navigate the resulting solution space without subjective weight tuning, the optimization problem is decomposed into distinct operational regimes triggered by the real-time Network Available Ratio (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</sub>). Two complementary routing algorithms are developed: the Shortest Path First Cluster (SPFC), which achieves extreme energy economy during stable states, and the Adaptive Differential Evolution Cluster (ADEC), which performs urgency-aware global search to restore sensing integrity. Simulation results demonstrate that ADEC achieves a near-optimal 95.5% survival ratio, representing an 18% improvement over existing state-of-the-art directional charging baselines, while SPFC reduces long-term energy consumption by 86.6% compared to fundamental heuristics. Across heterogeneous topologies, the proposed framework consistently establishes a superior Pareto balance between network longevity and operational efficiency.

title: A Novel DNN-Based Semi-Parametric Calibration Method for Parallel Robots Considering Non-Kinematic Parameters
authors: Shuai Fan, Ye Liang, Yudong Wang, Lisi Geng, Cheng Zeng, Shaohui Feng, Rui Huang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: The pointing accuracy of pose adjusting parallel robots (PAPRs) is critical for the imaging quality of Cherenkov telescopes, making kinematic calibration crucial for improvement. However, non-geometric error sources like elastic deformation and joint clearances create an inevitable difference between theoretical and actual kinematic models, restricting accuracy. While various calibration methods exist, models considering non-kinematic nonlinear parameters are often highly complex and involve stringent measurement requirements. To achieve efficient, stable, and high-precision calibration, this paper proposes a novel deep neural network (DNN)-based semi-parametric model calibration method, combined with a Light-Spot-Mapping Kinematic Model (LKM). This approach enables high-precision matching between the theoretical and actual robot models through the semi-parametric framework, enhancing calibration performance. It also utilizes a high-precision camera as the measurement device, effectively reducing calibration time and labor costs. An experiment on a PS+PSS+PSR-type PAPR demonstrated that the pointing accuracy was improved by 47.7% after geometric parameter calibration, and by 81.2% (relative to the uncalibrated state) after non-geometric parameter calibration, achieving a final absolute accuracy of 156 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mu rad}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The proposed method is effective for improving the accuracy of PAPRs while accounting for the influence of nonlinear parameters.

title: Surface Stress Characterization and Trends in Thermally Strengthened Photovoltaic Module Glass_supp1-3668779.pdf
authors: Elizabeth Palmiotti

title: Day-ahead Electricity Market Strategy for PV- Storage-Charging Stations Based on XGboost
authors: Yucheng Hou, Hao Wang, YingYing Hu, Zhe Ji, Juan Xing, Peng Zou, Weisheng Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Photovoltaic-storage-charging electric vehicle charging stations (EVCSs), as composite entities integrating photovoltaic (PV) generation, energy storage (ES), and electric vehicle (EV) charging resources in the day-ahead electricity market, confront the core challenge of devising optimal operational and bidding strategies to maximize profits by incorporating day-ahead market price forecasts. The day-ahead electricity market constitutes a dynamic game landscape involving multiple participants, where EVCSs' strategic decisions are inherently shaped by power generators, other EVCSs, user behaviors, and price forecasts—rendering game theory coupled with price forecasting a superior analytical paradigm. Within noncooperative game models, each PV-Storage-Charging EVCS acts as a rational agent, optimizing time-interval-based electricity bidding strategies and ES charge-discharge scheduling schemes by incorporating day-ahead market price forecasts so as to mitigate the dual uncertainties associated with market clearing prices and charging load fluctuations. From a game-theoretic standpoint, this study embeds day-ahead market price forecasts to unravel the interaction dynamics and interest equilibrium mechanisms among entities under price signals. Validation conducted in a representative regional electricity market demonstrates that the XGBoost-driven strategy model coupled with price forecasting outperforms neural networks and phase space heterogeneous methods in terms of training efficiency and decision-making precision. It empowers PV-Storage-Charging EVCSs to devise scientifically sound and robust operational strategies based on price forecasts, thereby augmenting their market competitiveness and overall operational efficacy.

title: Pre-Trained Actor-Guided Proximal Policy Optimization for Feasible and Safe AC Optimal Power Flow in Large-Scale Power Systems
authors: Liangcai Zhou, Pan Wu, Rui Chen, Xin Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Real-time AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) optimization poses a critical challenge in modern power systems, due to the computational complexity of traditional solvers and the infeasibility issues associated with the reinforcement learning (RL) methods. We propose Pre-trained Actor-Guided Proximal Policy Optimization (PAG-PPO). This framework initializes an RL agent with the actor neural network cloning from historical OPF solutions to enhance learning efficiency. This approach enables rapid convergence to physically feasible and numerically stable control policies, ensuring consistent power flow convergence while minimizing feasible action exploration in the training. Evaluated on the IEEE 300-bus system under diverse load conditions and line outages, PAG-PPO achieves power flow convergence in 500 episodes. Compared to the MATPOWER solver, PAG-PPO reduces inference time by 114×, with a 0.79% increase in generation cost and a 99.6% feasibility rate. Infeasible cases involve minor voltage violations, with no power flow divergence. These results demonstrate that PAG-PPO offers a practical, scalable, and near-optimal solution for real-time AC-OPF optimization, advancing RL-based decision-making for large-scale power systems.

title: Simulated Annealing Algorithm-based Fuzzy Control Strategy for PEMFC Cold-Start
authors: Weirong Li, Yinke Dou, Pengyue Li, Bin Wang, Liwei Kou, Chunyan Ma
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Fuel cells have established themselves as a promising clean energy technology owing to their high energy density, environmental friendliness, and zero pollution. However, cold-start performance remains a critical bottleneck for their commercialization. While simple and easy to implement, traditional control strategies struggle to address uncertainties when confronted with the strongly coupled and complex relationships between battery states and control variables during cold starts, rendering parameter optimization methods based on accurate models ineffective in such scenarios. Fuzzy control, which does not require precise mathematical models, can handle fuzzy information and nonlinear relationships inherent in cold-start processes. Nevertheless, its performance heavily depends on rule design and membership function parameters, and initial parameter settings often fail to achieve multi-objective global optimization. As a global optimization algorithm, the simulated annealing algorithm can break through local optima and efficiently explore better parameter combinations. For proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs), cold starts in extremely low-temperature environments are particularly challenging; external heaters can actively supply heat to increase the temperature, thereby facilitating the start-up process. To address these issues, this paper proposes a PEMFC cold-start fuzzy control strategy based on the simulated annealing algorithm, which integrates passive and active start-up methods.

title: Reactive Power Optimization in Critical Grid Areas Under Geomagnetic Storms
authors: Zhengxiang Xu, Yinke Dou, Yuchen Wang, Boyang Huang, Bin Wang, Haoqin Wang, Yizhuo Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Geomagnetically Induced Currents (GIC) triggered by geomagnetic storms and their secondary GIC-Q phenomena can increase reactive losses in transformers, disrupt the reactive power balance of power grids, and compromise the stable operation of power systems. To effectively mitigate the adverse effects of GIC on power grids, a comprehensive vulnerability assessment index considering grid topology and operational characteristics is established. A multi-attribute decision-making method is employed to comprehensively rank structural and operational indicators of nodes and lines. Through unified weight fusion, priority identification results for critical nodes and lines in the power system are obtained. Treating critical areas as reactive power compensation points, an improved Pareto genetic multi-objective reactive power optimization strategy is proposed. This strategy aims to minimize both node voltage fluctuations and reactive power compensation requirements, ensuring grid reactive power balance under geomagnetic disturbance. Simulation results from the GIC-Benchmark case validate these findings. Results demonstrate that: Comprehensive indicators considering both grid topology and operational characteristics, combined with multi-attribute decision-making, achieve more accurate vulnerability zone identification; The improved multi-objective reactive power optimization strategy exhibits stronger convergence and higher efficiency.

title: Electricity Trading Clearing in Micro-Markets via an Enhanced Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Tao Wei, Jian Geng, Lingling Pan, Shaoyuan Yu, Ailin Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the rapid integration of distributed renewable energy into distribution networks, micro-market electricity trading is characterized by multi-participant involvement, temporal flexibility, and complex combinations. These features make the clearing problem challenging in high-dimensional discrete spaces, often leading to computational difficulties and efficiency bottlenecks. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Cognitive Learning and Cooperative Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm (CLC-PSO). In the alternating grouping evolution process, the algorithm introduces a cognitive learning mechanism to construct and refine local structural cognition, enabling the identification, transmission, and utilization of high-quality solution fragments. This helps to alleviate search stagnation caused by sparse fitness signals. Furthermore, by integrating cooperative evolution with a probabilistic update strategy, CLC-PSO effectively enhances global search efficiency and solution quality. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves convergence speed and solution stability in large-scale, complex trading scenarios, thereby verifying its applicability and advantages for micro-market clearing optimization.

title: Risk-Aware Planning and Operation of Compute-Integrated EV Charging Stations Under Energy and Workload Uncertainty
authors: Qianyu Dong, Yujia Huang, Rufei Ren, Qiuye Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The increasing integration of electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure with edge computing resources is reshaping the design and operation of future urban energy systems. This paper proposes a unified planning and operational framework for compute-integrated charging stations (CICSs), where EVs not only receive energy but also support low-latency AI workloads via on-board computing. A two-stage stochastic mixed-integer linear program (MILP) is formulated to jointly optimize station siting, server capacity sizing, and EV-side computing enablement under uncertainty in EV demand, workload arrivals, renewable availability, and electricity prices. The model incorporates co-optimization of energy and computing flows, day-ahead and real-time market participation, and user-centric service quality indicators. Spatial accessibility is modeled through a p-median structure, while temporal convenience is captured via charging wait-time proxies and backlog-aware computing constraints. To ensure robustness against unfavorable scenarios, a CVaR-based risk term is introduced. Case studies conducted on a 12-zone urban network demonstrate that the proposed CICS framework yields a high increase in profitability compared to conventional energy-only charging stations, while simultaneously enhancing service quality. The results highlight significant synergies between renewable energy generation and computational workloads, with strategic backlog management facilitating efficient resource utilization during periods of peak EV availability.

title: Fast and Accurate Human Fall Detection with Hybrid Deep Learning and OpenCV-Based Human Detection
authors: Rosario Gilmary, N Manvizhi, V Thendral Balaji, P Nirmal
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Falls among the elderly population constitute a significant public health concern, frequently resulting in severe injury or mortality, particularly when emergency response is delayed. While machine learning models like Random Forest have been applied to fall detection, their efficacy is often constrained by limited feature representation capabilities and insufficient real-time performance. To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces a hybrid deep learning framework for robust fall detection in video streams. The proposed system employs a two-stage architecture: first, human subjects are localized using efficient OpenCV-based detectors, specifically comparing Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) and a MobileNet Single Shot Detector (SSD). Subsequently, the cropped human regions are fed into a Convolutional Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-LSTM) model. This spatiotemporal classifier leverages convolutional layers to extract salient spatial features and recurrent LSTM layers to model temporal dependencies and motion dynamics across frames. This integrated approach enables highly accurate discrimination between activities of daily living and genuine fall events, thereby minimizing false alarms. Upon detecting a fall, the system automatically triggers a multi-channel alert mechanism via SMS, email, and an audible alarm to facilitate immediate intervention. Designed for computational efficiency, the proposed framework is suitable for deployment on low-cost hardware, presenting a practical and reliable solution for real-world fall monitoring applications.

title: Invisible Image Watermarking through the use of Concentric High-Frequency Bands in the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) Domain
authors: Victor A. Toporan, Mihai V. Micea
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: In order to address the necessity of invisible image watermarking, this paper proposes a robust and imperceptible method for embedding data into an image. It makes use of a variable size DFT kernel in order to subdivide the original image, and in each subdivision several concentric high-frequency bands are chosen in order to encode a bit of the watermark image.

title: Inertia feedforward-based sub-synchronous oscillation suppression strategy for grid-forming energy storage converters
authors: Qichao Chen, Xu Cao, Yiqi Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Nowadays, power electronic converters are widely used in a wide range of application scenarios, and the combined operation mode of "new energy and energy storage" has been widely used, and energy storage can solve the problem of new energy consumption and also contribute to the improvement of the stability of the new energy system, among which, the grid-forming energy storage systems (GFM-ESS) adopts the grid-type virtual synchronous machine control method, and the rotor equations of motion can make the power electronic device have the inertia and damping characteristics. GFM-ESS mainly adopts the grid-type virtual synchronous machine control method by simulating the equations of motion of the rotor, which makes the power electronic device have the inertia and damping characteristics of synchronous generator, and thus it is widely used, but when it is integrated into the power grid with different strengths, the phenomenon of sub-synchronous oscillation occurs easily due to the interaction effect. In order to address this problem, this paper firstly models the sequential impedance of GFM-ESS and power grids of different strengths, analyzes the mechanism of the subsynchronous oscillation phenomenon by using the bode plot, and proposes a subsynchronous oscillation suppression method based on inertia feed-forward to achieve the effect of quickly suppressing the oscillation by compensating for the phase in the bode plot. Meanwhile, the parameters in the proposed method are rectified, and finally the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by MATLAB/simulink.

title: Mining Analytically Defined Families with Periodic Patterns for Chordal Ring Networks Using LLMs
authors: Oleg Monakhov, Emilia Monakhova
publish: 2025 5th International Siberian Scientific Workshop on Data Analysis Technologies with Applications (SibDATA)
abstract: Chordal ring networks are a well-known class of graphs used as a model for communication networks in parallel computing systems and distributed networks. This class of graphs has a ring connection of vertices and additional edges (defined by generators) connecting them, forming a 3-regular graph. In this paper, an optimal graph is defined as a graph with a minimum diameter for a given number of vertices. The authors previously developed a large database (dataset) of optimal chordal ring networks. This paper is devoted to finding families of optimal chordal ring networks, that is, optimal networks that have common analytical descriptions of the number of vertices and generator, regularly repeating with a certain period of graph diameter variation. To solve this problem, two large language models (LLMs) were used as intelligent assistants. The algorithms and programs developed by these models were applied to the analysis of the dataset and allowed the discovery of over a hundreds families of optimal chordal ring networks.

title: Energy Foresight: Predicting Daily Demand with Combined Techniques
authors: Elena Hadarau, Raluca Portase, Rodica Potolea
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Accurate forecasting of electricity demand plays a crucial role in modern energy systems, enabling efficient planning, maintaining grid stability, and optimising resource allocation amid increasing consumption and variable demand patterns. This paper presents HeySF (Hybrid EnergY Series Forecasting), a modular method for forecasting electricity consumption that integrates statistical models (SARIMA) and machine learning techniques (XGBoost, Random Forest, MLP, LSTM and PSOoptimised SVR). The approach is validated on real-world consumption data from the PJM Interconnection for daily energy consumption forecasting. The paper evaluates both individual predictors and ensemble schemes using standard error metrics, and includes a cost-aware analysis of training and inference latency measured as wall-clock time.

title: Influence of Feature Site Selection on the Recognition of Stray Current Types
authors: Zeyang Tang, Ling Ruan, Xin Mei, Wanting Deng, Yibo Cui, Xin Shu, Wei Rao, Yiwen Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Accurately recognition different types of stray current and proposing targeted mitigation measures for each is essential to ensure effective stray current control. To investigate how different combinations of characteristic monitoring sites influence the recognition of stray current types, this paper develops a stray current type identification model based on XGBoost. The model analyzes 255 different combinations of eight characteristic sites in the Yichang. Experimental results show that when the number of combined characteristic sites is three, the feature extraction and prediction time is the shortest at 5.3 seconds, and the accuracy reaches the highest level of 90%, meeting the precision and speed requirements for stray current type recognition.

title: Accurate Classification of Dry Bean Varieties Using Machine Learning Techniques for Enhanced Agricultural Analysis
authors: Rajakumar M P, M. Balasubramani, R. Suganthi, K. Abinaya, Ishwarya Kothandaraman, M.Robinson Joel
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Comprehensive analysis of dry sap bracket using machine literacy ways. The bracket of dry sap is pivotal for colorful agrarian and marketable purposes, including quality assessment, sorting, and request segmentation. In this study, we explore the operation of machine literacy algorithms to directly classify different types of dry sap grounded on their physical characteristics similar as size, shape, color, and texture. We use a dataset containing attributes of multiple dry bean kinds and employ colorful bracket algorithms, including decision trees, support vector machines, k- nearest neighbors, and neural networks, to estimate their performance in directly grading the sap. also, point selection and dimensionality reduction ways are applied to enhance model effectiveness and interpretability. The results of our analysis give perceptivity into the effectiveness of different bracket approaches for dry sap and offer precious guidance for unborn exploration and practical operations in husbandry and food assiduity. Also, look into how preprocessing styles like data addition, point scaling, and normalisation affect bracket delicacy. The report also addresses the difficulties that arise during the bracket process, similar as class imbalance, noisy data, and model overfitting, and suggests results. The results of this study have counter accusations for adding delicacy and effectiveness in a variety of agrarian and food processing operations, as well as for the development of automated bean bracket systems.

title: Calculation Method for Short-Circuit Current of New Energy Stations with Different Control Modes
authors: Yi Lu, Qian Chen, Xiaojun Ni, Chao Ding, Po Li, Guoteng Wang, Ying Huang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The conventional single-machine equivalent method for short-circuit current calculation in new energy power stations suffers from limited accuracy, as it fails to account for the differentiated control strategies of non-synchronous machine sources and the actual station topology. This paper categorizes non-synchronous machine sources into PV-type and PQ-type, and establishes scenario-specific calculation models based on terminal voltage conditions. A "PV – PQ – LVRT triple-machine parallel equivalent model" is proposed for applications in 220 kV and higher power grids, accompanied by a clear methodology for parameter acquisition. The total short-circuit current is derived via phasor superposition. Simulation results from PSCAD validate that the proposed approach achieves high computational accuracy with minor errors, fulfilling the requirements for engineering applications.

title: User Outage Probability Calculation Model Considering User Load Allocation Strategy Under Extreme Weather
authors: Haoran Liu, Zifa Liu, Jindou Yuan, Chang Liu, Heng Yang, Ting Hu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Under extreme weather conditions, large-scale user outage accidents may occur, leading to insufficient user security load and severe losses. To address the issue of user power supply guarantee under extreme weather, this paper proposes a user outage probability calculation model that considers user load allocation strategies. Firstly, the impact of extreme weather on distribution network components is quantified, and fault probability models for photovoltaic (PV) modules and lines based on typhoon wind speed are established. Secondly, based on component fault probabilities and load demand fluctuations, Monte Carlo sampling is used to generate grid-load scenarios under extreme weather. Thirdly, considering user importance and security load curtailment strategies, a load allocation model under extreme weather is constructed with the goal of minimizing load loss weighted by user importance. The user load loss under grid-load scenarios is calculated, and user outage scenarios are counted to obtain the user outage probability. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through case analysis.

title: Numerical Analysis of the Effect of Air Intake Exit Distortion on the Operational Performance of a Solid Fuel Ramjet
authors: Yonghui He, Wenxiang Cai, Qi Shao, Weimin Huang, Tianle Liang, Kaiqiang Fang
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: This study employs numerical simulations to investigate the effect of inlet flow distortion on the operational performance of a boron-containing solid fuel ramjet (SFRJ). Flow field analyses were conducted under several conditions: without a distortion plate, and with distortion plates having blockage ratios (S) of 0.156, 0.187, 0.203, and 0.233. The results revealed that the distortion plate disrupted the originally uniform and symmetric flow field within the engine. As the blockage ratio increases, the flow field becomes increasingly disordered, with vortices forming upstream and downstream of the plate, compressing the flow passage. Furthermore, the total pressure recovery coefficient of the internal flow field exhibits a monotonic decreasing trend with increasing blockage ratio, declining from 10.11 (without distortion plate) to 7.09 (at S = 0.233). Concurrently, both the temperature rise ratio and combustion efficiency also decrease.

title: Capacity Compensation Mechanism for Coal-fired Power Plants in China's National Unified Electricity Market
authors: Anbang Xie, Yihan Zhang, Naixun Li, Jingyi Shang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Against the backdrop of China's carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals and the construction of a new power system, the functional positioning of coal-fired power is transitioning from primary energy supplier to capacity support and ancillary service provider, necessitating the establishment of a scientific capacity compensation mechanism. This paper develops a capacity compensation pricing model based on the annual cost method, incorporating annualized fixed cost recovery requirements and energy market revenues as core elements. Using Henan Province as a case study, we conduct empirical research to quantify the impact of three key variables—feed-in tariff, coal price, and utilization hours—on capacity compensation through sensitivity analysis. Considering the electricity market reform process, we forecast the dynamic evolution of capacity compensation requirements during the 15th Five-Year Plan period (2026-2030). Results show that capacity compensation price exhibits strong negative correlation with feed-in tariff (sensitivity coefficient -7.5), positive correlation with coal price (6.6), and weak negative correlation with utilization hours (-0.9). The required compensation is projected to increase from 217-224 CNY/kW·year in 2026-2027 to 374-562 CNY/kW·year in 2028-2030. This research provides quantitative decision support for optimizing coal-fired power capacity pricing mechanisms.

title: VISION-RISK: Vision-Language Model for Risk Assessment in Autonomous Driving
authors: Andrei-Bogdan Constantin, Vlad Negru, Camelia Lemnaru, Rodica Potolea
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Autonomous driving involves the challenge of assessing and managing risks in complex environments. One approach for addressing this is the use of human-readable explanations of risk-related tasks to support justification of driving behavior. In this paper, we introduce VISION-RISK, a vision-language model (VLM) designed for risk assessment in autonomous driving using a lightweight architecture, optimized for deployment on edge devices. To train the model, we developed a custom dataset combining real-world driving scenarios from Honda Driving Dataset and extreme high-risk cases from Car Crash Dataset, augmented with synthetic annotations using Dolphins and refined via DeepSeek-V3. VISION-RISK stands out through three key characteristics: the integration of danger level classification with natural language explanation generation, a lightweight architecture optimized for deployment on resource-constrained devices, and a focus on safety through risk assessment to support trust in autonomous driving.

title: HarmLLaMA: Harmful Language Detection with Large Language Models
authors: Ciprian-Octavian Truică, Elena-Simona Apostol, Alexandru-Gabriel Ilie, Adrian Paschke
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Online platforms are complex systems that influence the commercial, social, and political environment, debating important real-life topics, e.g., health, emigration, elections, climate change, etc. These online environments offer users freedom of expression through anonymous posting. In addition to their obvious advantages, some users abuse this freedom to spread harmful content, e.g., misinformation, propaganda, harmful conspiracy theories, or abusive, aggressive, and offensive speech. Automated detection techniques can effectively reduce the negative influence of antisocial behavior used by these malicious actors. In this article, we propose HarmLLAMA, a fine-tuned LLAMA2 model using LORA. The experimental results on two real-world datasets show that our model, HarmLLaMA, outperforms current state-of-the-art models in terms of Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

title: Comparative Analysis of Large-scale Renewable Energy DC Collection Topology Architecture
authors: Yongxian Pu, Junpeng Ma, Shunliang Wang, Tingting Cheng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The selection of collection methods for large-scale renewable energy bases such as the desert, Gobi, and barren areas has become a critical aspect in building the new power system. Traditional AC collection methods suffer from poor stability, high reactive power loss, and limited transmission distances. In contrast, DC collection technology offers advantages including simplified control, high efficiency, and no synchronous stability issues, making it highly competitive for large-scale renewable energy collection and transmission across wide areas. This paper conducts a systematic comparative analysis of large-scale renewable DC collection systems across three dimensions: topology and electrical main wiring configuration, control strategies, and economic viability. First, it contrasts the technical characteristics of series and parallel DC collection topologies based on factors such as the number of step-up conversion stages, reliability, system efficiency, and stability. Second, it thoroughly examines the control strategies for various collection systems, evaluating their stability and complexity. Finally, the economic viability of different DC collection topologies is comprehensively evaluated from the perspective of initial investment costs. By synthesizing existing research, this study provides technical guidance for selecting appropriate DC collection methods across diverse application scenarios.

title: A Multi-Source Information Fusion-Based Substation Sensor Network and Early Warning System
authors: Guanjie Wei, Dong Wang, Jinzhong Li, Yuan Ge
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the continuous advancement of power system technology, substations—as critical nodes in the power grid—require increasingly robust real-time monitoring and early warning capabilities for their internal operational status. This paper proposes a substation sensor network and early warning system based on multi-source information fusion. By deploying ubiquitous sensor clusters to collect real-time data and establishing dedicated communication networks according to data types, the system ultimately leverages a backend expert early warning system. This approach enables comprehensive intelligent monitoring and control of primary and auxiliary equipment in smart substations, effectively resolving conventional substation operation and maintenance challenges.

title: A Scenario Generation Method for Renewable Energy Sources Power Output Considering Temporal and Spatial Correlations
authors: Chao Shen, Jian Wang, Rui Li, Kang Yue, Dong Pan, Haoming Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The growing integration of renewable energy sources (RES) challenges power system planning due to their inherent intermittency and uncertainty. While scenario generation is a pivotal tool for managing this uncertainty, existing methods often fail to capture the complex spatio-temporal correlations in RES output. To address this gap, this study develops a hybrid framework utilizing a Copula-Markov chain for temporal correlation and an R-vine copula for spatial correlation. Based on this framework, we propose a novel RES scenario generation method and establish a comprehensive set of evaluation metrics. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms alternatives in coverage ratio, ramp similarity, and scenario bandwidth. Furthermore, the generated scenarios accurately replicate the historical autocorrelation and Kendall’s correlation structures of the original data.

title: GPU-based Parallel Collision Detection for AGV Path Planning
authors: Annalisa Panicieri, Ernesto Fontana, Dario Lodi Rizzini, Stefano Caselli
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: The growing deployment of Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs) in industrial and logistical environments demands efficient and scalable collision detection mechanisms to ensure safe and uninterrupted operation. Traditional CPU-based methods struggle to meet the real-time performance requirements of large-scale, dynamic AGV systems. This paper presents a parallel collision detection algorithm specifically optimized for execution on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs). By leveraging the inherent parallelism of GPU architectures, our approach significantly accelerates proximity checks and collision resolution among multiple AGVs operating in complex environments. We explore data-parallel strategies, including spatial partitioning and bounding volume hierarchies, to manage computational load and reduce latency. Performance evaluations conducted on AGV fleets show substantial speed-up — up to 30x compared to OpenMP-based CPU implementations — while maintaining high detection accuracy. The results highlight the potential of GPU-accelerated collision detection to enhance responsiveness and safety of next-generation AGV systems.

title: A Coordinated Control Strategy with Energy Storage for Enhancing LVRT Capability in PMSG-Based Wind Systems
authors: Lei Hou
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The large-scale integration of renewable energy, especially wind power, introduces significant challenges to the stability and security of modern power systems. Direct-drive permanent magnet synchronous generators (PMSGs) controlled by virtual synchronous generator (VSG) technology can provide inertia and damping support. However, low voltage ride-through (LVRT) remains a critical challenge under fault conditions. This paper proposes an energy-storage-assisted coordinated control strategy to enhance system stability and ensure secure operation. The strategy integrates the machine-side and grid-side converters of the PMSG with supercapacitor energy storage, allowing excess energy to be absorbed during LVRT, thus preventing system instability. Simulations conducted in MATLAB/Simulink validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy and demonstrate its superior fault ride-through performance compared to conventional methods.

title: Technology of Grid-Forming Energy Storage: Characteristics, Applications, Opportunities, and Challenges
authors: Zhanzhan Qu, Ruohuan Yang, Meng Niu, Yating Liu, Xin Wen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: High penetration of renewable energy and large-scale application of power electronic equipment significantly reduce the frequency/voltage stability of traditional power systems, which lead to transient overvoltage, wideband oscillation etc., under electromagnetic timescale, and the stable operation of power systems is facing new challenges. Grid-forming energy storage has the stable energy support capability autonomously responding to voltage and frequency variations, externally manifested as voltage source characteristics participating in the whole process of frequency and voltage control under large and small disturbances and has been carried out pilot applications in Xizang, Xinjiang, etc. Combining with the development and application of grid-forming energy storage, this paper analyzes the technical connotation and dynamic response characteristics, summarizing the typical application scenarios and the status of the test evaluation, and finally elaborate the development opportunities, risks and challenges of grid-forming energy storage in modern power system.

title: Transmission Line Icing Thickness Prediction Method Based on AGA-BP Neural Network
authors: Ma Zhu, Jian Zhou, Sheng Chen, Lei Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: In response to the issues of insufficient accuracy and poor generalization ability in current prediction models for transmission line icing thickness, this paper proposes a BP neural network prediction method optimized by an Adaptive Genetic Algorithm (AGA-BP). Based on meteorological parameters and icing data collected by an online icing thickness monitoring system of an electric power company in Zhejiang Province, China, a three-layer BP neural network structure with five meteorological input features (temperature, humidity, atmospheric pressure, wind speed, and precipitation) is constructed. By dynamically adjusting the crossover and mutation probabilities, the adaptive genetic algorithm globally optimizes the initial weights and thresholds of the BP network, thereby improving parameter search efficiency and effectively avoiding convergence to local optima. Finally, the optimized network parameters are used for training to achieve high-precision prediction of icing thickness. Experimental results show that the AGA-BP model achieves a mean square error (MSE) of 0.317, a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.548, and a coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.963, with prediction errors primarily concentrated within the range of ±1 mm. Compared to the standard BP neural network and the traditional GA-BP model, the AGA-BP model demonstrates significant improvements in both prediction accuracy and stability, providing an effective technical means for ice disaster early warning in power grids.

title: Two-layer Collaborative Optimization Method for Active Distribution Networks Considering Wind and Solar Uncertainty
authors: Huanan Yu, Binghui Liu, Shiqiang Li, Jing Bian, Jia Tang, Shouxian Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The high penetration of distributed generation (DG) poses significant challenges to distribution networks, primarily due to the inherent uncertainties of renewable sources and their impact on secure operation. To address these issues, this paper proposes a bi-level optimization model coordinating DG allocation and line upgrades, explicitly considering both economic and security objectives. A key methodological contribution is the application of Quasi-Monte Carlo simulation combined with Singular Value Decomposition to generate correlated wind-solar power output scenarios, which enhances the modeling accuracy of uncertainties. The upper-level model focuses on minimizing DG investment costs through optimal siting and sizing, while the lower-level model determines the optimal line upgrade strategy to minimize renovation costs. A Cell Membrane-inspired Particle Swarm Optimization (C-PSO) algorithm is employed to solve this coordinated planning problem efficiently. Case studies demonstrate that the resulting planning strategy significantly improves distribution network stability and achieves an optimal trade-off between system economy and operational security, confirming the validity and effectiveness of the proposed model and solution methodology.

title: Research on the Surface Integrity of Inconel 718 Processed by Ultrasonic Vibration-Assisted Milling
authors: Hao Li, Jinda He, Benjie Wang, Wenyang Zhou, Min Xu, Zhongpeng Zheng, Yan Wang, Xiaoming Yin
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: Milling experiments on Inconel 718 alloy were carried out employing ultrasonic vibration milling (UVM) to investigate the surface integrity of this representative difficult-to-machine, high-temperature nickel-based superalloy. In this paper, study and compare a series of surface integrity parameters such as milling mechanism, micro-morphology of machined surface, residual stress and micro-morphology of subsurface between ultrasonic dynamic milling and conventional milling (CM) machining technology. Experimental findings demonstrate that UVM is capable of producing a more uniform and consistent surface finish, markedly enhancing surface microhardness and residual compressive stress, while also promoting the formation and depth of the plastically deformed layer in the subsurface.

title: Design of Robust MPC-Based Master–Slave Control with Disturbance Observer for Islanded Microgrids
authors: Zouhir Boumous, Samira Boumous, Samia Latréche, Badreddine Babes, Mohamed K. Metwaly, Hisham Alharbi, Saleh S. Alharbi, Aymen Flah
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Modern islanded microgrids face critical stability and resilience challenges due to increasing penetration of distributed generation (DG) units and heightened fault susceptibility. This paper proposes a novel robust adaptive control framework that simultaneously integrates: (i) an augmented state-space model with embedded disturbance dynamics for real-time fault estimation, (ii) a hybrid fault detection system combining energy thresholds and harmonic analysis, and (iii) an adaptive Model Predictive Control (MPC) featuring dynamic prediction horizon adjustment and soft-constrained optimization. Experimental validation under severe d-axis (70% sag) and q-axis (60% oscillatory) faults demonstrates precise voltage regulation (<5% reactive power deviation, <8% THD in currents), rapid fault recovery (50 ms for voltage sags, 20 ms for harmonic disturbances), and enhanced dynamic response (42% faster settling time, 50% reduction in overshoot compared to conventional methods). These results confirm the novelty and effectiveness of the proposed strategy in providing a scalable, resilient solution for stable power delivery in RES-rich microgrids, while fully complying with IEEE 1547-2018 standards.

title: Beam Cross Sections Create Mixtures: Improving Feature Localization in Secondary Electron Imaging_supp1-3673616.pdf
authors: Vivek Goyal
publish: N/A
abstract: Secondary electron (SE) imaging techniques, such as scanning electron microscopy and helium ion microscopy (HIM), use electrons emitted by a sample in response to a focused beam of charged particles incident at a grid of raster scan positions. Spot size—the diameter of the incident beam's spatial profile—is one of the limiting factors for resolution, along with various sources of noise in the SE signal. The effect of the beam spatial profile is commonly understood as convolutional. We show that under a simple and plausible physical abstraction for the beam, though convolution describes the mean of the SE counts, the full distribution of SE counts is a mixture. We demonstrate that this more detailed modeling can enable resolution improvements over conventional estimators through a stylized application inspired by semiconductor inspection: localizing the edge in a two-valued sample. We derive Fisher information about edge location in conventional and time-resolved measurements (TRM) and also derive the maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) from the latter. Empirically, the MLE computed from TRM is approximately efficient except at very low beam diameter, so Fisher information comparisons are predictive of performance and can be used to optimize the beam diameter relative to the raster scan spacing. Monte Carlo simulations provide an example of the MLE giving a 5-fold reduction in root mean-squared error (RMSE) of edge localization as compared to conventional interpolation-based estimation. The RMSE is substantially below both the beam diameter and the raster scan spacing and thus sub-pixel localization is demonstrated. Applied to three real HIM datasets, the average RMSE reduction factor is 5.4.

title: Stability Improved Strategy for Near-area Grid-forming Converters Under the Weak Grid Condition
authors: Peng Wang, Junpeng Ma, Shunliang Wang, Rui Zhang, Tianqi Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: For the voltage source inverters (VSIs) based on grid-forming control (GFM-VSIs) in the same renewable energy plant, it is prone to occur the interactive stability issues due to the stiff-connected conditions among GFM-VSIs. To address this issue, a stability improvement strategy for multiple GFM-VSIs is proposed to enable GFM-VSIs to be stably connected in parallel under near-area and stiff-connected conditions, which cannot be achieved by the traditional grid-forming control. By employing the 2-norm (H2), the output characteristics of the VSIs utilizing the hybrid controller to emulate GFM-VSIs are quantitatively described and compared. Thus, the necessity for installation of GFM-VSIs is further demonstrated. Furthermore, the interaction mechanism among GFM-VSIs is elucidated via the simplified stability criterion based on the common mode and differential mode. By applying the proposed strategy, the system stability can be maintained even GFM-VSIs are directly paralleled. Finally, the experimental tests with two parallel GFM-VSIs are conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Control Design Impacts of Grid-Forming STATCOMs for Wind Farm Systems: Weak-Grid Integration and Stabilizing Effects
authors: Dongyeong Lee, Eros Avdiaj, Francisco J. Cifuentes Garcia, Jef Beerten
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: Grid-forming (GFM) control-based converters such as static compensators (STATCOM) are considered as promising solutions for their system support characteristics and robustness against weak grid conditions. Contrary to common belief, GFM converters can also exhibit weak-grid instability, and it is unclear to what extent they stabilize the network. This paper investigates how control design affects both the stable integration of GFM converters into weak grids and their resulting stabilizing effects on the interconnected power system, with particular emphasis on wind farm integration. The analysis reveals that weak-grid stability and system-level stabilization critically depend on the adopted control structure rather than on the grid-forming concept itself. This highlights the necessity of adopting different control design approaches for grid-following (GFL) and GFM converters by demonstrating the opposite effects of voltage feed-forward (VFF) action in the current controller on weak-grid stability. The influences of key GFM converter parameters, such as virtual impedance (VI), are analyzed in terms of passivity for stable integration into weak grids. Their impact on the frequency-dependent power flow Jacobian (FD-JB) is also evaluated for system stabilizing effects. The case studies confirm the findings that although the passivity approach is effective for the converter itself, the resulting stabilizing effect on the complete system can be different depending on its control design.

title: A New Adaptive Harmonic Phasor Estimation Algorithm and its Hardware Implementation
authors: Sarita Nanda, Kuber Saxena, Subhransu Ranjan Samantaray, Innocent Kamwa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: Traditional synchrophasor estimation in distribution network encounters emerging challenges, such as frequency deviations, multiple interferences, and dynamic system behaviors. To address these issues, this paper introduces a Champernowne based second-order adaptive harmonic phasor estimation (HPE), enhanced with super-resolution frequency estimation using an energy-weighted subspace tracking (EWST) based Estimation of Signal Parameters via Rotational Invariance Techniques (ESPRIT) method. The proposed method dynamically tracks the frequency variation in noisy environment, ensuring both high accuracy and fast response to system changes. The dynamic phasors have been modelled using power series that assumes integer powers of AC and DC components, to yield better exactness in the estimation with fewer calculations. Furthermore, the Champernowne function (CMF) with an improved proportionate rule is exploited along with Levenberg Gradient Descent (IPC-LGD) framework which can handle harmonic oscillations through smoother convergence. Utilization of CMF enhances robustness and the improved proportionate notion utilizes the inherent time varying system sparsity for faster rate of convergence. In addition, the outcomes and processing period of the proposed design on the Raspberry Pi board meets the IEC/IEEE 60255-118-1 standard.

title: VAGP-based adaptive Kalman filter for force estimation of robot
authors: Yanjiang Huang, Xinyu Liu, Jianhong Ke, Xianmin Zhang, Lixin Yang, Yanlin Chen, Chunjin Wang, Jun Ota
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: In human-robot interaction, external force measurement is fundamental to achieving robot compliance control. Parameter identification based on robot dynamics enables external force detection without expensive sensors. However, the unmodeled dynamic errors inherent in real robots pose a challenge to force estimation accuracy. In addition, existing force estimation methods often suffer from high computational dimensionality and an excessive number of tuning parameters, which limits their generalizability and migration to other platforms. In this letter, we employ a Variational Approximate Gaussian Process Regression (VAGP) model to learn the robot's dynamic errors, capturing both the mean and covariance of the error. Then we introduced the indirect measurement form and proposed a dimension-reduced Kalman filter (DRKF) to simplify the state space equation. Finally, we propose a VAGP-based adaptive Kalman filter (VAGAKF) that utilizes the least squares method to reduce the number of tuning parameters. VAGAKF effectively separates external forces from dynamics model uncertainty, reducing reliance on highly accurate robot and external force models. VAGAKF reduces average RMSE and time delay by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${23.06\%}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${66.7\%}$</tex-math></inline-formula> respectively, relative to existing methods.

title: Event-based 6-DoF object tracking with distance field reaching 130 Hz
authors: Yufan Kang, Ryoichi Ishikawa, Guillaume Caron, Takeshi Oishi
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Event cameras report asynchronous, per-pixel brightness changes with microsecond latency, making them attractive for high-speed 6-DoF object tracking. We present EDFT, a real-time 6-DoF object tracker that uses only a monocular event camera and a 3D model. At the core is an event-based distance field (EDF) that endows sparse events with two-sided spatial gradients around object edges—overcoming the one-sided behavior of Time Surface maps—and a 3D–2D registration that aligns a sparse, model-derived point set to the EDF. We evaluate on the E-POSE benchmark and on the 5events16 dataset we introduce with this article. On 5events16 (UR10 arm providing ground truth poses, five objects, ten sequences plus 6 handheld sequences), EDFT tracks accurately all sequences successfully contrary to three previous state-of-the-art methods. On E-POSE where objects occupy only a small fraction of the field of view and the camera moves rapidly, EDFT still maintains start-to-finish tracking.

title: Connecting the Dots: Customizing Digital Supply Chain Finance for Dealers via Signaling-Screening Mechanisms
authors: Hua Song, Xinge Ding, Siqi Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: This study identifies a key distinction between digital and traditional supply chain finance (SCF): technology-empowered financial service providers (FSPs) are no longer completely uninformed parties in financing markets. By examining an underexplored digital SCF scenario involving upstream focal firms and financially constrained downstream dealers, we reveal the effective signals that enable downstream small and micro enterprises (SMEs) to access SCF, and explore how and when FSPs use different screens to refine financing decisions. Using a dataset from MYBank, a leading Chinese big tech lender with nationwide coverage and a dominant market share in digital SCF, we find that a dealer's procurement amount from focal firms is a strong signal, particularly when credit limits are higher. Meanwhile, FSPs utilize stakeholder cues and digital footprints within their ecosystems to refine financing decisions. Three key stakeholder groups—owners, focal firms, and peer dealers—and regional digital financial inclusion influence the effectiveness of procurement signals. The impact of distinct signaling-screening mechanisms varies across firm size, platform registration duration, and regional marketization. Robustness tests, including IV-2SLS regressions, the Heckman two-step method, stringent fixed effects and subsample analysis, validate our findings. The study enriches the SCF literature by revealing the role of supply chain data in credit creation and identifying various signaling-screening mechanisms. It also extends screening theory by illustrating the contingent nature of screens. The findings respond to China's recent policy initiatives on decentralized supply chain loans and provide guidance for SCF practitioners.

title: Nonlinear Bayesian Filtering with Natural Gradient Gaussian Approximation
authors: Wenhan Cao, Tianyi Zhang, Zeju Sun, Chang Liu, Stephen S.-T. Yau, Shengbo Eben Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Practical Bayes filters often assume the state distribution of each time step to be Gaussian for computational tractability, resulting in the so-called Gaussian filters. When facing nonlinear systems, Gaussian filters such as extended Kalman filter (EKF) or unscented Kalman filter (UKF) typically rely on certain linearization techniques, which can introduce large estimation errors. To address this issue, this paper reconstructs the prediction and update steps of Gaussian filtering as solutions to two distinct optimization problems, whose optimal conditions are found to have analytical forms from Stein's lemma. It is observed that the stationary point for the prediction step requires calculating the first two moments of the prior distribution, which is equivalent to that step in existing moment-matching filters. In the update step, instead of linearizing the model to approximate the stationary points, we propose an iterative approach to directly minimize the update step's objective to avoid linearization errors. For the purpose of performing the steepest descent on the Gaussian manifold, we derive its natural gradient that leverages Fisher information matrix to adjust the gradient direction, accounting for the curvature of the parameter space. Combining this update step with moment matching in the prediction step, we introduce a new iterative filter for nonlinear systems called Natural Gradient Gaussian Approximation filter, or NANOfilter for short. We prove that NANO filter locally converges to the optimal Gaussian approximation at each time step. Furthermore, the estimation error is proven exponentially bounded for nearly linear measurement equation and low noise levels through constructing a supermartingale-like property across consecutive time steps. Real-world experiments demonstrate that, compared to popular Gaussian filters such as EKF, UKF, iterated EKF, and posterior linearization filter, NANO filter reduces the average root mean square error by approximately 45% while maintaining a comparable computational burden.

title: Understanding Adversarial Imitation Learning in Small Sample Regime: A Stage-Coupled Analysis_supp1-3673238.pdf
authors: Tian Xu
publish: N/A
abstract: Imitation learning (IL) learns a policy from expert trajectories, serving as a fundamental paradigm in both large language model training and embodied AI. This process is challenging due to the nature of sequential decision-making where errors can accumulate and distributions may shift over horizons. However, it has been found that a kind of IL approach, adversarial imitation learning (AIL), can have exceptional empirical performance. With just one expert trajectory, AIL often matches the expert performance even in a long horizon, on tasks such as robotic locomotion control. There are two fundamental yet unsolved questions: why does AIL perform well with so few trajectories, and why does it maintain good performance over long horizons? Previous theoretical results fail to answer these questions as they are meaningful only in large sample regime (i.e., lots of expert trajectories) and have dependence on the decision horizon. In this paper, we analyze a total-variation-distance-based AIL (called TV-AIL), showing a horizon-free imitation gap ${\mathcal {O}}(\min \lbrace 1, \sqrt{|{\mathcal {S}}|/N} \rbrace )$ on a class of instances abstracted from robotic locomotion control tasks. Here $|{\mathcal {S}}|$ is the state space size for a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and $N$ is the number of expert trajectories. We emphasize two important features of our bound. First, this bound is meaningful in both small and large sample regimes. Second, this bound suggests that the imitation gap of TV-AIL does not increase with the decision horizon. Together, our bound can therefore explain the empirical observations and provide insights into how AIL addresses the distribution shift issue. Our analysis leverages the multi-stage policy optimization structure in TV-AIL and presents a new stage-coupled analysis. This tool also helps analyze the worst-case imitation gap of TV-AIL, disclosing its limitations in general MDPs.

title: Method for Reconstruction of Hourly ~1 km All-Weather Land Surface Temperature from FY-4A Geostationary Satellite Observations
authors: Han Gao, Yunhao Chen, Kangning Li, Ji Zhou, Haiping Xia, Weizhen Ji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Land surface temperature (LST) is a key parameter for characterizing the surface energy state and plays a crucial role in agriculture, ecology, and climate research. In recent years, the demand for high spatiotemporal resolution and continuous LST data has been rapidly increasing. Geostationary satellite observations offer great potential owing to their high temporal resolution, but coarse spatial resolution and extensive cloud contamination limit their use. Most existing studies on geostationary satellite LST are limited to a single task, focusing solely on either spatial downscaling or gap-filling, which fails to produce continuous high-resolution datasets. In this study, we propose a novel method for reconstructing hourly ~1 km all-weather (AW) LST based on FY-4A geostationary satellite observations, which simultaneously achieves spatial resolution enhancement and reconstruction within a unified framework. Specifically, a kernel-driven model constrained by the diurnal temperature cycle (DTC) is first developed to conduct spatial downscaling and reconstruct the ideal LST. Subsequently, a hybrid neural network integrating meteorological and radiative drivers is employed to infer weather-induced perturbations. Ground validation demonstrates that the method achieves high accuracy and robust performance across different times, land surface types, and cloud cover conditions (RMSE: 2.53–4.24 K; MAE: 2.00–3.27 K; R²: 0.92–0.95). This study not only provides a new approach for high spatiotemporal resolution AW LST reconstruction from geostationary satellites, but also offers valuable data support for related research fields.

title: Edge ML and IoT-Based Real-Time Detection of Unauthorized Connections in Low Voltage Power Distribution_supp1-3673523.pdf
authors: Arvind Gupta
publish: N/A
abstract: Electricity theft in low-voltage distribution networks not only results in financial losses but also degrades power quality, posing significant risks to sensitive consumer electronics and smart home appliances due to voltage fluctuations. Connecting other appliances before the energy meter or short-circuiting the input and output terminals of an energy meter are two prevalent methods of compromising the reliability of the residential energy supply. To detect such tampering, this study presents a consumer-centric Edge ML solution for real-time theft detection that sends a small test signal through the customer’s power line. By adding the traps, this injected test signal is limited between the electric pole and the energy meter’s output terminal. The detection of power theft is greatly aided by this injected test signal. The consumer’s line current is measured using a current transformer (CT). The test signal is separated from the other line components using a bandpass filter, and its features are extracted using the fast Fourier transform (FFT), standard deviation (STD), and kurtosis. To enable low-latency inference at the consumer edge, the trained model is optimized and deployed on a microcontroller-based edge device. The proposed system demonstrated effective real-time detection capabilities and communicated power-theft alerts to service providers via an Internet of Things (IoT) interface. Both offline and real-time evaluations validate the system’s effectiveness in securing the smart residential energy environment.

title: PhasedRR: Read Reclaim Scheduling without Page-level Access Counting
authors: Li Cai, Jun Li, Zhibing Sha, Zhigang Cai, Jianwei Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Read reclaim (RR) is used to avoid read disturb errors by periodically migrating all valid data pages of the RR block to other free blocks. In order to reduce the negative effects of RR that will block user I/O requests for a long time, existing RR optimization schemes migrate the hot read data pages in advance before the appearance of read disturb errors, if there are idle intervals between two I/O requests. Such advanced RR schemes, however, employ page-level counters for tracking read frequency on the pages in the RR block to direct early hot read data migrations, which must require considerable space overhead and then impact I/O responsiveness. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel RR scheduling scheme, called PhasedRR. Instead of using page-level access counters, PhasedRR incrementally constructs working sets over successive time windows to track the read frequency of recently accessed pages in RR blocks, thus reducing the space overhead caused by maintaining the counters. Furthermore, PhasedRR supports phased migration for moving the data pages having varied levels of read hotness in the RR block to another block(s) in different phases before the hard RR threshold, meanwhile the cold data pages remain in the RR block until the block read count reaches the threshold. Through a series of simulation experiments based on several realistic disk traces, we illustrate that our proposal can reduce the overall I/O latency by 31.9% on average, in contrast to existing RR scheduling methods.

title: Zero-Shot Deep Anti-Aliasing Prior for Residual Artifact Suppression in non-Cartesian k-space MRI
authors: Chuanjiang Cui, Jaeuk Yi, Soo-Hyung Lee, Changmin Ryu, Dong-Wook Kim, Chan-Hee Park, Kyu-Jin Jung, Dong-Hyun Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Non-Cartesian k-space sampling in MRI is widely used, yet images reconstructed on scanners with preliminary corrections (e.g. off-resonance) often exhibit residual artifacts (e.g. ringing and streaking) that can compromise interpretation. We propose a zero-shot residual artifact suppression method that operates directly on scanner-reconstructed images without requiring labeled data, pre-training, or an explicit degradation model. The method builds on a decoder-style generative prior and incorporates a fixed blur-kernel operator that reshapes the network's inductive bias without introducing additional learnable parameters. We formulate the procedure as an optimization problem by minimizing a data-fidelity objective between the network output and the corrupted input image. We evaluate the method on simulated data and demonstrate improved image quality over conventional baselines, while remaining competitive with supervised comparisons under acceleration factors up to R = 4. Across these settings, relative to the artifact-corrupted input, SSIM improves by up to 38% and PSNR increases by up to 10.64 dB. In in vivo experiments, the proposed method consistently attenuates residual aliasing-like artifacts, indicating reproducible performance across acquisitions. Overall, the proposed framework offers a practical and general-purpose post-processing strategy for artifact suppression in non-Cartesian MRI, with applicability across diverse sampling patterns and imaging settings.

title: RustC4++: Improving Rust Code-Comment Inconsistency Detection via Hybrid LLM and Static Analysis
authors: Yichi Zhang, Yang Feng
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: Quantum-Resistant Security for Blockchain-Enabled 6G Networks: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Pasindu Udugahapattuwa, Chamitha De Alwis, Engin Zeydan, Uditha Wijewardhana, Madhusanka Liyanage
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The exponential advancement of quantum computing poses an unprecedented threat to blockchain technology and emerging Sixth Generation (6G) networks, necessitating an urgent transition to post-quantum cryptographic solutions. This comprehensive review proposed post-quantum Cryptography (PQC) integration within Blockchain Enabled 6G (BE6G) architectures, highlighting vulnerabilities by Shor’s and Grover’s algorithms according to existing cryptographic standards, including Rivest-Shamir-Adleman (RSA), Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA), and Elliptic-Curve Cryptography (ECC). The paper addresses quantum computing fundamentals and blockchain security implications, exploring quantum-resistant cryptographic schemes including lattice-based, hash-based, code-based, and multivariate approaches. Through systematic examination of post-quantum threats, this review shows vulnerabilities affecting nearly 25% of existing cryptocurrency assets, presenting how quantum computers can break public-key systems in polynomial time. The study proposes solutions for implementing quantum-resistant blockchain architectures throughout 6G components, including Radio Access Network (RAN), edge computing, and core transport networks, while meeting ultra-low latency and scalability requirements. Key findings elaborate that hybrid cryptographic approaches combining classical and quantum-resistant algorithms provide promising directions. Lattice-based algorithms, particularly CRYSTALS-kyber and CRYSTALS-dilithium, are identified as primary deployment candidates. The review concludes that successful Quantum-Resistant Security deployment for BE6G Networks requires unprecedented coordination among telecommunication operators, blockchain developers, equipment manufacturers, and regulatory bodies. The ”harvest now, decrypt later” threat model creates sudden implementation, valuable for long-term digital infrastructure security.

title: On the Spectrum of OTFS/VOFDM Signals: PSD Analysis and Bandwidth Allocation
authors: Wei Wang, Xiang-Gen Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS)/vector OFDM (VOFDM) is widely regarded as a promising waveform for next-generation mobile communications. However, its spectral characteristics are not yet fully understood. The bandwidth allocation scheme, which is crucial for OTFS’s integration into practical wireless standards, also remains unexplored. In this paper, we investigate the spectral characteristics of OTFS signals by analyzing their power spectral density (PSD). We demonstrate that the PSD of discrete-time OTFS signals is periodic with a period of 1/MTs , where M is the size of the time/Doppler domain in OTFS, a.k.a., the vector size in VOFDM, and Ts is the sampling interval length of digital to analog converter (DAC), resulting in M identical spectral components within the spectral range [− 1/2Ts , 1/2Ts ) of the continuous-time OTFS signal. The periodicity makes bandwidth allocation for OTFS/VOFDM signaling substantially challenging. Furthermore, we establish a relationship between the PSD of OFDM signals and that of OTFS signals, revealing that, when the information symbols are independent, the PSD of OTFS signals is equal to the sum of the PSDs of the component-expanded OFDM (CEP-OFDM) signals. Lastly, we derive a relationship between the information symbols and the corresponding OTFS spectrum, and based on which, we propose a null-space-based linear precoding (NSLP) method for OTFS signals to enable flexible bandwidth allocation. Numerical results validate our analytical results regarding the PSD of OTFS signals and show the effectiveness of our proposed NSLP method in tailoring the spectrum of OTFS signals.

title: Economic Dispatch of Power System Considering the Characteristics of Hydrogen Energy Storage System
authors: Jiyuan Han, Minglei Xie, Sixiao Xin, Wenxuan Cui, Liang Zou, Zhiyun Han, Rongzhao Jia
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the increasing demand for environmental protection, new energy represented by wind power generation is increasingly occupying an important position in the world energy structure.
