title: GDB-TR: Graph-Based Double-Layer Bidirectional Model for Query-Based Trip Recommendation
authors: Xueyao Wang, Wenjing Luan, Liang Qi, Guanjun Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Query-based trip recommendation is an important task in location-based services (LBS), which aims to provide users with a sequence of points of interest (POIs) based on their queries. In trip recommendation, the effect of the visited POIs on the following decisions of users, called a forward effect, is mined by the existing studies. However, the effect of the following POIs on the previously visited ones, called a reverse effect, receives no attention. Therefore, this work proposes a graph-based double-layer bidirectional model for trip recommendation (GDB-TR), which is designed to mine both forward and reverse effects through bidirectional computation. Forward computation explores the influence of user’s history preferences on the next choice; reverse computation explores the influence of future goals on the current decision. Specifically, the model uses a heterogeneous graph to model users’ check-in trajectories with spatial and temporal information. Subgraphs are extracted from the heterogeneous graph, and an adjacency matrix is built for each subgraph. Vector representations of POIs and POI categories are obtained by fusing matrices based on a neural network. The double-layer bidirectional neural network is used to recommend a trip based on the user query, with one layer mining users’ preferences for POIs and the other layer mining the preferences for POI categories. Bidirectional computation is performed between the initial and destination nodes in each layer, capturing both forward and reverse effects. Specifically, a forward computation mines the influence of preceding POIs or POI categories on following ones, while a reverse computation does that reversely. Finally, experiments are conducted on five popular real data sets. The results show that GDB-TR outperforms all baseline models on F 1 and pairs-F 1 values, which validates the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Vision-Based Autonomous Drone Landing on Moving Platforms With Uncertain Motion via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Woojae Shin, Minwoo Kim, Taewook Park, Geunsik Bae, Seunghwan Kim, Hyondong Oh
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: This paper addresses vision-based autonomous landing of quadrotor drones on moving platforms with uncertain motion. Vision is attractive due to its low weight, low cost, and ability to provide direct relative observations without global reference frames. However, traditional visual landing relies on requiring accurate estimation and tuning, which limits robustness. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers a data-driven alternative but often degrades under motion uncertainty or intermittent visual loss from a limited field of view (FOV). The key challenge is active perception, maintaining visual observability of the landing pad under FOV constraints during aggressive maneuvers. To address this challenge, we propose a vision-based DRL framework that jointly learns perception, estimation, and control, guided by an active-perception reward that couples visibility maintenance with control performance for stable touchdown. Simulation results demonstrate robustness over visual servoing and existing DRL baseline, including landings on a platform moving at speeds up to 8 m/s under limited visibility. Real-world experiments further confirm the feasibility and stability of the proposed approach.

title: A Dual-Timescale Model Predictive Voltage Optimization Control Method Considering Distribution Network Dynamic Reconfiguration
authors: Zhihui Wang, Bo Yang, Xin Gao, Yutong Wu, Chen Wang, Chongchong Xu, Lukang Zhang, Hao Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The high-penetration integration of large-scale distributed generation has posed new challenges to voltage control in distribution networks. Traditional methods show significant limitations in handling model parameter uncertainties and coordinating multi-timescale control objectives. To address these issues, this paper proposes a model predictive voltage control method that integrates dynamic reconfiguration with a dual-timescale architecture. By introducing a dynamic network reconfiguration mechanism, the method establishes a two-layer model predictive control optimization framework: the upper layer optimizes slow-response devices at long timescales, while the lower layer adjusts fast-response devices at short timescales, achieving coordinated optimization of network topology and multiple heterogeneous control resources. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with traditional single-layer model predictive control methods, the proposed approach can more effectively suppress voltage violations, significantly reduce system network losses, and exhibits stronger adaptability to model parameter uncertainties, demonstrating superior engineering applicability and operational economy.

title: Transient Overvoltage Mechanism and Suppression in DC Commutation Failure Based on DFIG Reactive Power Optimization Control
authors: Jing Bian, Xiaolu Shang, Jia Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Addressing the issue of wind turbines disconnecting from the grid due to continuous voltage fluctuations ("low-to-high") at the sending-end grid caused by commutation failures in high-voltage direct current (HVDC) systems, this paper analyzes the control blind spots inherent in traditional national standard control during continuous voltage changes. Based on revealing the formation mechanism and morphological characteristics of transient overvoltages, it proposes a fault-riding control strategy optimized for reactive power based on doubly fed induction generators (DFIGs). By introducing reactive current coefficients to refine current commands within the blind zone, this strategy enhances the system's transient voltage support and operational stability. MATLAB/Simulink simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method more effectively suppresses transient overvoltages during commutation failure recovery compared to traditional national standard control.

title: Progressive Training Method for Multi-Aircraft Radar Trajectory Strategy Network
authors: Xuchao Gao, Mingqiang Li, Jianjun Ge, Kai Guan, Zihan Li, Kuoye Han
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: This paper addresses the trajectory-decision problem of a multi-aircraft radar network in complex electromagnetic environments. A neural policy network is designed to map states to control commands. To accelerate training and escape local optima caused by greedy heuristics, expert trajectories generated by a single-step greedy optimizer are used to pre-train the policy via behavior cloning, after which Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) refines the network for global, long-horizon planning. Simulations with four radars and four jamming targets show that the PPO-enhanced policy attains a cumulative reward of 19.8, outperforming both the greedy baseline (13.9) and the knowledge-initialized network (12.2), thereby validating the method’s effectiveness and real-time potential.

title: Sub-synchronous Oscillation Mechanism and Characteristics Analysis of Flexible Low-frequency Transmission System based on RLC Equivalent Circuit
authors: Kai Lv, Xun Mao, Zhen Wang, Wangchao Dong, Yingpei Wang, Shulai Wang, Zhitao Wang, Bingbing Shao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Flexible low-frequency transmission technology has received extensive attention in the new power system due to its significant advantages in the large-scale and medium-long distance transmission of new energy. However, it presents a wideband response characteristic, and there is a risk of sub-synchronous oscillation in the interaction between the control link of the AC-AC converter in the system and the low-frequency AC grid. Therefore, this paper first establishes an electromagnetic transient simulation model of the flexible low-frequency transmission system based on the modular multilevel matrix converter (M3C), and uses the impedance analysis method to establish the RLC equivalent circuit of the system. Then, from the perspective of the equivalent negative resistance resonant circuit, the mechanism of sub-synchronous oscillation caused by the interaction between the low-frequency AC grid and the low-frequency side control link of M3C is revealed. Finally, the influence of the M3C controller parameters on the low-frequency side and the low-frequency line parameters on the sub-synchronous oscillation damping and frequency characteristics is analyzed. The results show that under specific operating conditions, sub-synchronous oscillation is prone to occur on the low-frequency side of the flexible low-frequency transmission system. The low-frequency side control link of M3C shows the nature of "negative resistance and capacitive reactance" at the sub-synchronous frequency, forming an equivalent negative resistance resonant circuit with the low-frequency AC grid which shows "positive resistance and inductive reactance", and causing sub-synchronous oscillation in the system due to the negative damping effect. Moreover, the inner and outer loop control links of the low-frequency side of M3C and the low-frequency line parameters have a significant impact on sub-synchronous oscillation.

title: A Comparative Study of Explainable Hibiscus Leaf Disease Classification Using EfficientNet-B0 and Grad-CAM
authors: Daya Rameshbhai Thummar
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Hibiscus plants are prone to several leaf diseases that have a huge effect on their yield and ornamental value. The current paper examines how deep-learning methods can be used to automatically classify six different states of Hibiscus leaf disease, i.e. Healthy, Chlorosis, Leaf Spot, Senescent, Powdery Mildew and Pest Edge damage, using both real and artificial data sets. At first, a set of authentic images were collected in the eastern areas of Bangalore. This dataset has been used to train an EfficientNet-B0 model, which had training and test accuracy of 0.95 and 0.91 respectively. To overcome these limitations in the dataset, more synthetic pictures have been generated using Stable Diffusion v1.5 with Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) and Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) set to 0.60 and 0.40, respectively. Synthetic augmentation gave a training accuracy of 0.96, but test accuracy dropped to 0.78. When the real and synthetic data were merged, the accuracy of training was 0.95, and the accuracy of testing improved to 0.87. Grad-CAM visualization revealed class-specific characteristics of disease and provided greater understanding of the model. This finding indicates that synthetic augmentation does improve class representation but needs to be refined further to avoid overfitting.

title: Intelligent Accident Detection and Response System Using YOLOv8m and SlowFast Networks
authors: V. Sivakumar, Akash Kumar Verma, Piyush Kumar, Nitesh Saw, V. Hari Krishnan
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Accidents along public areas and roadways remain significant threats to human lives mainly because of delayed identification and emergency response. This work provides an intelligent real-time accident identification and response system that combines YOLOv8m for spatial object detection with the SlowFast network for temporal motion analysis. The combined deep learning model well identifies accidents from regular activities by juxtaposing spatial and temporal features from surveillance videos. When an incident is detected, the system instantly sends alerts with location, timestamp, and visual proof to nearby local authorities for quick response. Experimental tests on real-world and simulated data prove 86.48% accuracy, 83.64% precision, and 79.88% recall, confirming the robustness of the system across various environmental settings. Combining automated detection with emergency communication greatly minimizes response time, thus improving public safety. The suggested framework identifies the potential for sophisticated computer vision methods in building AI-based smart city security systems for real-time incident detection and instant support provision.

title: Design and Experimental Study of a Solar Sail Reflectivity Control Device with Multi-channel and Multi-state Regulation Capabilities
authors: Jun Wu, Kaiwu Tu, Zhu Sun, Guofu Wu, Guanwei He, Shiyao Zhu
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: The solar sail spacecraft can reflect sunlight to obtain light pressure thrust, and can also adjust the reflectivity of the sail surface to obtain attitude adjustment torque to realize the light pressure attitude control function. Firstly, a Reflectivity Control Device (RCD) is developed for a hexagon solar sail spacecraft equipped with Liquid Crystal Membrane (LCM) array, and the differential drive signal with half-cycle pulse width modulation is used to ensure the zero-crossing alternating and isochronous drive characteristics required for liquid crystal membrane control, and effectuate the multi-state and multi-channel control of the reflectivity parameters of the sail surface. Secondly, two ground testing systems are developed in proportion, and the test results indicate that the RCD can consummate the multi-state regulation of the reflectivity parameters of membrane array in multi-channel way, demonstrating its potential as an effective solution for advanced solar sail applications.

title: Optimal Planning of Distributed Energy Storage to Enhance the Source–Load Hosting Capacity of Mountainous Distribution Networks
authors: Dong Fu, Liang Yuan, Hao Peng, Guixi Miao, Xin Wang, Haozheng Yu, Xingxu Zhu, Cuiping Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy into mountainous distribution networks and the increasing complexity of load characteristics, mountainous distribution networks are facing severe challenges in terms of supply-demand balance, operational stability, and voltage quality. To address the operating traits and hosting bottlenecks of distribution feeders in mountainous regions, we develop an optimal distributed-storage configuration to enhance source–load hosting capability. Firstly, an evaluation method for the source-load carrying capacity of mountainous distribution networks is proposed to narrow down and determine the range of nodes where energy storage is to be connected. Secondly, a two-level optimization is established for DESS placement and sizing with both economic and technical objectives; at the upper level, PV absorption and voltage-quality compliance are maximized to select connection nodes and size power and energy. the lower level focuses on minimizing the energy storage investment cost and network loss cost to optimize the charging and discharging strategies of energy storage. Finally, a simulation verification is carried out using the IEEE 33-node system as a case study. The results show that this configuration method reduces the energy storage configuration cost by 12.52%, improves the system power quality, and reduces the network loss by 8.322%.

title: Insulation Defect Detection for Urban Rail Transit Based on XGBoost
authors: Zeyang Tang, XueLi Pan, Gang Li, Xin Mei, Ling Ruan, Zhuang Ruan, Zhen Gao, Xi Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To locate insulation defects in urban rail transit tracks, this paper establishes a simulation model using CDEGS to investigate the variation of stray currents in substations near the tracks when insulation defects are present. A feature engineering framework for stray currents was developed, extracting 53 features from nine aspects including Basic Difference Features. These engineered features were input into an XGBoost model for training. Test results demonstrate that the model can effectively identify the locations of rail insulation defects.

title: Voltage Sag Economic Loss Assessment Method via Physics-Informed Non-equilibrium Dynamics with Minimal Data Requirements
authors: Wenzheng Li, Siying Chen, Qi Cao, Xuyuan Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Voltage sag has emerged as a critical power quality issue causing substantial economic losses to sensitive industrial processes. Existing assessment methods suffer from either limited accuracy due to single-factor considerations or impractical data requirements for comprehensive approaches. This paper presents a minimal data requirement modeling framework based on non-equilibrium thermodynamics theory to overcome these limitations. The proposed method establishes a dynamic model that captures the intrinsic evolutionary mechanisms of voltage-sensitive processes. Through rapid model calibration with minimal measurement data, the approach reduces data requirements from extensive long-term monitoring to sparse sampling at critical points, eliminating engineering barriers of large-scale field experiments. Transforming uncertain voltage tolerance curve (VTC) regions into deterministic events via process parameter deviation quantification, dramatically enhancing practical applicability. Validation demonstrates over 90% accuracy across diverse sag events, providing quantitative basis for optimal mitigation equipment deployment and investment decisions.

title: Comparative Study on Wideband Oscillation Characteristics of Renewable Integration Systems
authors: Yun Gu, Cheng Tang, Jie Sun, Jinqi Zhang, Weiwei Wang, Yiping Yu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy, wideband oscillations have become a critical issue affecting power system stability. This paper presents a comparative study of three typical renewable integration systems: photovoltaic (PV), doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG)-based wind power, and voltage source converter-based high-voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC). A unified modeling and simulation framework, along with fast Fourier transform (FFT), Prony analysis, and time-domain response, is used to identify dominant oscillation modes, damping ratios, and energy distribution across frequency bands. Results show that PV systems are prone to high-frequency oscillations (300-500Hz), DFIG-based wind systems exhibit low-frequency and sub-synchronous oscillations (20-40 Hz) under varying wind speeds and compensation levels, while VSC-HVDC systems display medium(10-20Hz) and high-frequency (>300Hz) oscillations sensitive to controller and filter parameters. The study highlights that distinct control mechanisms and system interactions lead to different oscillation behaviors, providing guidance for targeted suppression and stability enhancement in future multi-source power grids.

title: Detection of AI-Generated Image Using DINOv2 and CLIP
authors: Satheesh K, R. Sujatha, Vasanth dhayanithimaran A, D. Thamizhisai
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: The unprecedented growth of artificial intelligence in visual content creation has made it increasingly difficult to distinguish between real and synthetically generated images. Recent advances in diffusion and generative models such as StyleGAN3 and DALL•E 3 have produced images that exhibit near- perfect realism, posing challenges to digital authenticity, media forensics, and online trust. To address this issue, this paper presents a novel framework for detecting AI- generated images using two powerful deep learning architectures—DINOv2 and CLIP. The proposed system leverages the self-supervised feature extraction capabilities of DINOv2 and the cross-modal semantic understanding of CLIP to enhance the discrimination between authentic and synthetic visual content. Models are trained on datasets containing ProGAN and Latent Diffusion outputs and evaluated on unseen generators, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities. The detection performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{F 1}$</tex>-score, while Grad-CAM++ and UMAP are employed to provide interpretability and visual insights into the learned representations. Experimental results indicate that the hybrid DINOv2- CLIP framework achieves superior detection accuracy and robustness compared to conventional CNN-based methods. This research contributes to the growing field of AI forensics by improving the reliability, transparency, and interpretability of automated synthetic image detection systems.

title: A Rapid Hot-Spot Temperature Computation Model for Dry-Type Transformers Based on Synergistically Optimized Thermal-Neural Network
authors: Zhiwu Yang, Yingting Lou, Mo Shi, Wei Wang, Enze Zhou, You Zhou
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Hot-spot temperature is a key indicator for monitoring the operational condition and assessing the insulation life of dry-type transformers. Traditional fast-calculation models apply a constant ambient temperature as the boundary condition, neglecting the dynamic variation of air temperature within the cooling ducts. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a fast-calculation method to evaluate the winding hot-spot temperature of dry-type transformers by integrating a thermal network model with a neural network. The study uses an SC13-100/10 Class H epoxy resin cast dry-type distribution transformer as the research subject. A multi-physics field simulation model was constructed to analyze temperature variation in different cooling ducts. A simulation dataset generated under parameterized operating conditions then was used to trains a Transformer neural network to refine the duct temperature node in the thermal network model. Results indicate, After dynamic correction of duct temperature nodes in the thermal network model, the calculation errors of hot-spot temperatures in the low-voltage and high-voltage windings were reduced from 7.1% and 2.7% to 1.9% and 1.6%, respectively. The proposed method significantly enhances the computational accuracy of hot-spot temperature in dry-type transformers compared to traditional thermal network models.

title: Examining Blue Collar Adoption of Supply Chain Platforms Using Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
authors: Hendy Tannady, Harits Hijrah Wicaksana, Agung Edi Rustanto, Eva Yuniarti Utami, Degdo Suprayitno
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This study investigates the factors influencing blue-collar workers' intention to adopt supply chain management (SCM) platforms in an Indonesian industrial context. Using a quantitative research design, data were collected from 100 respondents and analyzed through multiple regression analysis. The independent variables examined include perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, beliefs, and trialability. The results show that all variables significantly and positively influence the intention to use SCM platforms, with a coefficient of determination of 72.4% and statistically significant p-values. These findings suggest that both functional and experiential perceptions play a critical role in shaping platform adoption. Based on the results, it is recommended that companies enhance platform interface design, regularly evaluate current SCM systems, offer employees trial access, and utilize beta versions prior to full implementation.

title: DriverTracker: A Hybrid Approach for Driving Behavior Analysis
authors: Tudor-George Goian, Marius-George Marcu
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: This paper presents efforts to enhance driving safety through the analysis of on-road behavior and personalized driver feedback. It reviews recent literature focused on smartphonebased driving assistance applications, highlighting trends in sensor-driven behavior monitoring and in-app feedback. The hybrid mobile application DriverTracker, developed using the Ionic framework, is analyzed, detailing its architecture, implementation, and driver scoring algorithm. Experimental results suggest that DriverTracker can identify unsafe driving behaviors by integrating sensor data with contextual user input before and after the trip. While the current study is limited in scope, it offers a proof of concept for combining subjective and objective metrics in driver behavior analysis.

title: Coordinated Fault Ride Through Strategy for Multi Inverter-based Generators Considering Short-Term Voltage Security
authors: Yinhong Lin, Huifan Xie, Hongtao Liu, Zixiao Lin, Junchen Zhong, Ziyou Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: In high penetration photovoltaic (PV) microgrids, due to the lack of synchronous generators, the dynamic voltage support capacity (DVS) of the grid is insufficient. Meanwhile, since PVs and energy storage systems (ESSs) are generally connected to the grid through inverters, their ability to withstand voltage disturbances and overcurrent is poor. When there is a short circuit fault in power grids, the risk of large-scale disconnection of PVs and ESSs due to transient voltage sag (TVS) and overvoltage (TOV) will significantly increase. To solve this problem, it is necessary to fully utilize and coordinate the DVS of multiple inverter-based generators (IBGs). This problem is essentially a large-scale mixed integer nonlinear programming problem with high-dimensional DAEs, which is difficult to solve. Therefore, based on the influence of different control parameters on voltage dynamic characteristics, a multi IBGs coordinated optimization model with short-term voltage security (STVS) constraints was constructed, and a fast solution method based on physical characteristics was proposed. The effectiveness of the method was verified through time-domain simulation.

title: The Mechanism of Battery Energy Storage Enhancing Transient Power Angle Stability of Sending-End Systems
authors: Zhiyang Yu, Jiayu Liu, Xiaobin Liang, Yu Wang, Xifang Huang, Xingxing Zeng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: An equivalent model of the sending-end system with centralized integration of battery energy storage systems (BESS) was established, and the impact of integrating energy storage into the sending-end system on transient power angle stability was analyzed. Based on the low-voltage ride-through (LVRT) characteristics of energy storage and the equal-area criterion, the influences of the reactive power characteristics of energy storage and the active power characteristics under different charging/discharging states on system transient stability were analyzed. The research results show that the active and reactive power characteristics of energy storage during LVRT can improve system transient stability, and the energy storage system in the charging state is more conducive to enhancing the transient stability of the system.

title: Governor Time Constant and System Damping Identification for Accurate System Frequency Response Model
authors: Zining Liu, Liquan Yu, Wenyun Li, Shiyang Li, Suwei Zhai, Yiping Chen, Changshuo Yu, Yuming Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Accurately predicting the frequency dynamics of low-inertia power systems with a high proportion of renewable energy is a key technical indicator to ensure their safe operation. This paper proposes a novel method for establishing governor time constant and damping identification based accurate SFR (TDI-SFR) model, designs frequency steady-state value based system damping identification process, and adopts an optimization algorithm to recognize the response time constant of the TDI-SFR governor. An IEEE 10-machine 39-node model is built based on the PSCAD/EMTDC simulation platform. Simulation results show that the proposed TDI-SFR construction method has a prediction error of key frequency characteristic values within 5% under two disturbances of different degrees, and can accurately predict the dynamic response trend of the power grid frequency.

title: Comparative Analysis of Filtering Algorithms for Brain Tumor Image Enhancement
authors: Riyajacob, J. Jenkin Winston
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Pre-processing of medical images are important in enhancing the quality of brain tumor images by noise elimination and preserving essential anatomical structures. However, noise introduced during image acquisition like Gaussian and impulse noise, can affect diagnostic accuracy. This work presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of different filtering models for brain tumor image enhancement. Linear filtering is efficient for simple noise elimination but prone to blur tumor boundaries. Non-linear filters enhance preservation of edges but are limited in managing complex textures. Advanced filtering models like Wavelet based filtering, Anisotropic Diffusion Filtering (ADF), Total Variation (TV) denoising, and Non Local Means (NLM) filtering are efficient with on the basis of the denoising potential and structural memory. Among these, experimental outcomes show that NLM filtering ensures the best performance by reducing noise and maintaining fine tumor edges and texture details. The findings show the importance of NLM as a preferred filtering model for high brain tumor image enhancement and achieved better PSNR (48.3 dB) and SSIM (0.999).

title: Fixed-Time Cooperative Control Strategy for Frequency Response of Heterogeneous Demand-Side Resources
authors: Suwei Zhai, Wenyun Li, Zining Liu, Rongzhao Yang, Yiping Chen, Shiyang Li, Siting Zhu, Yuming Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The rapid expansion of renewable energy sources has severely weakened power system frequency regulation capability due to reduced system inertia, necessitating innovative control solutions for distributed demand-side resources. Existing distributed frequency regulation methods rely primarily on asymptotic consensus algorithms whose convergence rates depend heavily on initial conditions and network topology, failing to provide deterministic response time guarantees critical for emergency frequency control. This paper presents a novel fixed-time cooperative control framework for heterogeneous demand-side resources that achieves guaranteed finite settling time independent of system initial states. The proposed approach integrates energy storage systems (ESSs) and inverter air conditioners (IACs) through a two-layer control architecture: (1) a fixed-time dynamic average consensus estimator that enables each agent to reconstruct global frequency signals within an explicitly bounded time and (2) a temporal resource substitution mechanism that orchestrates ESS-to-IAC power handover to exploit fast-response and sustained-regulation capabilities respectively. Comprehensive simulation studies under typical disturbance scenarios demonstrate that the proposed strategy outperforms conventional asymptotic control methods in terms of frequency nadir improvement, regulation speed, coordination performance, and resource utilization efficiency, while maintaining system stability under heterogeneous parameters and communication topology constraints.

title: An adaptive inertia and damping method of Sub-synchronous Oscillation suppression for Grid-Forming Inverter
authors: Yulong Peng, Pingan Tan, Ruiyang Zhou, Changcheng Hu, Jiangnan Xiong
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Grid-Forming Inverters (GFMIs) with Virtual Synchronous Generator (VSG) control provide power electronic devices with inertia and damping similar to synchronous generators by emulating the rotor motion equation. However, such GFMIs are prone to subsynchronous oscillations (SSO) when connected to series capacitor-compensated power grids. To address this issue, this paper first establishes the sequence impedance models of GFMIs (with VSG control) and the series-compensated grid under grid-forming mode. It explores the SSO mechanism via Bode diagram analysis and identifies the limitations of traditional active damping methods. On this basis, a subsynchronous oscillation suppression strategy based on impedance reshaping for GFMIs (with VSG control) is proposed: an impedance reconstruction controller is integrated into the feedback loop of the VSG’s damping coefficient to reshape the system’s impedance characteristics.

title: A CNN-Transformer Dual-Stream Architecture for Enhanced Hyperspectral Image Categorisation
authors: Anitha Govindaram, G. Charlyn Pushpa Latha, K. Logu, Nelson Kennedy Babu C, C. Anuradha, Narenthirakumar Appavu
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: The usage of hyper spectrum images (HSIs) in satellite imagery cataloguing is imperative because it enables correct credentials of materials and land cover categories. While conventional CNNs can be good at identifying localized spatial features, they do not perform as well when dealing with the longrange spectral relationships found in HSIs. On the other hand, Transformers can find long range spectral relationships but tend to ignore small-scale spatial features. Therefore, this research developed DeepsNet, an innovative dual-branch CNNTransformer architecture designed to utilize both the spectral and spatial characteristics of HSIs to create highly accurate HSI classifications. One branch uses a CNN to identify high resolution spatial characteristics of the HSI while the other branch utilizes a Transformer to find the long-range spectral relationships between the bands. Dual-branch architectures such as DeepsNet provide the means to precisely monitor environments, map land use and manage resources, thus contributing to SDG aligned sustainable development and environmental protection initiatives. The complementary representations from each branch are then fused together using a methodically designed fusion process to improve classification accuracy and discriminability. Experiments conducted on several commonly referenced HSI datasets demonstrated that DeepsNet produces higher levels of accuracy than the state-of-the-art methods and performs better under conditions where there are limited amounts of training data available. Furthermore, the experiments showed that DeepsNet is supplementary efficient than the current state-of-the-art devices when processing large volumes of data. Total, the investigational results demonstrate the effectiveness of the dual-branch design for multifaceted HSIs and provide a solid source for upcoming research studies into their likely.

title: Adaptive Matching of Operating Scenarios with Prediction Algorithms for Wind Power Prediction Performance Enhancement
authors: Yuchen Huang, Lipeng Zhu, Anyan Liu, Yunhe Hou
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: For data-driven wind power prediction, it is difficult for a single prediction algorithm to always maintain high performance across all operating conditions in practical wind farms. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an intelligent matching method that adaptively selects appropriate prediction algorithms for different operating scenarios to persistently enhance prediction accuracy against diverse operating conditions. First, a comprehensive performance evaluation index is introduced based on the entropy weight method and the technique for order of preference by similarity to ideal solution. This index scores different prediction algorithms by integrating multi-dimensional indicators, assessing not only their overall performance but also their adaptability to varying conditions and their capability of mitigating large prediction errors. Then, a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) is introduced to cluster historical operating data into three representative operating scenarios based on wind speed fluctuations, i.e., steady-state, small-fluctuation, and large- fluctuation. By applying the comprehensive evaluation index, the optimal prediction algorithm is then identified for each distinct operating scenario. During online application, the predicted wind speed information extracted from numerical weather prediction is fed into the GMM to determine which scenario the current condition belongs to. Based on the scenario assignment result, the corresponding optimal prediction algorithm is called to perform wind power prediction for the current condition. Case studies with field wind power data acquired from real-world wind farms verify the effectiveness and applicability of the proposed method.

title: X-Ray-Based Tuberculosis Detection Using Portable Edge AI Device in Rural Regions
authors: Anika Kanigeri, Amrutha K Illam, Yadu Harish, Sanjeevi G, Rahul Krishnan Pathinarupothi
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) continues to be a major global health challenge, especially in low-resource regions where diagnostic infrastructure is limited. Deep learning models using chest X-rays (CXR) have shown high accuracy for TB detection. However, their reliance on cloud computing restricts deployment in rural and offline settings. In this study, we evaluate three convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures—VGG16, InceptionNet, and ResNet—for automated TB detection and integrate Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) to enhance interpretability. Beyond accuracy, we assess the feasibility of deploying these models on a low-cost edge device (Raspberry Pi) and benchmark inference time against a cloud GPU environment. To test robustness, the models are further validated on two independent datasets representing different regions. Results demonstrate that VGG16 achieves the best diagnostic trade-off between TB and normal cases (AUC: 0.98, F1 Score: 0.93), with high accuracy and clinically interpretable heatmaps, while inference on Raspberry Pi remains comparable to cloud-based performance. This work highlights the potential of interpretable edge AI solutions as affordable and accessible tools for TB screening in underserved communities.

title: A New Principle of Sampled-Value Accumulation Quantity Differential Protection Based on Negative-Sequence Control Differences of Low-Frequency Converters
authors: Shuyang Wang, YiBin Zheng, Zexin Zhou, Hong Cao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Flexible low-frequency AC transmission (LFAC) systems based on the Modular Multilevel Matrix Converter (M3C) represent an emerging power transmission technology. Their core principle involves reducing the AC transmission frequency (typically to 16.7 Hz or 20 Hz) to decrease cable charging current and line losses, while leveraging the modular multilevel structure of the M3C to achieve efficient and flexible power transmission and control. Currently, the main protection for existing LFAC line projects still employs phasor differential protection based on Kirchhoff's current law. While phasor differential protection offers stable and reliable performance, its sensitivity is insufficient due to the influence of fault current limiting strategies employed by M3C AC/AC converters and wind turbine converters in low-frequency systems. This paper proposes an improvement scheme for phasor differential protection, based on the differences in converter control characteristics within flexible LFAC systems. The scheme incorporates information on the magnitude difference of negative-sequence currents from both line ends into the restraining criterion. This maps the differences in negative-sequence current control among various low-frequency converters into an adaptive restraining coefficient. The equivalent restraining coefficient is dynamically adjusted according to the ratio of negative-sequence current magnitudes at both ends. During internal faults, the restraint quantity is significantly reduced, enhancing protection sensitivity. During external faults, the reliability of the original differential protection is maintained. This approach balances protection sensitivity and reliability, thereby improving the sensitivity of the main protection for flexible LFAC transmission lines.

title: Segmentation of the Retinal Vascular Network and Biomarker Quantification in OCTA Imaging
authors: Darius-Iulian Stan, Mihnea Bogdan Jurca, Raluca Didona Brehar
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: This paper addresses two closely related research directions in Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) imaging: (1) segmentation of the retinal vascular network, and (2) computation and quantification of vascular biomarkers. For the segmentation task, two key modifications to the I-MedSAM architecture are introduced: a Frangi filter-based enhancement component to emphasize tubular structures, and the SoftCLDice loss function to promote topological preservation during training. The adapted model is evaluated alongside two state-of-theart segmentation networks, SSW-OCTA and FRNet, using the OCTA-500 dataset. All models are trained and tested on an identical dataset split, employing both the 3 mm and 6 mm field-of-view (FOV) subsets to capture variations across imaging scales. A comprehensive evaluation-comprising both quantitative metrics and qualitative visual analysis-is conducted to assess segmentation performance and structural fidelity. For the second research direction, we propose a structured and robust pipeline for vascular biomarker computation, grounded in a formal representation of the retinal vascular (RV) network. Starting from the binarized and skeletonized vessel masks, a complex data structure is constructed in which the RV is decomposed into ordered segments and classified nodes. This representation explicitly identifies and categorizes key morphological features, including endpoints and vascular junctions, enabling precise localization and characterization of structural components. A quantification stage is also introduced to aggregate the computed biomarkers into global descriptors of the retinal vasculature, enabling statistical testing to assess their effectiveness in distinguishing between pathological and healthy cases.

title: Influence of Active Power Regulation Command on Dynamic Performance of Small-Scale Pumped Storage Plants
authors: Kangyi Cheng, Weijia Yang, Peilin Wu, Zhecheng Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the integration of variable renewable energies into power systems, small-scale pumped storage plants (SPSPs) play a significant role in enhancing regional grid flexibility with flexible deployment, convenient grid connection, and short construction periods. To address the insufficient quantitative analysis of the dynamic characteristics of SPSPs, this study investigates the influence of the rate and amplitude of active power regulation on the dynamic performance and proposes a quantitative evaluation method. In this paper, a simulation model of a SPSP is introduced in MATLAB/Simulink. Through time-domain simulations, the effects of different active power regulation commands on dynamic characteristics of the SPSP are analyzed. Considering active power response behavior and the wear characteristics, a dynamic method is proposed to quantify the dynamic performance of SPSPs. The results show that, when the pump-turbine operates under conditions of decreasing active power output, the active power regulation time is negatively correlated with the regulation rate, while the active power overshoot and wear characteristics are positively correlated with regulation rate and amplitude. Under the conditions analyzed in this study, the SPSP exhibits better performance at the maximum regulation rate under small-amplitude regulation, whereas improved performance is attained at the minimum regulation rate under large-amplitude regulation conditions. This paper could provide technical support for the safe and efficient operation of SPSPs in power systems.

title: Benchmarking electrical, thermal, and mechanical performance of various industrial REBCO coated conductors for magnet applications
authors: H. Himanshu, J. B. Song, O. Jay, X. Chaud
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: Rare-earth barium copper oxide (REBCO) superconducting tapes have experienced a rapid rise in demand and production, driven largely by high-field magnet and fusion applications. With the ongoing push for higher critical current density and long-length deposition, material used and fabrication processes continue to evolve. This makes regular independent evaluation and comparison of commercial products essential. In this work, we benchmarked tapes from four of the major manufacturers, assessing their electrical (at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{77}~\text{K}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), thermal, and mechanical performance (at room temperature). In addition, two batches of THEVA conductors from different production years were compared, highlighting improvements in their overall suitability for high-field magnet applications. The results provide a statistically reliable basis for magnet coil design, improvements in joint fabrication, and the establishment of safer operation margins.

title: Novel Structure of a Deep Neural Network-Based Material Removal Rate Prediction Model for Multizone Chemical Mechanical Planarization Process Control
authors: Bangxu Liu, Dewen Zhao, Xinchun Lu, Yuhong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Semiconductor Manufacturing
abstract: Multizone pressure chemical mechanical planarization (CMP), which is regarded as irreplaceable in semiconductor manufacturing, is highly dependent on a process control strategy. However, predictive models with strict rules for advanced CMP multizone pressure process control have rarely been examined, even with the development of machine learning applications in semiconductor fabrication. Establishing a precise data-driven model that takes the fabrication conditions into consideration is urgent for controlling the CMP process. A novel structure for the CMP material removal rate model consisting of two parts is proposed and validated according to split pressure experiments and feature importance learning by this article. The first part was devoted to the prediction of the polishing time and time dependent problem with a deep neural network. The other part focused on the mutual effect of the pressure distribution on the multizone material removal rate using deep neural network with fully connected layers. To improve the prediction model of the first part, this study devises an advanced predictive model that combines a bidirectional gated recurrent unit network with a multi-head self-attention module and a one-dimensional neural network. This deep network extracts time-varying factors from industrial dataset and establish a nonlinear mapping function from CMP process features to material removal rate with no pressure variables. Both part of this structure is tested and evaluated for the feasibility of the predictive model using dataset from industrial fabrication.

title: AMCL4SR: Adaptive Multitask Collaborative Learning for Sequential Recommendation via Pareto Optimization
authors: Qilin Li, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Recommendation systems aim to enhance user satisfaction by providing personalized recommendations based on historical behaviors. Sequential recommendation, capable of capturing temporal dynamics and predicting future actions, has gained significant attention. However, existing methods typically focus on individual sequences and overlook collaboration between sequences, which limits their effectiveness. To address this, we propose AMCL4SR, an adaptive multitask collaborative learning framework optimized via Pareto optimization (PO). The entire framework is optimized under the paradigm of multitask learning using PO. First, we adopt an adaptive approach to learn a global collaborative graph among all items and employ an unsupervised strategy to leverage this graph for capturing global collaborative information. Furthermore, we leverage dynamic positional encoding to capture temporal dependencies and global contextual information, optimizing the interaction between sequential and global information. Finally, the framework is optimized using the multitask learning paradigm, enabling the modules of AMCL4SR to work together and improve performance. Experimental results show that AMCL4SR significantly outperforms baseline methods, demonstrating strong generalizability, effective global information modeling, and mitigation of embedding dimensionality collapse.

title: A Deep Learning Approach for Gain-Scheduled PID Control Design via Advanced Supervisory Controllers
authors: Ercan Atam, Atakan Zeybek, Şaziye Betül Özateş, Nehir Güzelkaya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Proportional-integral-derivative (PID) control is by far the most widely used control type in industry. However, for some systems even the optimally tuned PID control system can be unsatisfactory, especially when the underlying system is nonlinear, includes parameter-varying components and time delays or when system dynamics change with time. Due to the long-standing development and readiness of the infrastructure of PID controllers in the industry, along with cost factors, it is essential to improve the system performance without replacing the established structure. Gain-scheduled PID control is a strong solution for such situations. In this paper, we present a new approach for the gain-scheduled PID control design using deep learning to learn from advanced controllers such as linear parameter-varying controller and reinforcement learning agent. For each gain of PID, a deep artificial neural network is developed as a function of the scheduling signals. To validate the proposed approach, we tested it on two challenging case studies and compared its performance against optimally tuned conventional PID controllers with fixed parameters, as well as advanced state-of-the-art controllers that the gain-scheduled PID controllers aim to emulate. The proposed gain-scheduled method provided up to 71.6% performance improvement relative to the optimally tuned PID controller with fixed gains.

title: Online Charge Balancing with Active Impedance Monitoring in Programmable Stimulator to Extend Electrode Lifetime
authors: Kangni Liu, Guangzong Chen, Kevin Woeppel, X. Tracy Cui, Rajkumar Kubendran
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Neurostimulation therapies are often applied as an alternative method to pharmaceutical treatment for pain relief.This paper demonstrates a programmable stimulator for analgesic nerve stimulation through invasive electrodes. It provides two modes of operation: constant-current biphasic and capacitor-coupled biphasic. The stimulator is integrated on a 50 x 30 mm Printed Circuit Board (PCB) and can generate up to ±1.6 mA current pulses in steps of 6.3 μA. A custom STM32 microcontroller library is used to tune the pulse duration range from 1 μs to 10 s and stimulation frequency range between 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. An Active Impedance Monitoring (AIM) unit in the PCB measures the frequency response from 1 Hz to 10 kHz with 8 samples per decade every 30 minutes. The electrode resistance, tissue resistance and RC time constant of the electrode-tissue system could be calculated from the frequency response curve, generated by an input square wave stimulation current at different frequencies. In different measurements with discrete RC circuit components, the PCB could calculate the impedance values with >90% precision. A novel charge-balancing algorithm implemented on the STM32 microcontroller could match the positive and negative charge within a 5% error. Through online adjustment of stimulation parameters using active impedance monitoring to achieve charge balancing, the proposed programmable stimulation system extends the electrode lifetime for more than 288 million continuous pulses in vitro studies. The in vitro studies clearly demonstrated the efficacy of charge balanced stimulation that enable the electrodes to last around 3 times longer with negligible degradation.

title: OAlight: Overflow-Aware Adaptive Traffic Signal Control via State-Wise Reinforcement Learning
authors: Xiang Fu, Haiyang Yu, Han Jiang, Zhiyong Cui, Yilong Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Adaptive traffic signal control dynamically adapts to real-time traffic flow variations, effectively mitigating traffic congestion through efficient decision-making. Current ATSC methods primarily focus on vehicle mobility metrics, such as average waiting time and average speed. However, urban road networks require not only high traffic efficiency but also proactive prevention of critical scenarios like overflow, which, if not intervened, may degrade network-wide traffic conditions and compromise safety. Therefore, in order to a balance between overflow prevention and efficiency, we model traffic signal control as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP), and propose OAlight, an overflow-aware adaptive traffic signal control framework based on reinforcement learning (RL). Unlike existing RL approaches that merely incorporate overflow-related features into the reward function, OAlight integrates overflow awareness throughout the entire RL lifecycle — environment interaction, state representation, and policy learning. Specifically, considering the explicit overflow factor of excessive queue lengths and the implicit overflow factor of excessive waiting time for individual vehicles, we construct a dual safeguard mechanism for overflow prevention through environmental interactions by developing a comprehensive reward-cost function framework. In this framework, the reward function simultaneously captures both explicit and implicit factors, while two specialized cost functions assess behaviour that violates threshold performance metrics of explicit and implicit factors, respectively. We then design a state encoder and a state-action joint encoder to extract overflow-aware representations from the state space. Finally, we employ a Proximal Policy Optimization with Lagrangian method to guide policy learning, ensuring compliance with overflow constraints while optimizing control actions. The experimental results indicate that our approach achieves superior performance on our proposed overflow cost metric without compromising traffic efficiency metrics on both synthetic and real-world datasets, compared to the current state-of-the-art traffic signal control methods. Furthermore, the environment interaction module exhibits broad compatibility with diverse RL methods, significantly reducing overflow risks.

title: A Dual-Band Dual-Beam Shared-Aperture Antenna for Multi-Orbit Satellite Communication
authors: Wenao Li, Su Ma, Ye Wang, Yihong Xu, Kaixu Wang, Lin Mei
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this letter, a dual-band, dual-beam shared-aperture antenna combining a variable inclination continuous transverse stub (VICTS) antenna and a patch antenna array (PAA) is proposed for simultaneous connections with two satellites. The VICTS generates a Ku-band scanning beam, whereas the PAA provides a K-band fixed-direction beam. By mounting the PAA on the surface of the radiating layer of the VICTS antenna, a shared-aperture configuration is achieved. The feeding network of the PAA is specially designed, and long slots are etched on both the substrate and the ground plane to effectively mitigate the interference. To verify the proposed design, a prototype was fabricated and measured, showing good agreement between the measured and simulated results.
