title: PSO-HEAD: Pseudo-Supervision Guided Spatial Optimization for View-consistent 3D Full-Head Reconstruction
authors: Peng Zhang, Yiheng Zhang, Xiaolin Zhang, Chongxin Liang, Caifeng Shan, Haojie Li
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: Fine-grained Object Recognition Algorithm Based on Self-Supervised Representation Learning of Multi-View Infrared Images
authors: Peng Wu, Zhen Zuo, Longqing Li, Xiaozhong Tong, Shaojing Su, Boyuan Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Utilizing multi-view infrared images to collaboratively identify the types of surface ship targets is a feasible approach in practice. This paper proposes a fine-grained object recognition algorithm based on self-supervised representation learning of multi- view infrared images. A contrastive learning framework for target recognition based on multi-view infrared images is introduced, employing a training method of pre-training with unlabeled data and fine-tuning with a small amount of labeled data, which enhances the generalization ability of the algorithm. By optimizing the instance construction method, the similarity and differences between instances are better extracted, learning invariant features of the same target from different views; by combining the cross-correlation matrix, redundancy in feature representation is reduced; a target recognition application method with adaptive input of multi-view images is proposed, improving the accuracy of downstream target recognition tasks; a multi-view infrared image dataset of ship targets is constructed and algorithm validation is performed. Experimental results show the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1 score of proposed method are 95.1%, 95.2%, 95.3%, and 95.2%, respectively, which are higher than the results of the SimCLR algorithm by 7.1%, 6.7%, 6.8%, and 6.7%, and higher than the results of the BYOL algorithm by 7.7%, 7.6%, 7.3%, and 7.4%.

title: An Enhanced Low-Computational-Complexity Predefined-Time Convergent Zeroing Neural Network for Constrained Time-Varying Quadratic Programming With Kinematic Control of Robotic Manipulator
authors: Junpei Yang, Zhan Li, Weibing Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Time-varying quadratic programming (TVQP) problems can be regarded as a challenging issue in a wide range of engineering applications, frequently incorporating equality, inequality, and bound constraints. By integrating a nonlinear complementarity problem (NCP) formulation, zeroing neural networks (ZNNs) can be generalized to solve TVQP problems effectively with such a full set of constraints. However, three issues may limit its computational efficiency and practical applications: 1) the inherent formulation of NCP functions for handling equality/inequality and boundary constraints substantially expands dimensions of coefficient matrices/vectors and solution-space variables; 2) the conventional ZNN (CZNN) framework inevitably necessitates matrix inversion operations for real-time solutions; and 3) insufficient robustness against noise interference compromises solution accuracy in practical implementations. To overcome these issues and promote solution performance, this article develops an enhanced lower dimension NCP low-computational-complexity (LCC) ZNN (ELNCP-LCCZNN) model for solving TVQP problems with time-varying equality, inequality, and variable boundary constraints. An ELNCP function is designed to reduce the matrix/vector coefficients of the model, and the LCCZNN model is utilized to construct a new dynamic model that eliminates the necessity for matrix inversion during solution. Furthermore, a nonlinear activation function is involved to guarantee predefined-time convergence and strengthen robustness against noise. The theoretical properties of the proposed ELNCP-LCCZNN model are validated through numerical simulations and experiments on robotic manipulator kinematic control. The results corroborate the analysis and demonstrate improved computational efficiency, enhanced noise robustness, and practical implementability compared with existing approaches.

title: Variational GAN Enhanced Causal Effect Inference for Interpretable Learning in Heterogeneous IoT Systems
authors: Xiaokang Zhou, Wei Liang, Katsutoshi Yada, Laurence T. Yang, Zheng Yan, Kenli Li, Qun Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: While data-driven learning techniques are revolutionizing the smart application design and development, fundamental issues of lacking generalizability and interpretability are posing great threats to their effectiveness in real-world implementations. Such weaknesses can be complemented by advanced Artificial Intelligence (AI) models that look at deeper relationships of association, intervention, and counterfactual, toward interpretable learning especially when facing heterogeneous big data generated through distributed devices or sensors in modern Internet of Things (IoT) systems. In this paper, we design and introduce a so-called Variational GAN with Causal Heterogeneous Graph (VGAN-CHG) model to facilitate the causality-inspired interpretable learning, which can effectively enhance the causal effect inference with the help of reconstructed latent variables in terms of the discovered temporal causality and enriched variational representation, and further generate more realistic counterfactuals for optimized Individual Treatment Effect (ITE) estimation, when tackling the issue of limited observational data in heterogeneous IoT systems. In particular, the variational GAN, incorporating a Bipartite Variational Autoencoder (BVAE) into a conditional GAN structure, is newly designed and applied to realize the Variational Representation Learning (VRL) and adversarial training together. While the CHG, constructed with a Graph Attention Network (GAT) mechanism, is first defined and implemented to seamlessly capture and model the covariational feature correlation, temporal dependency, and structural causal relationship in a unified form, so as to enhance the causal representation learning for feature-level interpretability and credibility in heterogeneous IoT scenarios. In addition, the BVAE, is devised and built to capture and reconstruct the so-called global and regional latent variables in terms of the sequential global context and time-specific fluctuation from inter-/intra-time intervals, thus can jointly optimize the bias reduction, counterfactual reasoning, and accuracy of ITE estimation. A refined representation learning scheme is then developed, in which the Dynamic Bayesian Network (DBN) is involved to enhance the cross-interval dependency considering the fused temporal causality effect, while the Similarity-Preserving Data Mapping (SPDM) is integrated to improve the geometry-aware feature consistency between the original and learned latent space, resulting in the further feature refinement with enriched interpretable context and sufficient variability. Experiment and evaluation results based on three public datasets demonstrate that the proposed model is able to achieve higher learning efficiency, better bias reduction, and optimal treatment estimation with refined feature representation, compared with six other state-of-the-art similar learning methods for modern intelligent system design and smart application deployment in heterogeneous environments.

title: New FFIMMC Control Design for the Detection and Mitigation of Hybrid Cyber Threats
authors: Vivek Kumar, Yogesh V. Hote
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The vulnerability to cyber threats has increased significantly with the rapid development of the modern power sector and the progressive integration of open communication systems. In response, this article proposes a proactive hybrid control concept to detect and mitigate mixed cyber threats. The proposed approach not only provides a robust defense against potential cyber-attacks but also addresses the control problems, such as uncertain delays. First, this article introduces a novel concept, fractional filter-based internal multimodel control (FFIMMC), designed to minimize the effect of uncertain delay. Second, the FFIMMC technique combines with the gradient-descent and Smith predictor methods to detect and mitigate hybrid cyber-attacks in power systems. Several parameters must be maintained and controlled in the power system to generate and supply power of better quality, such as frequency and tie-line power. Therefore, the proposed concept is rigorously tested via the load frequency control problem of the power system in the presence of time-varying loads, uncertain delays, and hybrid cyber-attacks. This thorough testing process ensures the reliability of the proposed approach. Finally, the proposed approach is validated through the hardware setup (OPAL-RT OP5600), which provides reassurance about its effectiveness.

title: Mask-Supervised Dual-Branch Detection Network for Oriented SAR Ship Detection_supp1-3673209.pdf
authors: Yong Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Oriented ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is widely used for maritime surveillance. However, existing methods suffer from two issues: 1) the feature enhancement process lacks explicit supervision, making it difficult to reliably enhance object features; and 2) only a prediction is generated for each proposal, which may cause missed detections. To address these two issues, we propose a mask-supervised dual-branch detection network (MSDD-Net), which integrates a mask-supervised feature enhancement (MSFE) module and a dual-branch detection head. In the MSFE module, multi-level features are first fused to generate a saliency map, and then this saliency map is explicitly guided to focus on the object regions by joint supervision of an object-aware mask and a center decay mask, with the aim of obtaining enhanced object features (i.e., addressing the first issue). Afterward, the enhanced object features are processed by a region proposal network to output multiple proposals. In addition, the dual-branch detection head generates two predictions for each proposal and matches each proposal with two ground-truth (GT) bounding boxes. Finally, a triplet loss is designed to improve the consistency between two predictions and their corresponding GT bounding boxes and the discrimination between two predictions, with the aim of reducing missed detections (i.e., addressing the second issue). Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of MSDD-Net over state-of-the-art methods.

title: Comparative Analysis of Deep Learning Models for Lesion-Wise Segmentation of Diabetic Retinopathy
authors: Akansha Gupta, Arjun Singh Rawat, Gunjan
publish: IEEE MultiMedia
abstract: In this paper, a systematic and reproducible comparative evaluation of seven widely used deep learning architectures U-Net, U-Net++, Attention U-Net, DeepLabV3+, PSPNet, Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), and Holistically-Nested Edge Detection (HED) has been carried out for the segmentation of diabetic retinopathy (DR) lesions using the publicly available DDR dataset. Particular emphasis has been given to the design of a unified benchmarking pipeline incorporating standardized preprocessing, patch-based sampling, and hybrid loss functions for evaluating the effect of each network’s architectural design. Performance is quantified through Intersection over Union (IoU), Dice, Precision, Recall, and F1-score to provide a balanced assessment of segmented area and stability. Results indicate that U-Net and U-Net++ provide the best balance between accuracy and robustness, DeepLabV3+ excels in exudate segmentation, Attention U-Net improves recall for microaneurysms, HED sharpens boundaries, and PSPNet and FPN are faster. Test-time augmentation (TTA) yields 1-2% IoU/Dice improvement with minor inference-time increase.

title: Edge-Aware Crop Field Boundary Mapping from Multimodal Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 Data
authors: Mehmet Furkan Celik, Claudio Persello, Andrew Nelson, Giulia Conchedda, Francesco Tubiello, Claudia Paris
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Crop field boundaries plays a pivotal role in agricultural monitoring. Accurately mapping these boundaries using satellite data, however, remains a significant challenge as the quality of the results is strongly influenced by the field size and the phenological state of the study area. Leveraging the complementary information of Sentinel-1 (S1) and Sentinel-2 (S2) data, this work proposes an edge-aware crop boundary mapping approach that fuses multitemporal S1 with S2 monthly composites in a dual-stream U-Net supported by Spatial and channel squeeze-and-excitation (scSE) and mid-level feature fusion. To refine the boundary delineation, we introduce a composite loss combining binary cross-entropy, Tversky loss, and a Sobel edge penalty. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed multimodal U-Net architecture achieves the highest accuracy, with the Sobel loss term contributing edge sharpness and continuity. On the publicly available AI4SmallFarms benchmark, which includes over 400,000 field polygons across 62 tiles ( 5×5 km) in Vietnam and Cambodia, our approach produces sharper, continuous boundaries and fewer spurious edges, particularly for smallholder farms with highly fragmented agricultural areas. The benchmark is extended by adding analysis-ready, multi-temporal S1 VH images (can be downloaded from https://doi. org/10.5281/zenodo.18921642) coregistered with the existing S2 data. The source code for the proposed framework is available at https://github.com/mf-celik/multimodal-crop-boundary.

title: Real-time Monocular 2D and 3D Perception of Endoluminal Scenes for Controlling Flexible Robotic Endoscopic Instruments_supp1-3672482.mp4
authors: ruofeng wei
publish: N/A
abstract: Endoluminal surgery offers a minimally invasive option for early-stage gastrointestinal and urinary tract, but is limited by basic surgical tools and a steep learning curve. Robotic systems, particularly continuum robots, provide flexible instruments that enable precise, intuitive tissue resection in confined spaces, potentially improving outcomes. This paper presents an integrated visual perception platform for a continuum robotic system in endoluminal surgery. Our objective is to leverage monocular endoscopic image-based perception algorithms to accurately identify the position and orientation of flexible instruments and measure their distances from surrounding tissues. This thorough understanding of continuum robots and surgical scenes enhances the robustness of robotic procedures. We introduce 2D and 3D learning-based perception algorithms and develop a physically-realistic simulator that models the dynamics of flexible instruments. This simulator features a pipeline for generating realistic endoluminal scenes, enabling control of flexible robots in a realistic environment and substantial data collection. Using a continuum robot prototype, we conducted extensive evaluations, including module assessments and system-level evaluation of the perception platform. Results demonstrate that our perception algorithms significantly improve control of flexible instruments, reducing manipulation time by over 70% for trajectory-following tasks and enhancing the understanding of complex surgical scenarios, leading to robust endoluminal surgeries.

title: Ultra-Thin Flexible Dual-Band Dual-Polarized Antenna for mmWave 5G Wearable Electronics
authors: Saeid Alamdar, Sahar Bagherkhani, Franco De Flaviis, Soheil Saadat
publish: IEEE Journal on Flexible Electronics
abstract: An ultra-thin, dual-band, dual-polarized antenna with improved isolation between the two polarizations, designed on a 248-μm thick flexible printed circuit (FPC) board, is presented in this paper. In thin flexible millimeter-wave antennas, maintaining high isolation between orthogonal polarizations across two frequency bands is particularly challenging due to strong coupling through the patch structure and limited substrate thickness. To address this issue, an asymmetric dual-polarized patch antenna employing a partial differential feeding technique is proposed, in which only one port is differentially fed using a wideband coupled-line phase shifter integrated with a T-junction. This configuration effectively suppresses coupling and enhances isolation. The proposed antenna operates simultaneously in the 27.5–28.7 GHz and 37–38.5 GHz bands, achieving measured isolation better than -25 dB between the two polarizations.

title: AI-Driven Real-Time Stock Forecasting Framework with Kafka and Cassandra
authors: Sridhar Udayakumar, E. Anbalagan
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Financial markets nowadays are an arena with high-frequency trading (HFT) and real-time decision-making, where milliseconds matter between making or losing money. Traditional batch-processing architectures can't keep up with the velocity, volume and volatility of modern financial data. In order to overcome this, we propose a scalable real-time forecasting system incorporating Apache Kafka for distributed ingestion, Apache Cassandra for scalable persistence, and hybrid AI models for predictive analytics. The framework takes in multiple data streams, such as tick-level trades, financial news, social media sentiment and macroeconomic indicators, and processes them through a Spark-based streaming pipeline. Forecasts are made with a hybrid ensemble of long short-term memory (LSTMs) and Random Forests based on a mixture of sequential learning and strong classification. Experimental validation on S&P 500 tick data (2015-2024) shows that the framework is able to achieve sub- 50 ms latency, maintain over 2.5 million events per second throughput, and provide forecasting accuracy gains over 50 % compared to traditional baselines. Case studies of the market crash during the pandemic (2020) and the Federal Reserve interest rate hike (2022) prove the robustness of the system during periods of extreme volatility. These results make the framework a production-ready solution for next generation stock market forecasting, with wide applicability to other areas such as healthcare, cybersecurity, smart transportation and energy systems, where reliable decision making in real time is equally as important.

title: Enhancing Workability Prediction of Concrete Using Ensemble Machine Learning Models
authors: Rupesh Kumar Tipu, Owais Ahmad Shah
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This study integrates ensemble machine learning models with traditional concrete research methods to predict concrete workability. Leveraging a dataset of 103 laboratory experimental samples, key parameters such as Cement, Slag, and Water were explored for their impact on Slump. Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost Regressors demonstrated high accuracy, with XGBoost emerging as the most precise predictor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}=\mathbf{0. 9 5 6}$</tex>). SHAP analysis revealed Water, Slag, and Coarse Aggregate as influential factors. This research pioneers an efficient and accurate methodology, showcasing the synergy of machine learning in civil engineering.

title: Stray Current Risk Prediction in Urban Rail Transit Using Marchine Learning
authors: Xin Mei, Zeyang Tang, Ling Ruan, Wanting Deng, Yibo Cui, Wei Rao, Yiwen Li, Shanshan Ke
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To predict stray current risks in urban rail transit systems, this study collected data from 17 substations, including stray current measurements, minimum distances between substations and metro lines, and DC resistance values of transformer high-voltage windings. A machine learning model—XGBoost—was developed to forecast stray current risks based on these influencing factors. The model demonstrated an accuracy of 0.9231 on the traning set and 0.75 on the test set. It is evident that features X9 and X6 hold the highest importance, each with an F score of 27.0, indicating they are the most influential in predicting the target variable. This research provides actionable insights for implementing stray current risk evaluation in urban power substations.

title: A State Task Network-Based Method for Adjustable Potential Analysis and Flexible Scheduling in Shipbuilding Processes
authors: Kun Shi, Feixiang Gong, Jiawei Dong
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The shipbuilding industry is characterized by long production cycles, strong resource coupling, and complex multi-stage processes, which often lead to conflicts, bottlenecks, and inefficiencies in conventional static scheduling. To enhance system flexibility and support grid demand response, this paper proposes a State Task Network (STN)-based method for adjustable potential analysis and flexible scheduling in shipbuilding processes. The method quantitatively models the adjustment potential of key processes by incorporating slack degree, load elasticity, and resource redundancy, and further integrates a temporal optimization and resource coordination mechanism. Based on stage-wise adjustment formulas and system-level scheduling strategies, the framework enables the extraction of differentiated flexibility from machining, assembly, welding, painting, outfitting, and testing processes. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach can effectively identify and aggregate process-level adjustment potential, achieving approximately 15% peak shaving and 10% improvement in resource utilization without disrupting production continuity. These findings confirm the feasibility of the STN-based framework in supporting low-carbon, intelligent, and resilient shipbuilding.

title: Study on Trajectory Optimization Design of Multi-pulse Long-range Guided Rocket Based on Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Zhihang Wang, Hu Ma, Zhenjuan Xia, Junjie Wu
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: To obtain the optimized trajectory of multi-pulse rockets, a jumping gliding trajectory is adopted to increase their range. An energy distribution optimization model for multi-pulse rockets is established, with range as the optimization objective. Optimization variables and constraints are selected based on relevant experience. A calculation program is developed based on the particle swarm optimization algorithm for solving. The influence of engine pulse energy distribution and gliding segment control angle of attack on rocket trajectory characteristics is analyzed. The influence law of gliding segment control angle of attack on flight trajectory is obtained, and the selection principle of gliding segment control angle of attack is determined. The results show that the particle swarm algorithm can effectively solve the energy distribution optimization problem of multi-pulse rockets.

title: Multiple Timescales Coordinated Rolling Scheduling Strategy for Hybrid Energy Storage Systems Considering Dynamically Constrained Time Windows
authors: Ziqi Xu, Yang Lei, Shichuan Ding, Bin Xu, Zeyi Li, Hongliang Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This study proposes a coordinated rolling scheduling strategy across multiple timescales with dynamic constraint windows for a hybrid energy storage system composed of different storage technologies, aiming to extend system lifetime, mitigate dispatch deviations, and reduce costs. For power-type storage, a dynamic constraint time window is adaptively determined from the device response time and the extreme-point statistics within the wind-power forecast window; dynamically adjusting this window enables full use of its wind-smoothing capability. Given the disparity in dynamic response capabilities among power-type, energy-type, and capacity-type, we propose a coordinated rolling scheduling method across multiple timescales that enables these storage types to cooperate and fully leverage their respective strengths. In real-time dispatch, the dynamic window together with deviation tracking reduces scheduling deviations. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method achieves enhanced coordination among different energy storage systems, extended service life, reduced costs, and significantly smoother wind-power fluctuations while performing peak shaving and valley filling.

title: Maximum Lyapunov Exponent-Guided Semi-Supervised Learning for Short-Term Voltage Stability Case Labeling
authors: Chao Deng, Hefeng Zhai, Chang Liu, Baoye Tian, Pengwei Sun, Hao Yuan, Zexiang Zhu, Lipeng Zhu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Machine learning (ML)-based short-term voltage stability (STVS) assessment has recently become a research hotspot in power system stability analysis due to its superior performance. Such methods generally rely on a large number of labeled training cases. However, STVS case labeling in practical systems are mostly performed via fixed threshold-based criteria, where instability is declared if bus voltages remain below a threshold over a preset time window. Although simple to implement, such practical criteria built upon empirical threshold setting may be lack of adaptability and reliability when applied to different operating scenarios. To address this issue, this paper proposes a semi-supervised learning-based automatic STVS case labeling approach with no reliance on domain expertise. Specifically, the approach first utilizes the maximum Lyapunov exponent (MLE) method with solid theoretical foundations to perform preliminary STVS analysis, which enables the automatic labeling of distinctly stable and unstable cases, with critical cases near the stability boundaries left as unlabeled data. Then, taking the labels obtained via MLE analysis as the supervised information, a mean teacher-based semi-supervised learning scheme is developed to capture primary stability characteristics of those unlabeled critical cases. Through iterative updates of the teacher model parameters, it achieves efficient and reliable automatic labeling of STVS cases. Finally, case analyses on a four-bus test system and a realistic provincial power grid in China validate that the proposed approach is capable of efficiently and reliably labeling STVS cases, thus providing high-quality learning data for the development of ML-based STVS assessment schemes.

title: A system generation-shedding strategy that considers water hammer effects of hydro units
authors: Yiying Gao, Yilang Jiang, Wenchen Zhang, Jian Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the advancement of the "West-to-East Power Transmission" strategy, the proportion of hydropower in the southwestern region has significantly increased, and power reversals caused by water hammer effects in hydro units pose a serious threat to system frequency stability. Existing studies mostly focus on mechanism analysis and control optimization under normal conditions and lack emergency control strategies that consider the dynamic characteristics of water hammer after transient faults. This paper analyzes the system frequency response mechanisms for large power deficit faults such as DC blocking, reveals the shortcomings of traditional generation-shedding strategies that ignore water hammer–induced power reversals, and proposes a system generation-shedding strategy that takes into account the dynamic process of water hammer effects. Simulation results show that the proposed strategy can effectively smooth frequency fluctuations and improve transient frequency stability characteristics of high-hydropower-penetration systems sending power via DC links.

title: Research on Adaptive and Robust Enhancement Methods for Generative Large Models in New Power Systems
authors: Ying Liu, Sai Zhang, Xiao Liang, Hao Liu, Yingqiang Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: As new power systems evolve toward high-penetration renewable energy integration and intelligent control, traditional models face limitations in real-time forecasting, decision support, and emergency response. Generative large models excel in semantic understanding and content generation but encounter challenges in knowledge transfer, insufficient generalization, and low robustness within power scenarios. This study proposes methods to enhance the adaptability and robustness of generative large models for power systems. By constructing a power knowledge graph and semantic embeddings, domain-specific knowledge is injected into the model. Combining adversarial training, structural perturbations, and confidence calibration improves robustness against noise, anomalous inputs, and adversarial attacks. Across three experimental scenarios—load forecasting, emergency strategy generation, and fault report generation—the proposed model significantly outperforms baseline models in prediction error, generation quality, consistency retention rate, and robustness metrics. These findings can be applied to intelligent dispatch assistance, emergency response, and smart operations and maintenance, providing technical support for the safe, intelligent, and efficient operation of new power systems.

title: A Multi-Source Data Fusion Approach for Wind Speed Calculation Based on Meta-Learning
authors: Ruijie Wu, Li Li, Shuwen Yang, Yongqian Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Wind resource assessment constitutes a critical phase in wind farm development, wherein the micro-siting stage necessitates positioning wind turbines at locations with superior wind resources to enhance the overall economic viability of the wind farm. Consequently, the temporal wind speed at hub height at the proposed turbine locations serves as the pivotal parameter determining the accuracy of wind resource evaluations. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) methods are typically employed to simulate the flow field within a wind farm, thereby obtaining the temporal wind speeds at various points within the site. However, factors such as terrain processing and boundary conditions can lead to lower accuracy in CFD numerical simulations of wind speeds, subsequently affecting the precision of wind resource assessments for the area. Where multiple meteorological masts exist within a wind farm, integrating observed data from these masts with mesoscale model outputs from the Wind Research Forum (WRF) and CFD-simulated wind speeds can significantly enhance the accuracy of simulated time-series wind speeds. This approach thereby improves the precision of regional wind resource assessments. This paper proposes a multi-source data fusion wind speed calculation method based on meta-learning. By integrating observation data from wind measurement masts, CFD-simulated wind speed data, and mesoscale model outputs, it simulates the temporal wind speed at hub height for target turbine siting locations. This provides a reliable data foundation for subsequent micro-siting work within wind farms.

title: Comparative Analysis of Modulation Methods for Optimizing the Performance of Single-Phase Full-Bridge Inverters
authors: K. Shalini, A. Lakshmi Devi
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: In this research, several pulse width modulation (PWM) methods utilized in single-phase inverters are compared. There are two inverter circuit cases examined. Case i: Using a variety of PWM approaches, including saw-tooth, random, square, and sinusoidal pulse width modulation (SPWM), an analysis is conducted for an inverter circuit with a constant resistive load. Case ii: total harmonic distortion (THD) of voltage and current is verified using an analysis based on the traditional PWM approach with resistive inductive loads. Use of the MATLAB/SIMULINK tool is used to validate the simulation. The results demonstrate that the random PWM approach outperforms other methods in terms of performance.

title: A Correlation-based Study on Ingredients of Popular Food of Indigenous People of North East and Establishment of Linkage with Ayurveda
authors: Manthan Ghosh, Sushanta Das, Smita Majumder, Swanirbhar Majumder
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This work tries to explore the correlation-based study on ingredients of popular food of indigenous people of northeast India (primarily the state of Tripura) and the establishment of linkage with Ayurveda. This can bring new insights into human health and wellness if the correlation of indigenous food habits of the mongoloid tribes of the northeast region can be established with the ancient traditional Ayurveda-inspired food and food habits. It has explored a few popular food items of the Northeastern region (of Tripura/Assam/Manipur/Meghalaya) and has tried to break them up based on their nutrition contents and also discovered the origins of this culinary culture and investigated which populations consumed specific types of cuisine. In the future, a cross-correlation with their ayurvedic counterparts with similar nutrition contents can be established. If this work can be further expanded and the whole of north-east is covered under the aegis of development of Indian Knowledge System (IKS) it will benefit India in particular and the global population as well.

title: A Preventive Scheduling Framework for Cyber-Physical Power Systems Against Extreme Weather Events
authors: Zhengping Chen, Yujie Sheng, Haitao Xu, Duanhong Zhang, Bijing Liu, Zhaoguang Pan
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Modern power systems are typical cyber-physical systems (CPS) characterized by deep integration of the cyber and physical sides. Extreme weather events pose significant threats to the security of CPS by simultaneously attacking both cyber and physical sides through damage to optical fiber composite overhead ground wire (OPGW). Furthermore, the inherent interdependencies between the two sides can exacerbate system vulnerability. In order to enhance CPS resilience against extreme weathers, this paper proposes an integrated preventive scheduling framework. The adopted preventive scheduling strategies include unit commitment, pre-dispatch load shedding and communication routing. Cyber-physical interactions are explicitly modeled in an optimization-friendly form. The framework is mathematically formulated as an adaptive robust optimization (ARO) problem with decision-dependent uncertainty (DDU). An efficient algorithm based on column-and-constraint generation (C&CG) is designed. Case studies on modified IEEE 30-bus system verify the efficacy of the proposed framework and algorithm.

title: Correlation Analysis of Direct Current of the Transformer and Noise Based on Granger Causality Test
authors: Yiwen Li, Xin Mei, Ling Ruan, Wanting Deng, Yibo Cui, Wei Rao, Shanshan Ke, Zeyang Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of abnormal noise in grid transformers caused by stray currents from rail transit systems by proposing a correlation analysis method between stray current variations and intrinsic transformer noise. The methodology employs granger causality testing to quantitatively analyze the impact of time-domain variations in stray current DC bias on transformer acoustic characteristics. Using measured data of stray current DC bias and transformer noise, this paper investigated correlations during two distinctive operational periods. Additionally, transformer load and noise were analyzed to evaluate load-dependent effects. Results demonstrate that transformer noise levels during rail transit operation periods are significantly higher than during non-operation periods, and that stray current DC bias constitutes the primary contributing factor compared to transformer load variations. The proposed framework offers practical support for condition monitoring and risk early warning systems of transformers operating in urban rail transit environments.

title: Resilience Assessment Method for Distribution-Microgrid Collaborative System Under Extreme Natural Disasters
authors: Xinyu Wu, Kefei Yan, Linbo Fu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Aiming at the challenge of quantifying the resilience of distribution networks with microgrids under extreme natural disasters, this study focuses on the emergency response capability of the distribution-microgrid collaborative system during disasters as the core. It proposes relevant resilience assessment indicators by considering the self-healing capability brought by microgrid support and the system's own network reconfiguration under disasters. To address the privacy protection needs of distributed energy resources in microgrids, a k-order approximation method is adopted to establish an operationally feasible region model for the flexible operation resources of microgrids. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can reasonably and effectively evaluate the self-healing capability of distribution networks under extreme natural disasters.

title: FPGA-Based Comparative Analysis of RoBA with Conventional Multipliers
authors: Manisha Meghani, Dileep Gangavath, Nikhil Guguloth, Anuraj V, Preeti Verma, Dhandapani Vaithiyanathan
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: This paper provides a comprehensive analysis and FPGA implementation of the Rounding-Based Approximate (RoBA) multiplier. RoBA substitutes full-precision multiplication with operand rounding to the nearest power of two, then utilizes shift-and-add operations. We implemented the unsigned RoBA design on a Xilinx Artix-7 AC701 evaluation board and evaluated key metrics such as slice utilization, critical-path delay, power, area-delay product (ADP), and power-delay product (PDP) for operand widths of 8, 16, and 32 bits. Results indicate that, in comparison to the exact multipliers like Array, Wallace, Dadda, Baugh-Wooley, and BA-WA multipliers, RoBA achieves a reduction of up to 74.2% in slice count, a speedup of 73.4%, power savings of 40%, a 93.1% decrease in ADP, and an 84.1% reduction in PDP, while introducing a bounded, predictable error of less than 0.5 LSB. The findings suggest that RoBA provides an advantageous balance among hardware simplicity, energy efficiency, and performance, making it suitable for resourcelimited, real-time image-processing systems.

title: Soft Robotic Technological Probe for Speculative Fashion Futures
authors: Amy Ingold, Loong Yi Lee, Richard Suphapol Diteesawat, Ajmal Roshan, Yael Zekaria, Edith-Clare Hall, Enrico Werner, Nahian Rahman, Elaine Czech, Jonathan M Rossiter
publish: ACM Transactions on Human-Robot Interaction

title: HGA2-FSL: A Heterogeneous Graph Aware-Aggregation Driven Few-Shot Learning Network for Hyperspectral Image Change Detection
authors: Siyao Li, Xiaohan Xie, Xiaoyang Zhao, Xianghai Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral image (HSI) provides reliable data support for repeated observations of the overlap area through fine spectral reflectance data from numerous bands, enabling accurate capture of regional variation characteristics. However, the vast spectral and spatial information in HSI makes manual annotation laborious and complex. Practical applications thus demand models that achieve high performance with minimal labeled training data. Pay attention to this practical need, we propose a heterogeneous graph aware-aggregation driven few-shot learning (FSL) network (HGA2-FSL). This network enhances model representation through flexible graph construction and aims to improve the accuracy of hyperspectral change detection (CD) under limited labeling by transferring change priors learned from other images. Specifically, we first design a correlation aggregation module to build multi-level graph structures that incorporate various types of geographic feature relationships, promoting effective capture and aware-aggregation of local and long-range information. Additionally, through joint FSL on very high-resolution image (VHRI) and HSI, the method utilizes the abundant labeled samples from VHRI and transfers learnable information, reducing the need for labeled samples of HSI. Meanwhile, we design a cross-domain node update-based domain adaptation method to explore a subspace that minimizes distribution differences, thereby reducing the modality gap between VHRI and HSI and improving the transfer learning performance. Extensive experiments on three public HSI-CD datasets validate the superior performance of HGA2-FSL. The code will be made publicly available at:https://github.com/lsylnnu/HGA2-FSL.

title: Editorial: How to Make It Into a Peer-Reviewed Journal? Some Basic Recommendations That Can Make a Difference
authors: Alexander Brem
publish: IEEE Engineering Management Review

title: Limit-Cycle Fiber-Laser Sensing via Optical-Loss-Induced Frequency Conversion
authors: Baoliang Liao, Qinlang Yuan, Chao Song, Yuan Cao, Li-Peng Sun, Yanyan Zhi, Jiejun Zhang, Xinhuan Feng, Bai-Ou Guan, Jianping Yao
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Conventional fiber sensors that rely on intensity modulation often suffer from power fluctuations and reduced signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), which limit their measurement precision. Here, we present a new sensing paradigm based on limit cycle dynamics in a passively Q-switched fiber laser (QSFL), which converts optical loss into measurable frequency shifts through modulation of the oscillation period. The demonstrated QSFL exhibits a stable limit cycle with a 59.43 μs period (16.92 kHz), enabling loss-induced changes to be read out as frequency variations with Hertz-level precision. By incorporating loss sensitive elements, such as a fiber coil and a tapered microfiber, we realize quantitative and high-SNR measurements of rotation angle, curvature, and refractive index. Beat-note linewidth of 5 Hz provides an exceptional figure of merit (FOM) and long-term stability of 16.84 Hz/h. The achieved resolutions are 0.02° over a 20° linear angle range, 4.46×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup> m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup> for curvature (0∼0.25 m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>), and the FOM is 2.42×10⁴ for refractive index (1.33∼1.36 RIU). These results establish QSFL limit cycle as a compact and versatile platform that integrates ultra-precise frequency readout, intrinsic noise suppression, and multi-parameter fiber sensing.

title: Bridging the Reality Gap in ASR for Low-Resource Dysarthric Speech: Evaluating Performance on Synthetic and Real Data_supp1-3673388.pdf
authors: Bozena Kostek

title: Haptic-Assisted Magnetic Navigation of Microswarm for Targeted Delivery in Dynamic Fluidic Environments_supp1-3673070.mp4
authors: Qianqian WANG
publish: N/A
abstract: Microswarms face challenges in precise delivery within dynamic biological fluids due to fluid disturbances and limited operational intuitiveness. Current approaches insufficiently utilize the operator’s perceptual awareness and interactive decision-making capabilities, particularly in complex tasks that require a balance between flexibility and precision. In this study, we propose a haptic-assisted magnetic actuation control strategy, establishing a human-in-the-loop control framework. The haptic perception system provides the operator with haptic feedback reflecting the interactions between the microswarm and the environment. A real-time tracking system monitors the position and pattern of the controlled microswarm in remote environments, and transmits this information to the control system for decision-making. After characterizing the magnetic field parameters and magnetic nanoparticles, we have achieved real-time navigation and morphology modulation of the microswarm in dynamic flow conditions and three-dimensional (3D) space. Comparative experiments under various flow rate conditions demonstrate that the haptic-assisted strategy enhances microswarm control stability and precision across different flow regimes. Moreover, the human-machine collaboration mechanism improves delivery success rates (97%) under sudden disturbances compared to preprogrammed automated control and purely manual control, validating its potential for applications in complex biomedical scenarios. Our work provides a haptic-assisted microswarm control method in dynamic conditions, expanding an adaptive microswarm control strategy in complex biomedical environments.

title: An Improved Dual-Antenna GNSS-IR Method for BDS-Based High-Elevation Soil Moisture Retrieval: Initial Results and Evaluation
authors: Nazi Wang, Yunqiao He, Zhenlong Fang, Lili Jing, Fan Gao, Yang Liu, Xiao Zhang, Tianhe Xu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: GNSS Interferometric Reflectometry (GNSS-IR) has been proven effective for soil moisture monitoring by leveraging the interference effects between direct and reflected signals at low elevation angles. However, at high elevation angles, the polarization of reflected signals shifts to left-handed circular polarization (LHCP), which cannot be captured by right-handed circular polarization (RHCP) antennas—thus rendering conventional GNSS-IR technology ineffective. To develop more robust solutions for harnessing high-elevation GNSS data, this study proposes an improved GNSS-IR configuration that integrates two geodetic GNSS receivers, one upward-looking RHCP antenna, one downward-looking LHCP antenna, and two power couplers for soil moisture retrieval. This optimized setup enables the acquisition and utilization of high-elevation GNSS signals for GNSS-IR-based soil moisture estimation. To validate the proposed method, an observational campaign was conducted at a farmland site. Analyses of the amplitude and phase measurements derived from BDS reflected signals indicate that soil moisture solutions retrieved from high-elevation signals achieve accuracy comparable to those obtained from low-elevation signals. Specifically, the maximum correlation coefficient between retrievals and in-situ data reaches 0.95, and the mean RMSE across all frequencies is no greater than 0.02 m³ /m³. Overall, this method maximizes the utilization efficiency of received GNSS signals and provides a more comprehensive detection range compared with conventional GNSS-IR method.

title: AAAC 2025 Cover Page
authors: N/A

title: Global Modeling Matters: A Fast, Lightweight and Effective Baseline for Efficient Image Restoration_supp1-3671691.pdf
authors: Xingyu Jiang
publish: N/A
abstract: Natural image quality is often degraded by adverse weather conditions, significantly impairing the performance of downstream tasks. Image restoration has emerged as a core solution to this challenge and has been widely discussed in the literature. Although recent transformer-based approaches have made remarkable progress in image restoration, their increasing system complexity poses significant challenges for real-time processing, particularly in real-world deployment scenarios. To this end, most existing methods attempt to simplify the self-attention mechanism, such as by channel self-attention or state space model. However, these methods primarily focus on network architecture while neglecting the inherent characteristics of image restoration itself. In this context, we explore a pyramid Wavelet-Fourier iterative pipeline to demonstrate the potential of Wavelet-Fourier processing for image restoration. Inspired by the above findings, we propose a novel and efficient restoration baseline, named Pyramid Wavelet-Fourier Network (PW-FNet). Specifically, PW-FNet features two key design principles: 1) at the inter-block level, integrates a pyramid wavelet-based multi-input multi-output structure to achieve multi-scale and multi-frequency bands decomposition; and 2) at the intra-block level, incorporates Fourier transforms as an efficient alternative to self-attention mechanisms, effectively reducing computational complexity while preserving global modeling capability. Extensive experiments on tasks such as image deraining, raindrop removal, image super-resolution, motion deblurring, image dehazing, image desnowing and underwater/low-light enhancement demonstrate that PW-FNet not only surpasses state-of-the-art methods in restoration quality but also achieves superior efficiency, with significantly reduced parameter size, computational cost and inference time. The code is available at: https://github.com/deng-ai-lab/PW-FNet.

title: The Analogue of JK Flip-flop Functions through An Electromagnetic Logic Metastructure Based on Coherent Near-perfect Absorption_supp1-3671383.doc
authors: Feng Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Based on the interference of electromagnetic (EM) waves, an electromagnetic logic metastructure (ELM) with the graphene layers (GLs) is proposed to emulate the functionality of a conventional JK flip-flop. When two coherent EM waves are simultaneously incident on the ELM from opposite directions, a coherent near-perfect absorption (CNPA) peak can be generated within the frequency range of 12~13.8 terahertz (THz), where the phase difference between the coherent waves is denoted as ΔΦ. If the value of the CNPA peak exceeds 0.55, the output level F of the ELM is defined as ‘1’; otherwise, it is defined as ‘0’. With the assistance of the ΔΦ, the chemical potential (μC) of the GLs and the incident angle (θ) are utilized as the input signals corresponding to the J and K inputs of the JK flip-flop, which can effectively simulate JK flip-flop functions: set function, clear function, toggle, and no-change. Therefore, by appropriately configuring the logical parameters ΔΦ, μC, and θ, the proposed ELM can effectively emulate the part functions of a JK flip-flop, offering a promising approach for enhancing the capabilities of EM logic devices.

title: LECF: A Lightweight Edge-Aware and Context-Gated Feature Fusion Network for Multiscale UAV Object Detection
authors: Zhisuo Sun, Yanika Kongsorot, Pakarat Musikawan, Phet Aimtongkham, Chakchai So-In
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) object detection remains challenging because of the substantial scale variations, frequent small objects, and complex backgrounds present in aerial imagery. In this paper, LECF, a lightweight edge-aware and context-gated feature fusion network designed for multiscale UAV detection, is presented. The network integrates three modules: (1) a multiscale edge feature fusion-enhanced module (MEFFEM) that strengthens edge cues and nonlinear feature representations; (2) a lightweight efficient upsampling-based multibranch feature pyramid network (LEMFPN) that is equipped with an efficient upsampling and spatial shifting (EUSS) component to enhance the cross-scale semantic aggregation process; and (3) a selective gate-based convolution enhancement module (SGCE) that refines feature responses by suppressing redundant activations. When evaluated on VisDrone and SeaDronesSee, compared with YOLOv11s, the AP and AP50 of the LECF model are improved by 2.5% and 3.4% on VisDrone, and by 2.4% and 2.0% on SeaDronesSee, respectively, while the number of required parameters is reduced by 28.7%. The LECF model also provides consistent improvements across multiscale categories and maintains comparable computational complexity, offering a practical lightweight alternative for use in UAV scenarios.

title: An Attention–Enhanced CNN–BiLSTM Model for Speech Assessment of Hard-of-Hearing Speakers
authors: Sandeep Chandran, Jesna Mohan, S Sreejith, N R Raji, A V Anjana
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Speech recognition systems work well for people who can hear normally; however, recognizing speech from hard-of-hearing speakers remains challenging due to limited datasets and distinct articulation characteristics. The proposed model uses a hybrid deep learning model using a convolutional neural network, a bi-directional long short–term memory network, and an attention mechanism for HoH speech recognition. Log–Mel spectrograms, along with their first-order (delta) and second–order (delta–delta) derivatives, are employed for the input features to represent spectral dynamics and the changes over time. To test the proposed method, a HoH speech dataset was developed with seventeen words taken from the standard word list developed by the All India Institute of Speech and Hearing, Mysore. Ten volunteers from the National Institute of Speech and Hearing, Kerala, participated in dataset creation. The model was further validated on the public Google Speech Commands dataset due to the absence of a standard HoH benchmark. The result of the experiment shows that the proposed model has an average accuracy of 95.20% and an F1-score of 0.95 for recognizing HoH speech effectively. The results show that the inclusion of temporal derivative features into an attention-based CNN-BiLSTM model results in a robust solution for assistive speech recognition systems.

title: Adaptive Cell Orientation Control With Slip Compensation and Attentive Vision
authors: Ziteng Liu, Haoyuan Gu, Yifan Huang, Wenyuan Chen, Hang Liu, Honglin Zhang, Songlin Zhuang, Changsheng Dai
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Precise orientation of oocyte is a prerequisite for many cell manipulations, especially Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection (ICSI), to prevent damage to the meiotic spindle. However, automating this process presents significant challenges due to the visual ambiguity of the polar body and the complex friction dynamics between the micropipette and the cell. In this letter, we propose a robotic system for high-precision oocyte rotation. First, we introduce a YOLO11-based detector enhanced with a high-resolution branch P2 and CBAM (Convolutional Block Attention Module) attention, achieving robust polar body detection (mAP@50 = 96.7% on 1028 images). Second, we develop a discrete-contour mechanics model that estimates minimum rotational torque for cell geometries, replacing traditional circle/ellipse assumptions. Finally, we design a dual-loop control framework that combines slip-compensated rotation with adaptive indentation regulation, enabling real-time compensation for biological variability of cells. Experiments on mouse oocytes show 0.51° orientation error with a maximum deformation (2.58 μm), outperforming PID-based baselines.

title: Bridging the Depth Gap: Adaptive Scene-Instance Alignment for Training-Free Depth Refinement in Robotic Manipulation Scenes
authors: Pihong Hou, Yongfang Zhang, Boxuan Ye, Yi Wu
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Monocular depth estimation foundation models provide robust depth priors with exceptional generalization capabilities; however, their predictions typically lack a reliable metric scale and contain local inconsistencies in a zero-shot setting, which limit their deployment in unconstrained real-world environments. Meanwhile, sensor-based depth measurements are typically sparse or noisy, especially in challenging scenarios such as transparent objects or cluttered robotic scenes. To address this limitation, we propose a training-free adaptive scene-instance alignment framework that refines pseudo-depth maps using sparse ground-truth samples without any additional training or fine-tuning. Our approach integrates three stages of alignment: (1) a global affine transformation via Theil–Sen regression; (2) multi-scale local corrections guided by Gaussian, color-similarity, and gradient-aware weights; and (3) an optional instance-level residual refinement driven by segmentation guidance. Extensive experiments on robotic grasping datasets (Linemod-Occluded, YCB-Video, HOPE, Wild6D) and transparent-object datasets (ClearPose, TransCG) show consistent improvements across evaluation metrics, including root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and thresholded accuracy <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The results demonstrate that our method reliably bridges the gap between monocular priors and sensor ground truth, generating pixel-wise metric depth maps with strong structural fidelity that are well-suited for open-world perception tasks.

title: Bioinspired Agile Swimming with a Highly Underactuated Soft Ribbon Fin
authors: Iman Adibnazari, Suyeon Jeong, Michael T. Tolley
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Soft autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) that employ biological swimming mechanisms have demonstrated immense promise in navigating marine environments with increased efficiency and adaptability and decreased environmental disturbances in comparison to their rigid counterparts. However, most AUVs with soft propulsors are poorly suited for precise swimming at low speeds and station holding tasks—two capabilities critical to underwater monitoring and manipulation. In contrast, gymnotid fish undulate an elongated ribbon fin to aptly swim at low-speeds with high agility and have inspired soft AUVs that mimic their swimming mechanics. However, these AUVs typically use numerous actuators to force undulatory kinematics in their ribbon fin rather than leveraging the fluid-structure interaction between the soft ribbon fin and surrounding water. In this work, we developed a sparsely actuated robot, inspired by the black ghost knifefish (apteronotus albifrons) and capable of highly agile swimming with only two actuated fin rays. Leveraging prior work on the hydrodynamics of ribbon-finned swimming, we constructed a heuristic for expected thrust generation as a function of the ribbon fin's actuation. Using this heuristic, we investigated four parameterized motion primitives for agile in-plane swimming: 1) forward swimming, 2) banked turning maneuvers, 3) pure yaw rotation, and 4) lateral (i.e., sideways) swimming. We developed a single parametric gait to express all of these primitives and experimentally characterized each primitive. Ultimately, we found that by leveraging fluid-structure interaction, our highly underactuated platform could execute the in-plane swimming capabilities necessary for agile locomotion.

title: Nonparametric Bellman Mappings for Value Iteration in Distributed Reinforcement Learning_supp1-3671726.pdf
authors: Konstantinos Slavakis
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper introduces novel Bellman mappings (BMaps) for value iteration (VI) in distributed reinforcement learning (DRL), where agents are deployed over an undirected, connected graph/network with arbitrary topology—but without a centralized node, that is, a node capable of aggregating all data and performing computations. Each agent constructs a nonparametric B-Map from its private data, operating on Qfunctions represented in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, with flexibility in choosing the basis for their representation. Agents exchange their Q-function estimates only with direct neighbors, and unlike existing DRL approaches that restrict communication to Q-functions, the proposed framework also enables the transmission of basis information in the form of covariance matrices, thereby conveying additional structural details. Linear convergence rates are established for both Q-function and covariance-matrix estimates toward their consensus values, regardless of the network topology, with optimal learning rates determined by the ratio of the smallest positive eigenvalue (the graph’s Fiedler value) to the largest eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian matrix. A detailed performance analysis further shows that the proposed DRL framework effectively approximates the performance of a centralized node, had such a node existed. Numerical tests on distributed extensions of three benchmark control problems confirm the effectiveness of the proposed nonparametric B-Maps relative to state-of-the-art methods. Notably, the tests reveal a counter-intuitive outcome: although the framework involves richer information exchange—specifically, through the transmission of covariance matrices as information on the basis of a subspace—it achieves the desired performance at a lower cumulative communication cost than several state-of-the-art DRL schemes, underscoring the critical role of sharing basis information in accelerating the learning process.
