Through iterative "strategy-price" feedback and mandatory adjustment of grid parameters, the collaborative optimization problem of shared energy storage configuration and market mechanisms is resolved. Finally, case studies demonstrate that the proposed optimization configuration scheme can effectively enhance the capacity for renewable energy consumption, reduce wind and solar curtailment, and improve the operational economics of both the shared energy storage station and renewable energy plants.

title: Identification of Key Influencing Factors in Power System Transient Stability Assessment Based on Causal Inference
authors: Licheng Sha, Liping Yang, Shujun Cao, Tianying Wei, Xinyue Zhao, Xinchen Zhao, Yifei Sun, Qinglai Guo
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Transient stability assessment (TSA) is a critical task for ensuring the secure operation of modern power systems. One of the most importamt task in TSA is to identify the key factors influencing transient stability. Traditional methods, such as Pearson correlation and mRMR, often neglect the causal relationships among variables, leading to limited interpretability and reduced robustness. In this work, we propose a novel key factor identification method based on mutual information and causal inference, termed MI-CI. The method first employs mutual information to capture nonlinear dependencies and select candidate features, and then applies the PC algorithm to construct causal graphs and identify causally relevant features for stability margin evaluation. This integration not only considers statistical relevance but also ensures that the extracted features reflect the underlying causal mechanisms of power system dynamics. Case studies on the IEEE 39-bus system and a practical Chinese 300-bus system validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Compared with Pearson correlation and mRMR, the MI-CI method achieves higher sensitivity in detecting critical factors and provides factors that align more closely with system topology. These results demonstrate that incorporating causal inference enhances both the accuracy and interpretability of TSA, offering a promising direction for robust and explainable stability analysis in complex power systems.

title: Mechanism Analysis of the Impact of Inertia Control Parameters on the Mechanical Dynamics of Direct-Drive Permanent Magnet Wind Turbines
authors: Zixuan Wang, Fei Li, Bo Wang, Xueyi Fu, Xiangshuo Meng, Yansen Jiao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Direct-drive permanent magnet synchronous wind turbines (PMSGs) have been widely applied due to their high efficiency and reliability. However, under maximum power point tracking (MPPT) control, the rotor speed is decoupled from the grid frequency, making it difficult to provide effective inertial support. This paper investigates the impact mechanism of inertia control parameters on the mechanical dynamic characteristics of direct-drive PMSGs. An electromechanical coupling model and a simplified shaft system model are established to analyze the effects of virtual inertia control parameters on the dynamic response, torsional vibration characteristics, and stability of the drive train. Furthermore, the coupling relationship between inertia parameters and mechanical system stability is explored from the frequency-domain perspective. The results demonstrate that virtual inertia parameters not only influence the grid’s inertial support capability but also have a significant impact on the shaft system stability of the wind turbine.

title: Consider Adaptive Differential Protection for Active Distribution Networks with Non-measurable Branch Access
authors: Lei Huang, Gengshen Tian, Lanhong Zhang, Changsheng Jiao, Ruilin Ma, Hong Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To mitigate the impact of connecting non-measurable load branches to active distribution networks with distributed generation on traditional current differential protection schemes, an adaptive current differential protection scheme is proposed. Firstly, the characteristics of the amplitude differences of the currents on both sides of the unmeasurable branch lines in the active distribution network under normal operation and different fault scenarios within and outside the zone were analyzed. This ratio was used to change the braking coefficient of the differential protection in real time. Finally, through simulation verification, it is found that the proposed scheme can effectively enhance the sensitivity of protection actions when faults occur outside the zone under the condition of unmeasurable load branch connection, while ensuring reliable non-action when faults occur outside the zone. Moreover, it has strong resistance to transition resistance, and has strong practical engineering significance.

title: Aerodynamic Vibration and Suppression Methods of Aircraft
authors: Cui Yuan, Ruojun Zhou, Yanjun Zhang, Bingyao Dou, Zimo Huo, Lu Xie
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: This paper employs numerical simulation methods to study the aerodynamic vibration and its suppression of aircraft. Using the CHN-T1 standard model as a foundation, this study employs unsteady CFD methods to compare and analyze the effects of adding bulges on overall aerodynamic forces. Through spectral analysis and flow field studies, it explores the mechanisms by which bulges cause aircraft vibrations. Additionally, it investigates controlling the flow in the wake separation region behind the bulges by installing flow splitter plates. Simulation results indicate that the flow splitter plates have a significant suppression effect on oscillations in the wake.

title: Transmission Line Defect Detection Based on Improved YOLOv11
authors: Bo Chen, HongYu Zhang, Kang An, ZeXu Du, QiZhe Zhang, GuoZheng Peng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To address feature confusion, poor multi-scale defect recognition accuracy, and small-target missed detection in transmission line defect detection under complex scenarios—while enhancing intelligent inspection reliability and efficiency—this study proposes an improved YOLOv11-based detection method. First, the OverLoCK network replaces YOLOv11’s original backbone. Via the Deep Stage Decomposition (DDS) strategy and Context-Mixing Dynamic Convolution (ContMix), it strengthens global-local feature correlation of defects in complex backgrounds and improves feature extraction pertinence. Second, the C3K2-LSConv feature fusion module is designed, integrating LS convolution’s large-kernel global perception and small-kernel local focusing into the original C3K2 module to optimize multi-scale (especially small-target) defect feature expression. Finally, the MPDIoU (Minimum Point Distance Intersection over Union) loss function is introduced; by optimizing vertex distance between predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes, it resolves traditional CIoU loss’s sensitivity to position errors in small-target localization. Experiments on a self-built dataset (3960 images, 6 target/defect categories) show that compared with original YOLOv11, the proposed method achieves mAP50=0.887 and mAP50-95=0.538, with significant gains in precision and localization accuracy. Ablation experiments verify the collaborative optimization of OverLoCK, C3K2-LSConv, and MPDIoU, proving the method effectively meets high-precision transmission line defect detection needs.

title: Stray Current Correlation Analysis: A Case Study of a Specific Power Supply Area
authors: Zhuang Ruan, Zeyang Tang, Ling Ruan, Xin Mei, XueLi Pan, Gang Li, Zhen Gao, Xi Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To analyze the correlation of urban rail transit stray currents monitored at substations, this study collected monitoring data from six substations in a power supply area covering the period from August 1 to August 31, 2024. Correlation analyses were conducted at multiple time scales, including typical days, multiple days, and hourly intervals. The results indicate that substations CJZ, HP, and SMT exhibit a strong positive correlation with substation TYG, while substation QR shows a significant negative correlation with TYG. Through FFT analysis, it was found that the low correlation between substation GJW and the other substations may be attributed to the intrusion of external stray currents.

title: A Study on Data Modeling and Analysis in the Energy and Power Sector Based on Business Logic
authors: Wang Yinan, Chen Xingtong, Huang Bibin, Meng Heli
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the widespread application of smart grid, distributed energy, and electric vehicle technologies, the volume of data generated by energy and power systems has experienced explosive growth, accompanied by increasingly complex data types, including structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data. This study proposes a business logic-based approach to data modeling and analysis in the energy and power sector, breaking the traditional bottleneck of disconnection between data and business processes and enabling their collaborative development. By systematically mapping energy and power business workflows, clarifying data requirements and logical relationships at each stage, and constructing data models aligned with actual business needs, this approach achieves efficient data organization and management, thereby providing a solid foundation for data analysis.

title: Short-term power load forecasting for polar regions based on the SSA-CNN-BILSTM-ATTENTION model
authors: Limin Yang, Xingtao Tian, Qinyong He, Yixun Xue, Xinyue Chang, Hongbin Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Polar power systems face unique challenges due to extreme climate conditions, limited resources, and strong fluctuations in demand. Accurate short-term load forecasting is essential for maintaining stability and optimizing renewable energy utilization in such isolated grids. This paper proposes an Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA)- Convolutional neural network Model (CNN)-Bi-directional-Long Short – Term •Memory-Model (BILSTM)-Attention model that integrates CNN is used for extracting local features, BILSTM for long-term temporal dependencies, and an attention mechanism for key feature enhancement. The SSA is employed to optimize model hyperparameters, improving adaptability under small-sample and high-volatility scenarios. Using Belgian load data for verification, experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model significantly reduces prediction errors and outperforms conventional CNN-LSTM and CNN-BILSTM models, offering a robust solution for reliable scheduling and stable operation of polar power systems.

title: Development of Electrochemical Energy Storage Systems for Polar Environment
authors: Qiuhua Li, Bin Wang, Yu Su, Jihong Zhu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: A cryogenic electrochemical energy storage system has been specifically designed for polar environments, addressing the significant variability in wind and solar energy and the unreliability of microgrid power supplies in these harsh conditions. The system employs lithium titanate batteries, renowned for their broad temperature range, high safety, and durability, as the primary energy storage medium. These batteries are integrated with a DC/AC power conversion unit within a PACK design. This battery energy storage system, based on a cascade multilevel topology., meets the stringent safety, reliability, and efficiency requirements necessary for polar clean energy applications in extreme cold. Since its implementation, the system has operated reliably, effectively fulfilling the energy demands of China's polar scientific research activities.

title: "Should I Give Up Now?" Investigating LLM Pitfalls in Software Engineering
authors: Jiessie Tie, Bingsheng Yao, Tianshi Li, Hongbo Fang, Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed, Dakuo Wang, Shurui Zhou
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: AI Can See What You Can't See: How LLM-Agents Complement Human-Based Gender-Inclusive Usability Testing
authors: Joy Geuenich, Frank Joublin, Antonello Ceravola, Stefan Brandenburg, Jonathan Dodge
publish: ACM Transactions on Interactive Intelligent Systems
abstract: None

title: Comments on “LAS-SG: An Elliptic Curve-Based Lightweight Authentication Scheme for Smart Grid Environments”
authors: Akhtar Badshah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Lightweight authentication is essential for resource-constrained devices in smart grids. Recently, Chaudhry et al. (2023) proposed an elliptic curve cryptography-based lightweight authentication scheme for smart grids (LAS-SG), claiming to ensure both security and efficiency. This comment presents a detailed cryptographic analysis of LAS-SG and identifies a critical inconsistency in its design, namely, a misinterpretation between scalars and elliptic curve points. This nonstandard formulation compromises the protocol's correctness and undermines its claimed security guarantees. To address these issues, a corrected version of LAS-SG is proposed, rectifying the identified flaws while preserving the original goals of efficiency and mutual authentication.

title: Adversarially Optimized Multi-Space Prototypical Network for Intrusion Detection in 5G-Enabled IoT Systems
authors: Shahid Latif, Jawad Ahmad, Wadii Boulila, Djamel Djenouri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Existing state-of-the-art IDS solutions in 5G-enabled IoT, including deep convolutional architectures, are constrained by data scarcity, limited edge resources, and poor robustness in few-shot learning (FSL) scenarios with scarce attack samples. To address these limitations, we propose MSPL-IDS, a robust FSL-based intrusion detection framework tailored for 5G-enabled IoT environments. Unlike existing methods that rely on a single embedding space and are highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, MSPL-IDS employs multi-space prototypical learning to project traffic features into multiple complementary embedding spaces, generating diverse class prototypes that strengthen decision boundaries against evasion attacks. A dual-loss adversarial training strategy jointly improves classification accuracy and robustness, while an attention mechanism adaptively selects the most reliable embedding spaces during inference, enabling low-latency deployment at the network edge. Comprehensive experiments on the 5G-NIDD benchmark demonstrate that MSPL-IDS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art deep learning and FSL-based IDSs under adversarial conditions. While representative models such as ResNet and Inception experience catastrophic accuracy drops to as low as 21.2% under evasion attacks, MSPL-IDS consistently maintains detection accuracy above 87% in identical few-shot settings. On average, the proposed framework achieves accuracy gains of 31.8% under FGSM and 26.4% under PGD attacks, with improvements exceeding 54% at high perturbation levels (ϵ ≥ 0.15), while preserving near-optimal clean-data accuracy (88.2–94.9%).

title: Reconfigurable Antennas for Next-generation Mobile Communication Networks: A Comprehensive Survey and Tutorial
authors: Yizhe Zhao, Long Zhang, Halvin Yang, Kun Yang, Rui Zhang, Lingyang Song, Yuanwei Liu
publish: IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials
abstract: The transition to next-generation mobile communication networks, particularly 6G, demands advanced technologies to meet the requirements for ultra-reliable, low-latency communication, massive connectivity, and intelligent applications. Reconfigurable antennas (RAs) play a crucial role in achieving these objectives by enabling dynamic adjustments to the radio frequency (RF) characteristics of antennas, such as gain, radiation pattern, impedance, and polarization. Unlike traditional fixed-position antennas, RAs can alter both their radiation patterns and positions, offering flexibility in response to varying communication environments. This paper presents a comprehensive survey and tutorial on RAs, with a focus on fluid antennas (FAs), movable antennas (MAs), pinching antennas (PAs), and reconfigurable holographic antennas (RHAs), examining their potential in next-generation mobile networks. We explore the channel modelling and estimation, performance analysis, resource allocation strategies, and their synergy with other emerging wireless technologies for each type of RA. Finally, we provide a comparative analysis of different RAs and discuss the open challenges and future research directions, offering insights and guidance for future investigations in the exciting research area.

title: Comprehensive Assessment of LiDAR Evaluation Metrics: A Comparative Study Using Simulated and Real Data
authors: Syed Mostaquim Ali, Taufiq Rahman, Ghazal Farhani, Mohamed H. Zaki, Benoit Anctil, Dominique Charlebois
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: For developing safe Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS), rigorous testing is required before they are deemed safe for road deployments. Since comprehensive conventional physical testing is impractical due to cost and safety concerns, Virtual Testing Environments (VTE) can be adopted as an alternative. Comparing VTE-generated sensor outputs against their real-world analogues can be a strong indication that the VTE accurately represents reality. Correspondingly, this work explores a comprehensive experimental approach to finding evaluation metrics suitable for comparing real-world and simulated LiDAR scans. The metrics were tested in terms of sensitivity and accuracy with different noise, density, distortion, sensor orientation, and channel settings. From comparing the metrics, we found that Density Aware Chamfer Distance (DCD) works best across all cases. In the second step of the research, a Virtual Testing Environment was generated using real LiDAR scan data. The data was collected in a controlled environment with only static objects using an instrumented vehicle equipped with LiDAR, IMU and cameras. Simulated LiDAR scans were generated from the VTEs using the same pose as real LiDAR scans. The simulated and LiDAR scans were compared in terms of model perception and geometric similarity. Actual and simulated LiDAR scans have a similar semantic segmentation output with a mIoU of 21% with corrected intensity and an average density aware chamfer distance (DCD) of 0.63. This indicates a slight difference in the geometric properties of simulated and real LiDAR scans and a significant difference between model outputs. During the comparison, density-aware chamfer distance was found to be the most correlated among the metrics with perception methods.

title: Enabling High-Speed Taxiing Motion in a Bionic Robotic Fish Using a Compliant Passive Joint
authors: Lingchang Meng, Zhengxing Wu, Haipeng Li
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Achieving cross-medium locomotion remains a longstanding challenge in the field of bionic robotic fish. Current locomotion strategies often fail to enable effective aerial motion after water exit, primarily due to limitations in propulsion performance. To address this, we propose a novel cross-medium approach inspired by the taxiing behavior of flying fish. To validate this strategy, we developed a lightweight, high-speed robotic fish that integrates an electromechanical coupling drive system and a compliant passive joint. Based on the Lagrangian method, we established a kinetic model for the taxiing motion to investigate how passive joint stiffness and driving frequency influence taxiing performance. Extensive simulation and experimental results demonstrate that surface propulsion performance can be effectively enhanced by tuning passive joint stiffness to match driving frequency and intrinsic frequency to enter a resonant state. This enables the robotic fish to achieve a successful transition to taxiing motion at the resonant frequency, with a stable speed of 1.74 m/s (equivalent to 6.78 body lengths per second) and a lower Cost of Transport. These findings provide valuable insights for the design and optimization of robotic fish with passive joints and offer a promising pathway to realizing efficient cross-medium locomotion.

title: Illumination-Prior Guided Monochrome–Infrared Fusion for Low-Light Aerial Imaging
authors: Peiyang Lin, Junbo Wang, Liqun Lin, Xiangtao Zheng, Tiesong Zhao, Sam Kwong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The superior performance of visible-infrared image fusion in ground-level low-light imaging has driven significant advances in this field. However, aerial low-light scenes are characterized by wide illumination variations, complex textures, and motion blur, making it difficult for existing methods to simultaneously restore details, correct illumination, and integrate complementary information. To overcome these challenges, we propose an Aerial Low-light Monochrome-Infrared fusion Network (ALMIN), which pioneers the use of monochrome–infrared imaging for aerial low-light enhancement. First, we propose an illumination prior extractor that leverages local illumination priors to enhance the texture details of aerial monochrome images, effectively preventing detail loss during subsequent fusion and generating dynamic weights that capture regional illumination variations in aerial scenes. Second, we introduce prior illumination dynamic weights to guide a dual-modality fusion restorer, effectively integrating monochrome and infrared information for the reconstruction of illumination and reflectance components. Within this framework, we design a Frequency-Refined Denoising Block (FRDB), a Global Illumination Perception Block (GIPB), a Fine-Grained Reflectance Perception Block (FRPB), and a Light-Guided Fusion Block (LGFB) to achieve effective brightness correction and detail restoration. Third, we construct Aerial Low-light Fusion Set (ALFSet), the first multimodal low-light aerial image dataset captured with a monocular and an infrared stereo camera, to evaluate the proposed ALMIN. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate the superiority and effectiveness of our ALMIN. The source code and dataset will be released on https://github.com/PeiyangLin1/ALMIN.

title: ResNeXt-SqueezeViTNet: A Ubiquitous Sensing Framework for Predictive Risk Assessment in Smart City Logistics Systems
authors: Abdulwahab Ali Almazroi, Nasir Ayub
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Modern smart city logistics operate in highly unpredictable environments shaped by congestion, fluctuating demand, and diverse IoT sensing conditions. These factors produce complex, imbalanced, and temporally inconsistent data that challenge traditional predictive modeling approaches. To address these limitations, this study introduces ResNeXt-SqueezeViTNet, a hybrid residual, compressed, and attention-driven framework designed for multi-label logistics risk prediction. The architecture integrates ResNeXt grouped convolutions for structural feature extraction, SqueezeNet modules for compact multi-scale representation, and a Vision Transformer attention layer to capture long-range temporal and contextual patterns. A tailored preprocessing and feature-engineering pipeline strengthens the learning process. Clustered Adaptive Synthetic Balancing (CASB) improves minority-class representation, while the Adaptive Composite Feature Selector (ACFS) identifies critical attributes using weighted statistical divergence, density-guided enhancement, and correlation-based pruning. Additional feature enrichment from AAS and ART further enhances the input space. Experiments were conducted on 400,000 logistics records from Southern California, using strict temporal separation to avoid data leakage. The model achieved 99.0% accuracy, 98.4 F1-score, and 0.96 AUC, consistently surpassing strong baseline methods such as SafeLogFL, Hybrid TCN Transformer, FedFormer TS, and Swin GNN FusionNet. Comprehensive ablation studies, statistical analyses, and efficiency evaluations confirm the robustness and real-time suitability of the proposed framework for intelligent logistics risk forecasting in smart city environments.

title: D2DAP: Provably Secure and Efficient Drone-to-Drone Authentication Protocol Using Threshold Cryptography and PUF
authors: Krittibas Parai, Prasanta Kumar Roy, Pankaj Kumar, SK Hafizul Islam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Drone-to-Drone (D2D) communication has significant applications in many real-time monitoring and surveillance systems. However, communication security and efficiency are crucial for ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and availability in such systems. Physical security of drones is also an essential requirement, as they typically operate in hostile environments. Traditional authentication systems incorporate a trusted third party, known as a ground station, to facilitate secure communication. However, the inclusion of a third party introduces additional delays and increases overall communication latency. Moreover, this approach may lead to availability issues at the ground station if a large number of D2D communications occur simultaneously. This article presents a robust and efficient D2D authentication protocol, called D2DAP, as a countermeasure. It employs a (2, n)-threshold scheme to preserve system security and privacy. The protocol ensures the physical security of drones by integrating a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF). By eliminating third-party involvement, D2DAP significantly reduces communication delay and improves system efficiency during the D2D authentication process. The security and privacy properties of D2DAP are validated using formal, informal, and automated tool-based security analyses. Furthermore, its real-time performance is evaluated on a hardware-based model across various security levels, demonstrating remarkable results.

title: MTSNet: A Dual-Branch Geometric Feature Aggregation Network for Mapping Single Tree Stems From Terrestrial Laser Scanning Point Clouds
authors: Xijian Fan, Changwei Bi, Heng Zhang, Yanjie Li, Tardi Tjahjadi, Miaomiao Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurately mappingtree stems is essentialfor the analysis and estimation of tree parameters derived from terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds, including critical measurements such as Diameter at Breast Height (DBH), wood volume, and above-ground biomass. Traditional methods often struggle to address the variability in stem size and morphology across different tree species and face challenges in distinguishing tree stems from branches, particularly in complex structural environments. To address these challenges, we propose MTSNet, a deep learning network designed for mapping tree stems across multiple species using TLS point clouds, which incorporates the Dual-branch Hybrid Geometric Contextual Feature Extraction (HGCFE) module and the Multi-level Feature Adaptive Aggregation (MFAA) module. The HGCFE module leverages a dual-branch architecture to fully exploit local geometric relationships: one branch employs dynamic weight kernels to model the varying stem shapes across different species, while the other branch captures local mesh topology to enhance the network's ability to distinguish tree stems from branches at intersections. The MFAA module aims to adaptively fuse various types of geometric features at multiple levels, which introduce confidence scores to automatically balance the contributions of different geometric features, thereby facilitating effective multi-level feature aggregation. Experiments are conducted on TLS point cloud datasets featuring 11 tree species with diverse structures and shapes, which validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method for stem mapping across different species. Furthermore, key structural parameters related to tree stems, such as DBH, are estimated. The results demonstrate that incorporating stem mapping significantly improves the accuracy of DBH estimation.

title: Uneven Urban Expansion under National Policy: A Spatial Causal Analysis of the Belt and Road Initiative Using Remote Sensing and Difference-in-Differences Modeling
authors: Zujun Wang, Min Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: National development strategies can reshape urban land-use trajectories, but empirical evidence on how such strategies translate into spatially heterogeneous urban expansion across diverse geographic contexts remains limited. This study examines urban expansion in China’s Silk Road Economic Belt (SREB) over 2000–2023 under the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI). We integrate Landsat-derived built-up land mapping (and the Urban expansion intensity, UEI) with an inter-calibrated nighttime light (NTL) series, spatial hotspot–coldspot statistics, and a quasi-experimental difference-in-differences (DID) design to assess corridor-associated changes. Results show substantial built-up land growth across the five study provinces, with clear east–west differentiation: Jiangsu and Henan contribute the largest absolute increases, whereas Xinjiang exhibits stronger relative growth. Hotspot–coldspot analysis reveals persistent spatial inequality, with high-expansion clusters concentrated around metropolitan cores and key node–corridor systems, while extensive low-expansion clusters persist in peripheral or constrained areas. The DID estimates indicate a statistically significant post-2013 differential increase in UEI for corridor cities relative to comparable non-corridor cities, and parallel-trend and placebo diagnostics support the robustness of this finding. Methodologically, the study offers a replicable workflow that links satellite-derived land-conversion outcomes with causal-inference diagnostics and spatial clustering evidence. Conceptually, it advances “policy elasticity of urban space” as a lens to interpret heterogeneous responsiveness to state-led corridor strategies and to inform adaptive, region-specific planning in transregional development corridors.

title: Towards Non-Contact Snore Detection Using Video-Based Laryngeal Motion Analysis
authors: Keith Marlon R. Tabal, Jennifer C. Dela Cruz
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this study, we present a proof-of-concept snore-event detection system that operates on audio-free RGB videos of a throat to analyze the laryngeal-prominence motion. The pipeline integrates Eulerian Video Magnification (EVM), which enhances subtle motions, and Gunnar Farnebäck vertical optical flow, which quantifies directed displacement, followed by an adaptive detector with trimmed Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) thresholds, high/low hysteresis, and a cooldown period to prevent double counting. A self-recorded mimicry-based dataset under controlled conditions was collected from a single-participant. The video clips were anonymized and annotated at the event level, producing 200 events (100 snores and 100 non-snores), and independently validated by a licensed physician. To assess the annotation reliability, the Cohen’s Kappa coefficient was κ = 0.87 (“almost perfect agreement”), supporting the clinical plausibility of the ground truth. With respect to detection performance, the full pipeline (EVM-optical flow) achieved an accuracy of 90.5%, precision of 91.8%, recall of 89.0%, and F1-score of 90.4%. Meanwhile, Wilson scores with 95% confidence intervals were also computed for proportion metrics to reflect the uncertainty of the sample size. Moreover, an ablation study confirmed that the combined method outperformed EVM-only and optical flow-only baselines, underscoring a magnify-then-gate design that is sensitive to low-amplitude throat motion and robust against non-snore artifacts. Although limited by its single-participant design, this approach demonstrates the potential for non-contact, privacy-preserving snore detection and motivates multi-subject and clinical evaluations with future integration into video-based vital-sign monitoring for broader sleep health screening.

title: Temporal and Spatial Information Enhanced Heterogeneous Information Network Embedding
authors: Susu Zheng, Weiwei Yuan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Heterogeneous Information Network (HIN) embedding learns low-dimensional representations of nodes while preserving temporal and spatial information. Although some HIN embedding methods have been proposed, they did not fully use the temporal and spatial information, which limits the representation ability of HIN embedding. In addition, existing methods usually only consider the order of nodes in the random walk process, which cannot distinguish the different time interval information between edges. To address these issues, we propose a temporal and spatial information enhanced heterogeneous network embedding (TSHE) method, which improves embedding performances via fully use the temporal and spatial information. To better capture both of the temporal and spatial information, TSHE first improves the random walk strategy to generate sequences that follow the timestamp and metapath simultaneously. Then it enhances temporal and spatial information in sequence learning to obtain HIN embeddings. For temporal information enhancement, the time interval information is introduced into the sequence learning to obtain node embedding. In this way, our model can not only learn the node embedding at different times, but also the learned node embedding can distinguish the difference between the timestamps of different edges. And for spatial information enhancement, TSHE adopt the attention mechanism to fuse the embeddings learned from multiple metapaths to obtain the final node embeddings. Thus, the node embeddings contain information in multiple semantic spaces to improve the embedding performance. Experimental results on the link prediction task show that the TSHE model performs better than advanced baselines.

title:
LADY:
                    <u>L</u>
                    inear
                    <u>A</u>
                    ttention for Autonomous
                    <u>D</u>
                    riving Efficienc
                    <u>y</u>
                    without Transformers
authors: Jihao Huang, Xi Xia, Zhiyuan Li, Tianle Liu, Jingke Wang, Junbo Chen, Tengju Ye
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving has emerged as a promising paradigm. However, state-of-the-art methods rely heavily on Transformer architectures. The inherent quadratic complexity of Transformers restricts their ability to model long-range spatial and temporal dependencies, particularly on resource-constrained edge platforms. Given the inherent demand for efficient temporal modeling in autonomous driving, this computational bottleneck severely constrains real-time deployment. While linear attention mechanisms offer a computationally efficient alternative, existing architectures are predominantly limited to self-attention, lacking the cross-modal capabilities essential for autonomous driving. In this work, we propose LADY, the first fully linear attention-based generative model for end-to-end autonomous driving. LADY incorporates a novel, lightweight linear cross-attention (LICA) mechanism to enable effective cross-modal interaction while preserving linearity. A key advantage of our framework is its ability to fuse long-range temporal contexts during inference with constant computational and memory costs (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(1)$</tex-math></inline-formula>), regardless of the historical sequence length. Experiments on the NAVSIM and Bench2Drive benchmarks demonstrate that LADY achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods, delivering competitive planning accuracy with significantly reduced latency. Furthermore, efficiency benchmarking on edge devices validates the model's feasibility for resource-limited scenarios.

title: Spatiotemporal Decoupled Learning for Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Chenxiang Ma, Xinyi Chen, Kay Chen Tan, Jibin Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) have gained significant attention for their potential to enable energy-efficient artificial intelligence (AI). However, effective and efficient training of SNNs remains an unresolved challenge. While backpropagation through time (BPTT) achieves high accuracy, it incurs substantial memory overhead. In contrast, biologically plausible local learning methods are more memory-efficient but struggle to match the accuracy of BPTT. To bridge this gap, we propose spatiotemporal decoupled learning (STDL), a novel training framework that decouples the spatial and temporal dependencies to achieve both high accuracy and training efficiency for SNNs. Specifically, to achieve spatial decoupling, STDL partitions the network into smaller subnetworks, each of which is trained independently using an auxiliary network. To address the decreased synergy among subnetworks resulting from spatial decoupling, STDL constructs each subnetwork’s auxiliary network by selecting the largest subset of layers from its subsequent network layers under a memory constraint. Furthermore, STDL decouples dependencies across time steps to enable efficient online learning. Extensive evaluations on seven static and event-based vision datasets demonstrate that STDL consistently outperforms local learning methods and achieves comparable accuracy to the BPTT method with considerably reduced GPU memory cost. Notably, STDL achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> reduced GPU memory than BPTT on the ImageNet dataset. Therefore, this work opens up a promising avenue for memory-efficient SNN training. Code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ChenxiangMA/STDL</uri>

title: SVD-MDL-Prony Based Identification of Wide-Band Oscillation Signals in Renewable Energy Distribution Networks
authors: Yu Zhang, Yifu Zhang, Yuhang He, Rundong Tian, Song Gao
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The integration of new energy sources into the power grid through power electronic devices creates impedance coupling with capacitive grid components across a wide frequency range, which can easily excite wide-band oscillations. These oscillations may lead to equipment disconnection and system instability, severely compromising grid reliability. Accurate identification of key oscillation parameters—such as mode, frequency, and amplitude—is essential for effective suppression and mitigation of wide-band oscillations. Traditional Prony algorithms are often susceptible to noise and sensitive to the choice of effective model order, which limits the accuracy of disturbance extraction. To address this issue, this paper proposes a denoising method based on the Minimum Description Length (MDL) criterion to improve the quality of wide-band oscillation disturbance signal extraction. By applying the MDL principle, overfitting is avoided and a more accurate model order is determined, thereby enhancing the identification precision of wide-band oscillation parameters. Finally, simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Simulation-based Analysis of CfD's influence on Negative Electricity Price: A Case Study of Gansu Province
authors: Chunxiang Yang, Chunhua Li, Yanhong Ma, Ningkang Pan, Xiaobin Zhang, Rukong Jin, Yin Ma
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The Contract for Difference (CfD) is a financial instrument supporting the development of renewable energy. However, its potential market price distortion effect in power systems with a high proportion of renewable energy has not yet been fully understood and quantified. Addressing the issue that the CfD mechanism may exacerbate the phenomenon of negative electricity prices, this study constructs a hybrid simulation model integrating the electricity spot market and financial contract settlement. Taking Gansu Province of China as an example, this paper abstracts the complex decision-making process of renewable energy units signed with CfDs into a set of agent behavior rules based on microeconomic utility maximization, and depicts their strategic behavior of offering extremely low prices to secure revenue. The simulation results show that the CfD coverage rate has a positive correlation with the occurrence frequency of negative electricity prices, and this effect is magnified as the renewable energy penetration rate increases. Further research reveals the market revenue redistribution effect caused by CfDs: while stabilizing the revenue of renewable energy, it erodes the profits of thermal power enterprises and harbors system reliability risks. This study clarifies the intrinsic mechanism of action between CfDs and negative electricity prices, providing a quantitative basis and policy recommendations for the implementation of the CfD mechanism in power systems with a high proportion of renewable energy.

title: Optimized power allocation strategy for hybrid energy storage system based on NGO-VMD
authors: Tengfei Wei, Cong Wang, Ji Chang Yang, Qi Kun Cheng
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Aiming at the problem of active power fluctuation of wind power caused by the randomness of wind speed, in order to improve the stability of wind power grid connection, it is proposed to adopt a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) to achieve power fluctuation suppression. Construct a double-layer Decomposition power allocation strategy based on the Northern Goshawk Optimization-Variational Mode Decomposition (NGO-VMD) algorithm. Firstly, the grid-connected power and the fluctuating power that HESS needs to suppress are determined through moving average filtering. First, the NGO-VMD method is employed to decompose the fluctuating components of wind power output, thereby accomplishing the initial power allocation for the Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS). Subsequently, an adaptive control strategy is incorporated to regulate the State of Charge (SOC) of energy storage devices, enabling secondary power correction for the HESS. This dual-step approach not only ensures compliance with national grid-connection standards but also maintains the SOC of energy storage units within the optimal operating range. Through case analysis and validation, it is demonstrated that the proposed strategy can effectively mitigate wind power fluctuations, avoid over-charging and over-discharging of energy storage devices, and ultimately realize the safe and stable operation of the HESS.

title: Multi-Level Coordinated Operation Framework of DSO-MGO Based on CPSS Mixed Games
authors: Anqi Feng, Mohan Lin, Gongjin Ma, Pingliang Zeng, Cheng Xie, Chao Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The widespread integration of distributed energy resources poses a significant coordination challenge for modern power distribution systems. A key problem is resolving the conflicting economic interests between a central Distribution System Operator (DSO) and multiple Microgrid Operators (MGOs). Here, we propose a multi-level coordination framework based on a Cyber-Physical-Social System (CPSS) perspective to address this issue. Our framework deconstructs the grid into three layers—physical, cyber, and social—to fully capture the dynamic behaviors of all agents. Based on this structure, we build a mixed game-theoretic model. The leader-follower relationship between the DSO and MGOs is modeled as a Stackelberg game, while a cooperative game allows MGOs to form coalitions. We solve this complex bi-level problem by reformulating it into a single-level Mixed-Integer Linear Program using Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions. Case studies on the Jingning distribution network show our approach reduces daily operating costs. These results validate our framework’s effectiveness in enhancing the economic efficiency and stability of highly decentralized power grids.

title: The Research on Synchronous Fault Diagnosis Method for Camera Based on Knowledge Graph and Large Language Model
authors: Dongqing Zhang, Wen Kang, Wenqiang Zhao, Chaofeng Zhang, Guohua Zhang, Chunan Ma, Wuhong Huang, Yuntao Ju
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the growing demand for reactive power support in new power systems, the stable operation of synchronous compensators has become increasingly critical. To address the challenges of fault knowledge being multi-source, heterogeneous, heavily reliant on expert experience, and difficult to reuse effectively, this paper proposes an intelligent fault diagnosis method that integrates Knowledge Graphs and Large Language Models (LLM). The improved BERT-BiLSTM-CRF model is used for precise fault entity extraction, achieving an F1 score of 92.1%, while BERT-TextCNN is employed for relation classification to construct a high-quality fault knowledge graph. Additionally, a large language model is introduced for natural language interaction and fault reasoning, supporting multi-round Q&A and diagnostic report generation. Through case studies on excitation system fault diagnosis, the proposed method's effectiveness in enhancing the intelligence level of fault diagnosis and the scientific nature of decision-making is validated, demonstrating its engineering practical value.

title: AI-Based Anatomical Landmark Recognition for Robot-Assisted Dynamic Dental Implant Surgery
authors: Tucan Paul, Mihaela Hedesiu, Vasile Bublucan, Rares Mocan, Daria Pisla, Calin Vaida, Bogdan Gherman, Cristian Dinu, Doina Pisla
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: This paper presents the development of an AI-based anatomical landmark recognition framework designed for robot-assisted dynamic dental implant surgery. The proposed framework integrates a YOLOv11 deep learning model with a RealSense D405 RGB-D (Red, Green, Blue and Depth) camera, mounted on a collaborative robot, to detect predefined dental landmarks in real time. These 2D detections are projected into 3D space using depth information and subsequently registered to a preoperative STL model derived from CBCT (Cone Beam Computed Tomography) scans using Unity. The implant position, defined in the planning phase relative to anatomical landmarks, is localized intraoperatively without the use of fiducial markers and projected on the STL model of the mandible/maxilla.

title: Coordinated Planning of Battery Swapping Stations and Distribution Networks Incorporating Joint Swapping and Inventory Management
authors: Jiale Liu, Xinwei Shen, Yangfan Luo, Wei Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the rapid growth of electric vehicles (EV) under the "dual-carbon" goals, large-scale EV integration poses challenges to distribution networks, including load concentration, voltage fluctuations, and equipment overloading. To address these issues, this paper proposes a coordinated planning framework for battery swapping stations (BSS) and distribution networks, incorporating orderly swapping and battery inventory management. The model integrates power-flow constraints, station siting, inventory dynamics, and EV scheduling to achieve coordinated optimization of grid security, economy, and battery utilization. Case studies on a practical 47-node network show that the proposed method reduces the overall charging and swapping costs by 5.63 % and improves load balance and battery usage, offering new insights into transportation-energy system integration.

title: Research on Temperature Control Methods for Electronic Products in Polar Ultra-Low Temperature Environments
authors: Xiguo Du, Ze Cheng, Yuanzhen Han, Aoqun Jian, Shengbo Sang, Haitao Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The extreme low-temperature conditions in polar regions pose severe challenges to the reliability and stability of electronic products. This paper proposes a multi-dimensional temperature control method aimed at ensuring the stable operation of electronic devices under extreme cold conditions through synergistic design of thermal insulation and conduction, as well as active heating control. First, based on Fourier's law of heat conduction, a cured aerogel material with low thermal conductivity and cryogenic resistance is selected as the insulating layer to suppress the intrusion of environmental cold. Next, a flexible graphene composite material with high thermal conductivity is chosen as the heat-conducting medium to achieve rapid and uniform distribution of internal heat within the device. Furthermore, the selection of heating elements is optimized by adopting metal thin-film resistive heaters to ensure fault-free operation in ultra-low temperature environments. Finally, a low-cost hardware temperature control circuit based on an analog PID controller is designed, which regulates the system through a positive feedback mechanism to maintain operating temperature fluctuations within the specified range of the product. Experimental results demonstrate that this method effectively ensures the reliable operation of electronic devices in ultra-low temperature environments while significantly reducing system energy consumption. This study provides a feasible thermal management solution for electronic products used in polar expeditions, spacecraft, and other ultra-low temperature application scenarios.

title: State Similarity-Based Planning Method for Enhancing Resilience of Integrated Community Energy Systems under Extreme Climate Events
authors: Jiawei Qu, Kai Hou, Hao Wu, Zeyu Liu, Hongjie Jia
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the increasing frequency and intensity of extreme climate events, such as heatwaves and cold spells, driven by climate change, the risk of energy supply disruptions is escalating. In response, this paper proposes a resilience-oriented planning model for integrated community energy systems, aimed at enhancing resilience under extreme climate events. The proposed model accounts for the uncertainties associated with extreme climate conditions, renewable energy, and energy demand. These uncertainties are quantified using a scenario-based approach. The proposed resilience-oriented planning model is formulated as a bi-level mixed-integer programming problem that incorporates massive operational scenarios. To mitigate the significant computational demands resulting from the massive uncertain scenarios, a state similarity method is introduced. This method uses active constraints as a criterion for determining scenario similarity, thereby transforming the optimization problem with massive scenarios into solving a system of linear equations. Numerical analysis demonstrates that the proposed model effectively reduces energy shortages during extreme climate events. Furthermore, the state similarity method enhances planning efficiency by an order of magnitude.

title: A Self-Optimized PSO-XGBoost Regression for Robust Feature Selection and Diabetes Prediction
authors: Deepshikha Gupta, Malaya Dutta Borah
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: An early and accurate prediction of diabetes is important in proactive healthcare, which will help in early intervention, better monitoring of the patient, and efficient utilization of healthcare resources. Conventional regression models often struggle to address nonlinear relationships, particularly in healthcare data characterized by high noise and dimensionality, which hampers robustness across diverse datasets. This paper proposed a self-optimized particle swarm optimization (SO-PSO) with extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) regression framework, which combines an evolutionary optimisation algorithm with gradient boosting for solid feature selection and classification. The SO-PSO mechanism adaptively refines the XGBoost hyperparameters according to dynamic inertia weights and learning factors, which balances global exploration and local exploitation. This approach avoids premature convergence and strengthens the tolerance to noisy and imbalanced data. This experiment was performed using the PIMA dataset, which contains 768 instances with demographic and clinical indicators such as blood glucose, BMI, blood pressure, insulin, and family history. The results indicate that SO-PSO-XGBoost outperforms all the other methods with the best performance of 99.23% accuracy, 0.992 AUC, 99.10% precision, 99.20% recall, 98.9% specificity, and 99.15% F1-score. In terms of the predictive accuracy and convergence rate, the proposed model outperformed the baseline models DTR (82.5% accuracy), RFR (99.0%), SVR (84.0%), and MLR (86.5%), demonstrating the scalability of the proposed method. This study indicated that the proposed SO-PSO-XGBoost model offers a cutting-edge predictive model for diabetes management, offering a healthcare performant predictive tool to optimize diagnostic accuracy and reliability, as well as the application of evidence-based clinical practice.

title: Detection and Identification of Monkeypox Viral Disease Using Deep Learning
authors: Chaithra I V, Aashitha S, Chaithra S N, Suchithra S, Shravya S Gowda, Kavya T M
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Monkeypox is a zoonotic viral disease that causes skin lesions that look like those of other skin infections, making it hard to diagnose quickly and correctly. This paper introduces a deep learning-driven automated framework for the detection and identification of Monkeypox utilizing skin lesion images. The suggested model uses U-Net for image segmentation and DenseNet-121 for feature extraction and classification. This makes it easier to find lesions and identify diseases. We used a publicly available dataset from Kaggle that had labeled images of Monkeypox, Chickenpox, Measles, and normal skin conditions for our tests. To make the model work better, the preprocessing pipeline includes converting images from grayscale to RGB, reducing noise, resizing, normalizing, and enhancing images. The model that was created had a training accuracy of 96.89 % and a validation accuracy of 95.09 %, which was backed up by high precision, recall, and F1-score values. This study is novel because it combines segmentation-driven preprocessing with DenseNet121's dense connectivity to make feature reuse more efficient, which leads to reliable classification of different types of skin lesions. This framework shows promise as a cost-effective and scalable diagnostic tool that helps doctors find Monkeypox early and lowers the chance of misdiagnosis in clinical settings.

title: Enhancing eVTOLs Emergency Landing via Real-Time Oriented Object Detection
authors: Jingjing Qian, Yang Cheng, Xinmin Tang, Bing Liu, Xin Chang, Zhiyun Gao, Siyao Zhou
publish: 2025 3rd Asian Aerospace and Astronautics Conference (AAAC)
abstract: Safe emergency landing is a critical requirement for the widespread deployment of electric Vertical Take-Off and Landing (eVTOL) aircraft in urban air mobility. To address this challenge, we propose a vision-based framework for autonomous landing zone detection that leverages oriented object detection (OOD) to overcome the limitations of traditional horizontal bounding boxes. Our method utilizes a CSPDarkNet-based one-stage detector enhanced with a Path Aggregation Feature Pyramid Network (PAFPN) for efficient multi-scale feature learning. A key innovation is our Log-GIoU loss function, which effectively resolves challenges in rotated object detection, including boundary discontinuity and square-like object ambiguity, leading to more stable regression. Furthermore, we bridge the gap between multiclass detection and emergency decision-making by introducing a semantic grouping strategy that consolidates detections into a binary classification of "safe" or "unsafe" zones. Extensive experiments on the DOTA dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves comparable performance (70.1% mAP), significantly outperforming existing methods. The results confirm the framework's robustness in complex urban environments, highlighting the feasibility of real-time, vision-only emergency landing systems that operate independently of external infrastructure, thereby enhancing the safety and autonomy of eVTOL operations.

title: Improved Genetic Algorithm-Based Volt/Var Coordinated Control Strategy for Wind Farm Clusters
authors: Xinglong Huo, Shicheng Zhong, Kerui Ma, Xu Jiang, Peng Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper addresses the operational challenges of voltage fluctuation, uneven reactive power distribution, and circulating currents in multi-wind-farm grid connections. A coordinated reactive power optimization strategy based on an improved Genetic Algorithm (GA) is proposed. The strategy achieves differentiated reactive power dispatch by accounting for the heterogeneous capacity and sensitivity characteristics of individual wind farms, ensuring the stability of the point of common coupling (PCC) voltage while effectively mitigating inter-farm reactive power circulation. The proposed strategy was validated across typical wind farm cluster topologies, with simulation results confirming its consistent superiority over conventional methods in both voltage tracking accuracy and reactive power allocation.

title: Optimizing Seat Utilization in Bus Transport Through AI-Powered Segment Management
authors: Joe Freddie Cheeran, Nirmal Varghese Babu
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Public transport seat utilization have been and still are a critical challenge for sustainable urban mobility. In this study, the authors introduce SPAR (Segmented Probabilistic Adaptive Reallocation), a new AI-based dynamic seat allocation framework that can help manage bus transport systems by the application of intelligent segment management. Through the proposed approach, real-time passenger boarding and alighting trends are modeled with a graph-based representation of bus routes, such that nodes symbolize stops and seats and edges capture the time-dependent occupancy relationships. A hybrid algorithm consisting of Graph Neural Networks (GNN), Reinforcement Learning (RL), and Entropic Optimal Transport (EOT) is applied to the dynamic seat reallocation based on the changing demand with the aim of minimizing idle capacity and at the same time maintaining a balanced occupancy over the route segments. To check the performance of SPAR, a synthetic dataset has been created which represents 5,000 bus trips with dynamic boarding events and 40 seating capacities. The experiment outputs show that SPAR maintains a Seat Utilization Ratio (SUR) of 0.94, a Dynamic Allocation Accuracy (DAA) of 0.93, and a 47 % decrease in Segment Utilization Variance (SUV) compared to the traditional static and heuristic allocation methods. Moreover, the model gets converged rapidly within 30 epochs with a stability index of 0.93. These results provide strong evidence that SPAR not only improves operational efficiency but also enhances flexibility in public transport systems, hence, a scalable framework is created for real-time intelligent fleet management and data-driven seat allocation in smart urban transit networks.

title: An Improved Particle Swarm Optimization-based Convolutional Neural Network State Estimation Method for Multiple Scenarios
authors: Zeyu Liang, Changyou Feng, Rui Zhou, Hongyang Jin, Zhen Yuan, Huahua Wu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy into the grid, the dynamics and complexity of the power grid have significantly increased. Improving the response speed and accuracy of state estimation has become a key to ensuring the safe and stable operation of the power grid. In response to the problems of insufficient accuracy and response lag of traditional state estimation in complex power grids, An improved particle swarm optimization-based convolutional neural network state estimation method for multiple scenarios is proposed in this paper. This method uses a particle swarm algorithm that integrates chaotic optimization and dynamic adaptive strategies to accelerate the global optimization process of CNN hyperparameters and obtain the optimal network structure. At the same time, a scene recognition classifier is constructed to automatically identify abnormal operating conditions based on input features and dynamically call the corresponding CNN model for prediction. Experimental results show that this method achieves high-precision and strong robustness state estimation in complex power grid environments.

title: A Prediction Method for Transmission Line Icing Thickness Based on CNN-BiLSTM Hybrid Neural Network
authors: Panfeng Li, Bin Feng, Zhenyang Fu, Dan Hu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: To address the power grid security risks caused by the insufficient prediction accuracy of icing disasters on transmission lines, this paper proposes an icing thickness prediction method based on a CNN-BiLSTM hybrid neural network. This method first uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) to extract the local spatiotemporal features of micrometeorological parameters, and then fuses the forward and backward temporal dependencies of the icing process through a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), thereby constructing an end-to-end prediction model. Based on the icing monitoring dataset of five-dimensional parameters of temperature, humidity, wind speed, atmospheric pressure, and rainfall from the mountainous areas of Shaoxing, Zhejiang, China, the coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of the point prediction results of the proposed model on the test set reaches 0.874, the root mean square error (RMSE) is 0.084 mm, and 92.7% of the prediction errors are concentrated within the range of ±0.1 mm. The experimental data indicate that the CNN-BiLSTM hybrid model has good prediction performance and application potential in the task of predicting the icing thickness of transmission lines.

title: Adaptive network high-voltage circuit breaker fault voiceprint recognition based on deep reconstruction of multiple subdomains
authors: Dong Wang, Lubo Zhou, Liyun Xie, Xipu Liu, Shiqi Dong, Junhua Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: Throughout the functioning of an elevated-voltage loop disruptor, the device generates a continuous voiceprint signal. These voiceprint data not only capture the sound characteristics of the equipment during normal operation, but also include abnormal voiceprint signals caused by potential faults, becoming an important basis to assess the wellness condition and technical performance of circuit breakers. In order to identify the status of elevated-voltage loop disruptors founded upon vocal-pattern indicators, a deep reconstruction multi-subdomain adaptation network is proposed. First, the distorted waveform is cleaned by the introduced novel wavelet boundary function, which significantly enhances the clarity-to-interference ratio of the resultant waveform. Secondly, a deep reconstruction multi-subdomain adaptation network is proposed to choose the optimal normal mixture model via the expectation maximization method and Akaike info criterion, dynamically ascertain the suitable count of groups, and use the k-medoid clustering algorithm to reconstruct the original domain as Multiple source domains, by subdividing the source domain into multiple sub-domains, can more accurately capture the characteristic changes of the data under each specific condition, helping to train a more robust and generalized model, thereby Better adapt to the actual situation in the target field. Ultimately, a weighted component is utilized to reduce the overall error and accomplish multiple origin domain joint analysis. Test outcomes demonstrate that the introduced framework can precisely identify the malfunction operation state of elevated-voltage switchgear and detect common malfunctions of elevated-voltage switchgear in various interference settings, with an accuracy rate of approximately 95.27%.

title: Predicting Drug Sensitivity in TP53-Mutated Cancer via Quantum Boltzman Machines
authors: Vasile Vancea
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: Mutations in the TP53 gene are among the most prevalent alterations in human cancers and are known to influence therapeutic responses. Accurate prediction of drug sensitivity based on TP53 mutation profiles remains a major challenge in precision oncology. In this study, we propose a quantum-enhanced framework for genomic classification using Quantum Boltzmann Machines (QBMs) applied to quantum-encoded DNA sequences. Exon 5–8 fragments of TP53 containing both wild-type and clinically relevant mutations were encoded using angle-based quantum rotations and input into a QBM architecture trained to predict binary drug response (sensitive vs resistant) based on half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) thresholds. Simulated results demonstrate that the QBM achieved over $\mathbf{9 1 \%}$ accuracy in classifying drug sensitivity, outperforming classical Boltzmann models and revealing interpretable latent features. This work showcases the potential of QBMs in modeling sequence-driven phenotypes and supports their application in biomarker discovery, drug response prediction, and quantum-enabled precision medicine.

title: Optimal Scheduling Based on Electricity-Carbon Trading in an Integrated Energy System
authors: Qiqi Dai, Junzhi Cheng, Yingxue Li, Xueting Zhang, Xijie Zhang, Wenjie Xiong
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: This paper investigates the optimal scheduling of an integrated energy system (IES) under an electricity-carbon market mechanism. First, we establish output models for each IES device, including wind power, photovoltaics , energy storage systems, gas turbines, combined cooling, heating, and power (CCHP), and a refined P2G chain. Second, we propose a demand response model to optimize the flexible resource utilization of electricity, heat, and gas. Next, we construct a carbon trading mechanism model. Furthermore, Using allocation rules such as the Shapley value, we achieve a fair distribution of the total surplus within the cooperative alliance. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model through case analysis. Results show that, compared to independent operation, the cooperatively operated IES reduces both total operating expenses and carbon costs, and the contributions and benefits of each agent are reasonable.

title: DPC-YOLO: Underwater Plastic Pollution Detection Using YOLOv11 with Dark Prior Channel Contrast Enhancement
authors: Rajat Amat, Anurag Sahoo, Mourina Ghosh, Amitava Nag, Robin Pathak
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: Underwater plastic pollution harms marine ecosystems, yet detection remains challenging due to poor visibility and color distortion in underwater images. This research offers DPCYOLO, an enhanced detection framework integrating YOLOv11 with a Dark Prior Channel Contrast Enhancement (DPCCE) technique to improve image clarity and contrast before detection. The approach offers better feature extraction under demanding underwater settings. Experimental results demonstrate that DPCYOLO obtains a precision of 98.01%, recall of 97.50%, F1-score of 98.59%, and mAP of 97.00%, indicating strong accuracy and resilience for real-time underwater plastic debris identification and marine pollution monitoring.

title: Integrating Air Quality Data and Health Surveillance: A Machine Learning Approach to Predicting Respiratory Disease in India
authors: Siddique Ibrahim S P, Anshul Jha, Akshar Jha
publish: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
abstract: India faces a critical challenge in addressing the health impacts of air pollution, with particulate matter (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P M_{2.5}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P M_{10}$</tex>) serving as key indicators of deteriorating air quality. Despite the expansion of air quality monitoring networks, significant gaps remain in exposure assessment and health impact prediction, particularly in rural and peri-urban regions. To help address these limitations, an integrated machine learning framework is developed to predict respiratory health cases using air quality data from the Central Pollution Control Board and health surveillance data from the Health Management Information System. The framework evaluates the performance of Random Forest and XGBoost algorithms in predicting monthly state-level respiratory disease cases based on measurements of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P M_{2.5}, P M_{10}$</tex>, and the Air Quality Index (AQI). Statistical relationships between air quality indicators and respiratory health outcomes across multiple Indian states are also examined. The approach effectively captures complex nonlinear interactions between pollution exposure and health outcomes while accounting for temporal variations and lag effects. The results indicate that combining environmental monitoring data with health surveillance information can support evidence-based policy decisions, healthcare resource planning, and targeted public health interventions. This integrated methodology offers a foundation for improving the prediction of respiratory health impacts associated with air quality conditions in India.

title: Impact of Separator Sizing on the Flexibility of N-in-1 Alkaline Electrolysis Systems in Renewable Energy Integration
authors: Xin Wang, Bin Cao, Jun Mo, Baiping Zhu, Yi Zhou, Yiwei Qiu
publish: 2025 IEEE 9th Conference on Energy Internet and Energy System Integration (EI2)
abstract: The volatility of renewable energy demands greater flexibility from hydrogen production systems. Alkaline water electrolysis (AEL) must balance thermal inertia and dynamic response to accommodate power fluctuations. This study investigates a large-scale N-in-1 AEL configuration using a coupled thermal-mass dynamic model to examine how separator size affects thermal inertia and operational flexibility. The model integrates hydrogen-in-oxygen (HTO) accumulation, thermal dynamics, and multidimensional performance metrics. Simulations for four separator sizes (50%, 80%, 100%, and 150% of the baseline) were conducted under fluctuating power inputs, evaluating startup time, HTO fluctuation, temperature uniformity, and low-load operation duration. Results show that smaller separators offer faster response but lower thermal inertia, leading to intensified HTO fluctuations and shorter low-load operation, while larger separators enhance stability and extend low-load duration at the cost of slower startup and reduced ramping capability. We find that separator sizing remarkably governs HTO accumulation, and design guidelines are provided accordingly.

title: Predicting Lap Times in Formula 1: A Deep Learning Approach Using LSTM Networks
authors: Muhammed Faruk Kara, Ayhan Küçükmanisa
publish: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Intelligent Computer Communication and Processing (ICCP)
abstract: One of the most critical factors influencing success in Formula 1 racing is the ability to make strategic decisions at the optimal moment. These decisions—such as pit stop timing and tire selection—are deeply tied to lap time dynamics. In this study, we propose a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based time series model for predicting the next lap time of a driver using real-world multidimensional race data. The model is trained on data from the 2021–2023 seasons and evaluated on the 2024 season. A comprehensive data preprocessing and feature engineering pipeline is introduced, integrating race-specific variables with driver and team characteristics to enhance predictive performance. The proposed architecture effectively captures both sequential dependencies and contextual information, leading to more accurate and realistic lap time forecasts. Beyond its predictive capability, the model offers a promising foundation for real-time race assistants and decision-support systems. Future enhancements may include driver-personalized architectures, dynamic race condition modeling, and integrated strategic recommendation systems.
